Abstract: Frontier scientific reasoning remains a major challenge for large language models (LLMs), where even the strongest commercial systems fall short of expert-level performance. A closer look at model behavior reveals substantial complementarity that single-model evaluation hides: different frontier models excel on different question types, and no single model captures the full picture. We present SciOrch, a framework that trains a lightweight 8B model to orchestrate frontier LLMs for scientific reasoning. The orchestrator decomposes each question, delegates sub-problems to selected commercial models through API calls, and synthesizes a final answer. Training such an orchestrator is fundamentally harder than conventional agentic RL: each action triggers an API call that is expensive in both dollar cost and latency, making standard online rollouts infeasible. We address this with MCTS-based approach, producing diverse orchestration trajectories, extracting per-node single-turn samples, and optimizing the orchestrator with GRPO-style training. On a 240-question test set spanning SGI-Reasoning and Scientists' First Exam, SciOrch reaches 56.66% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest single commercial model by 3.74% and the strongest multi-agent baseline by 3.33%. It also attains the best accuracy on both SGI and SFE with less than half the API cost of typical multi-agent methods.

</details>


### [201] [Free Energy Heuristics: Fast-And-Frugal Cognition as Active Inference Under Uncertain Precision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15877)
*Alex Bogdan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates how Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning affects model performance and attributes its success or failure to meta-uncertainty, presenting formal theoretical support and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand when and why additional reasoning from Chain-of-Thought (CoT) either helps or harms large language models, as prior findings suggest both outcomes depending on task characteristics.

Method: The authors theoretically show that policies minimizing expected free energy under uncertain precision cease integrating cues after finite high-quality evidence when precision priors are heavy-tailed. They benchmark this insight with a newly created dataset (FEH-79), running pre-registered experiments involving various CoT lengths across seven language models and measuring performance in high-meta-uncertainty regimes.

Result: The study finds a significant accuracy drop of 17.3 points in high-meta-uncertainty regimes when longer CoT was used, while matched low-uncertainty tasks showed no such degradation. The results vary depending on model size and capability.

Conclusion: Meta-uncertainty explains conditions under which CoT reasoning helps or harms. The work unifies Bayesian reasoning with heuristic approaches, showing that less-is-more effects relate to high-meta-uncertainty rather than refuting Bayesian cognition.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) improves large language models' performance in math and symbolic reasoning. But on planning, contested ethics, and tasks where the model cannot check itself, more reasoning makes things worse. Both effects are documented; what has been missing is a principled account of which property decides the outcome. We argue it is meta-uncertainty: how unsure the model is about the reliability of its own evidence. When that uncertainty is high, extra reasoning stops adding signal and starts manufacturing false confidence. We prove that the policy minimizing expected free energy under uncertain precision stops integrating cues after a finite number of high-validity ones when the precision prior is heavy-tailed (Theorem 2.6.1), and under a Descending Dominance condition, is sample-wise identical to take-the-best (Theorem 2.7.4). Fast-and-frugal heuristics and active inference are, then, two descriptions of the same computation. The prediction is that on high-meta-uncertainty items, longer CoT should degrade accuracy. We score the regime per item (simulate-and-recover rho > 0.96), build FEH-79, a benchmark of Knightian frames with matched controls, and run a pre-registered study across seven models (five open-weight 3B-32B, two frontier), five CoT lengths, and 7,875 responses. The gate, fixed before any data, required a negative interaction with posterior probability above 0.95 and an accuracy drop of more than 6 points. It held. The high-regime drop is 17.3 points (95% CI [7.7, 25.5]); matched items with definite answers show no cost. The effect is regime-dependent: decisive in capable mid-to-large models, directional in the two frontier systems, absent-to-reversed in the weakest. The framework answers when CoT helps and unifies the Bayesian and fast-and-frugal traditions: less-is-more effects are evidence about the meta-uncertainty regime, not against Bayesian cognition.

</details>


### [202] [Koshur Diacritizer: A Byte-Level Sequence-to-Sequence Model for Kashmiri Diacritic Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15883)
*Haq Nawaz Malik,Nahfid Nissar,Faizan Iqbal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Koshur Diacritizer, a sequence-to-sequence model to restore diacritics in Kashmiri text, supported by a new dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kashmiri digital text often omits diacritic marks, leading to ambiguity and challenges in NLP applications.

Method: The paper uses a ByT5-small byte-level sequence-to-sequence model with script-aware normalization, alignment validation, and skeleton-preserving inference.

Result: The system achieves a DERm of 0.2012, WER of 0.2159, and a mean accuracy of 77.5% as evaluated by a native linguistic expert.

Conclusion: The authors publicly release the dataset, model, and code to establish a reproducible baseline for Kashmiri diacritic restoration and support further research in low-resource languages.

Abstract: Kashmiri, an Indo-Aryan language written in a modified Perso-Arabic script, frequently omits diacritic marks in digital text, creating ambiguity and challenging downstream NLP applications. We present Koshur Diacritizer, a ByT5-small byte-level sequence-to-sequence model for restoring diacritics in Kashmiri text. To support this task, we release a publicly available dataset of 23.7k aligned undiacritized diacritized Kashmiri sentence pairs. The proposed framework combines script-aware normalization, alignment validation, and skeleton-preserving inference to ensure reliable restoration while maintaining the original base-letter sequence. Experimental results on a held-out test set achieve a DERm of 0.2012 and a WER of 0.2159. Additionally, evaluation by a native Kashmiri linguistic expert yields a mean accuracy of 77.5%. The dataset, model, and source code are publicly released to provide a reproducible baseline for Kashmiri diacritic restoration and future low-resource language research.

</details>


### [203] [Neuron Level Analysis of Large Language Model in Legal Domain Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15884)
*Eri Onami,Youmi Ma,Shuhei Kurita,Naoaki Okazaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study examines neuron-level reasoning in legal tasks for seven open-weight LLMs using neuron attribution scores, finding domain-specific and cross-task influential neurons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work seeks to understand how LLMs reason in legal and other applied domains by identifying influential neurons and their role in performance across tasks.

Method: The study used neuron attribution scores to identify and suppress influential neurons. It then observed task-specific and cross-task neuron behavior across seven open-weight models.

Result: Identified neurons are task-specific, with some being influential across multiple tasks. Legal domain neurons show high overlap, potentially spanning jurisdictions. Neuron location seems influenced by input format/content.

Conclusion: The study highlights task and domain-specific neuron behavior in LLMs, suggesting an interplay between neuron functionality and input characteristics, particularly for legal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: We presented a neuron-level analysis of legal-domain reasoning in LLMs, comparing it with other applied domain tasks across seven open-weight models. Using neuron attribution scores to rank and suppress influential neurons, we confirmed that suppressing the identified neurons collapses accuracy on the target task, whereas suppressing the same number of random neurons does not. We further found a small subset of neurons influential across all seven tasks; once these are removed, suppressing the remaining neurons degrades only the task they were identified from, revealing genuinely task-specific neurons in every model studied. Within the legal domain, the three benchmarks exhibit relatively high neuron overlap and tend to be affected jointly, suggesting of legal components neurons that span jurisdictions. The distribution of identified neurons in our experiments suggests that the hypothesis that influential neurons are concentrated in middle MLP layers may depend on the input format and content, rather than being a universal phenomenon.

</details>


### [204] [BALTO: Balanced Token-Level Policy Optimization for Hallucination Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15893)
*Ning Li,Zixuan Guo,Yan Xu,Wenbo Fei,Yifan Niu,Chang Luo,Yasheng Wang,Weiwen Liu,Yong Yu,Weinan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BALTO, a framework to mitigate hallucinations in large language models by redistributing credit at the token level and improving faithfulness without penalizing entire responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of hallucinations in large language models, which hinder their deployment in knowledge-intensive applications requiring faithful and evidence-grounded responses.

Method: BALTO framework utilizes checkable claims, verifies them against references, applies token-level credit assignment, and balances probability mass redistribution to enhance faithfulness and avoid suppressing supported content.

Result: BALTO demonstrates enhanced faithfulness in experiments over six models and benchmarks, surpassing competing methods by achieving the best trade-off between faithfulness and informativeness.

Conclusion: The proposed BALTO framework provides a stable and efficient approach to mitigating hallucinations in language models, showcasing significant performance gains in faithfulness and optimization stability.

Abstract: Hallucinations remain a major obstacle to deploying large language models (LLMs) in knowledge-intensive settings, where generated responses must be faithfully grounded in provided evidence. Reinforcement learning (RL) is a promising direction for hallucination mitigation, but response-level faithfulness rewards suffer from a granularity mismatch: localized hallucinations can cause supported content to receive spurious penalties. Although recent work introduces fine-grained feedback such as claim-level verification and token-level rewards, unbalanced credit assignment can still induce length, verbosity, or optimization-noise biases. We propose BALTO, a Balanced Token-level Policy Optimization framework for hallucination mitigation. BALTO extracts checkable factual claims, verifies them against the reference context, and projects claim-level judgments to token-level labels. A balanced token-level credit assignment mechanism is introduced into the framework. This design redistributes probability mass from unsupported content toward faithful content, rather than suppressing the entire response. We systematically analyze the limitations of response-level rewards from a theoretical standpoint, and prove BALTO's advantages in training stability and optimization efficiency for hallucination mitigation. Experiments on ConFiQA, RAGTruth, and FinLLM-Eval show that BALTO achieves the highest faithfulness across all six model--benchmark settings and consistently outperforms existing post-training baselines in Q-Score, demonstrating a stronger faithfulness--informativeness trade-off.

</details>


### [205] [Control-Plane Placement Shapes Forgetting: An Architectural Study of Agent Memory Across Thirteen System Configurations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15903)
*Dongxu Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates where a Large Language Model (LLM) fits in an agent's memory pipeline, analyzing how different configurations recover from forgetting failures using a benchmark suite (ForgetEval). Mutation-time LLM placement shows the best overall performance in memory management tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the gap in understanding how different memory management configurations, involving LLMs, affect an agent's ability to handle forgetting failures, which dominate production errors.

Method: The researchers compare thirteen system configurations using ForgetEval, a benchmark consisting of 1385 test cases. They analyze LLM placement at recall, inscribe, and mutation times, paired with a six-method Adapter Protocol for heterogeneous memory evaluation.

Result: The mutation-time LLM placement achieved the highest overall performance, with significant improvements in intent-aware deletion and category coverage (91.7-93.2% overall, $0.17 per run at 2.3s/case latency). Conversely, deterministic methods fell short in canonicalization, while inscribe-time LLMs failed in intent-aware deletion.

Conclusion: ForgetEval reveals critical trade-offs in LLM placements in memory pipelines. Mutation-time hooks exhibit the most balanced performance improvements. The study highlights the need to measure forgetting, not just recall, and provides open-source tools for further exploration.

Abstract: Where an LLM sits in an agent memory pipeline -- between the recall plane that retrieves stored facts (extensively benchmarked) and the control plane that mutates them via supersede, release, purge (largely untested) -- shapes which forgetting failure modes the system recovers. Comparing thirteen system configurations on a 385-case adversarial surface, we observe three placement regimes with partly complementary coverage: deterministic primitives suffice for lexical/temporal categories but fail canonicalization (5% on identifier-obfuscation, 0% on cross-lingual); inscribe-time LLM recovers canonicalization (100%) but cannot help intent-aware deletion (0% on prefix-collision and compound-fact); a mutation-time hook recovers intent-aware deletion (78-85%) and brightens nearly all categories simultaneously (91.7-93.2% overall, $0.17 per 385-case run, 2.3s/case mutation latency vs. 64-191ms/case deterministic, recall path unchanged).
  We expose the trade-off via ForgetEval, a 1000-case templated suite plus a 385-case adversarial layer (132 hand-crafted + 253 LLM-drafted oracle-validated) scored by deterministic substring match, paired with a six-method Adapter Protocol with honest N/A scoring that lets heterogeneous memory stores enter in 130 lines. Admission is corroborated by 10-annotator IAA (Fleiss' kappa = 0.958) and a 77-case external-authored subset (four blind contributors) that replicates the canonicalization asymmetry and amplifies the joint-placement lift (+27.8 pt). Production failures are predominantly forgetting failures rather than recall failures, yet existing benchmarks measure only recall. ForgetEval and all adapters are released under MIT.

</details>


### [206] [Calibrated Triage, Not Autonomy: Confidence Estimation for Medical Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15910)
*Reza Khanmohammadi,Kundan Thind,Mohammad M. Ghassemi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates how well vision-language models (LVLMs) used in medical visual QA can reliably abstain or automate cases based on confidence estimators, emphasizing trustworthy signal use over accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the critical risks in using vision-language models in medical decision-making, where unreliable outputs can appear trustworthy, leading to dangerous outcomes. The focus is on ensuring safe deployment through effective confidence measures rather than just accuracy.

Method: The study evaluates seven confidence estimators on five open-weight LVLMs across three medical VQA datasets spanning clinical imaging, radiology, and pathology. These tests involve models trained only with natural images and explore a selective prediction framework for safe handoff based on confidence thresholds.

Result: The analysis shows that standard metrics poorly distinguish between confidence estimators, and off-domain calibration can improve them without increasing deployable yield. No single estimator consistently performs the best. Safe automation recovers some cases but varies by domain, demonstrating that current capabilities are better suited for triage than autonomy.

Conclusion: Calibrated, confidence-based triage is viable for medical LVLMs today, automating only when scores indicate high confidence and routing uncertain cases to clinicians. However, achieving safe autonomy remains out of reach, and calibrated deployment depends heavily on base-model competence and domain-specific considerations.

Abstract: A vision-language model can answer a question about a medical image fluently and confidently while barely using the image, leaning instead on language priors. In medicine this is the failure that matters most, because the answer looks trustworthy and is not, and the only protection is a confidence score reliable enough to tell the system when to abstain. We ask a deployment question rather than an accuracy one: how much imaging work a model can safely handle alone, and which confidence signal makes that possible. We evaluate seven confidence estimators across five open-weight LVLMs and three medical visual-question-answering datasets spanning broad clinical imaging, radiology, and pathology, with every probe trained only on natural images and applied without adaptation. Recast as bounded selective prediction (automate a case only when confidence clears a threshold, defer the rest), the comparison is cautionary. The standard metrics are poor guides: discrimination barely separates the methods, and the weak calibration of a cheap self-report is cheaply removed by off-domain temperature scaling without changing deployable yield. What distinguishes a usable estimator is the high-confidence region a clinician acts on: the weakest baselines are confidently wrong on 41 to 45 percent of their errors against 1 to 4 percent for the best probe, and no estimator is reliably best across domains or models. Safe handoff is governed at two levels: base-model competence sets a ceiling, so a well-calibrated score recovers roughly a third of radiology cases at a 20 percent error tolerance but almost none of pathology; the confidence layer then decides how much of that ceiling is reachable. The usable role today is calibrated triage, not autonomy: automate the cases a calibrated score marks safe, route the rest to a clinician. We release all outputs, correctness judgments, and confidence scores, with code.

</details>


### [207] [Interactor: Agentic RL oriented Iterative Creation for Ad Description Generation in Sponsored Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15911)
*Penghui Wei,Jiayu Wu,Chao Ye,Zhi Guo,Shuanglong Li,Lin Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Interactor, a framework for generating ad descriptions using an iterative multi-turn process optimized with reinforcement learning, achieving significant improvements in ad quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ad descriptions often lack world knowledge to effectively address user search intents and highlight detailed selling points. The authors aim to generate informative and knowledge-rich ad descriptions automatically.

Method: Interactor is a framework where a generation model interacts with a custom environment using reinforcement learning. The environment includes generative reward models (GenRMs) that evaluate qualities like knowledge richness and consistency, providing binary and reasoning feedback for iterative refinement.

Result: Interactor significantly outperforms existing approaches in generating informative and faithful ad descriptions, based on experiments conducted on industrial datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed framework improves the quality and effectiveness of ad descriptions, and since its deployment in May 2026, it has enhanced ad revenue and user experience in a leading search ads system.

Abstract: This paper focuses on automatically generating informative ad descriptions in sponsored search. Unlike ad titles which are usually optimized to attract user click feedbacks, ad descriptions have a longer text span and possess the potential of incorporating world knowledge to address user search intents while presenting the fine-grained selling points of the ads. We propose Interactor, a multi-turn iterative creation framework optimized with agentic RL for ad description generation. The generation model acts as a policy that interacts with a customized environment consisting of multiple generative reward models. Given initial generations by the policy, the customized GenRMs evaluate multi-dimensional qualities including knowledge capacity and landing page consistency, providing both binary signals and reasoning feedbacks. The policy then iteratively refines the descriptions based on such feedbacks to ensure continuous improvement. Experiments on industrial datasets show that the Interactor framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in generating knowledge-rich and faithful ad descriptions. Since May 2026, it has been deployed online in a leading search ads system, contributing to both ad revenue and user experience.

</details>


### [208] [Contaminated Collaboration: Measuring Gender Bias Transfer in LLM-Assisted Student Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15914)
*Ariyan Hossain,Kazi Kamruzzaman Rabbi,Farig Sadeque,S M Taiabul Haque*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study investigates whether gender bias in large language models (LLMs) influences human-written career plan essays, finding that biased prompts lead to gender-differentiated and stereotyped content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether gender bias present in LLMs is transferred to human users' written outputs when these systems are used as writing assistants.

Method: The study involved verifying that gender-biased prompts cause gender-differentiated language in LLM outputs, followed by an experiment with 123 participants who wrote essays under different AI assistance conditions (none, neutral, or biased) based on gender-specific biographical profiles.

Result: Participants influenced by biased LLM assistance wrote essays with a greater agentic gap and more stereotypical gendered occupations. This bias transfer was asymmetric, suppressing agency in female-focused essays while leaving male-focused ones largely unaffected.

Conclusion: The findings underscore the risk of bias propagation from AI tools to human outputs, emphasizing the need for fairness-aware designs in educational AI systems.

Abstract: Gender bias in LLMs has been studied extensively in model outputs, with biased prompts shown to amplify stereotyped generations. Whether such bias propagates into text produced by humans who use these systems, however, remains underexplored. We investigate whether gender bias in an LLM writing assistant transfers into career plan essays written by students. We first verify that a gender-biased prompt induces gender-differentiated language in LLM-generated essays, while a neutral prompt does not. We then recruited participants (N = 123) in a controlled environment to write career plan essays for paired biographical profiles differing only in gender under three conditions: no AI assistance, neutral LLM assistance, or gender-biased LLM assistance. Students in the biased condition produced essays with a significantly larger agentic gap and more gender-stereotypic occupation suggestions than those in the control and neutral conditions. Our results also reveal that this bias transfer is asymmetric: agency is suppressed in female-target essays while male-target writing remains largely unaffected. Our findings highlight the risk of bias propagation in AI-assisted writing, calling for fairness-aware design in educational AI tools.

</details>


### [209] [Beyond NL2Code: A Structured Survey of Multimodal Code Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15932)
*Xuanle Zhao,Qiushi Sun,Jingyu Xiao,Xuexin Liu,Haoyue Yang,Qiaosheng Chen,Xianzhen Luo,Jing Huang,Yufeng Zhong,Lei Chen,Shuai Fu,Zhenlin Wei,Jinhe Bi,Lei Jiang,Haibo Qiu,Siqi Yang,Peng Shi,Jian Hu,Zhixiong Zeng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper surveys Multimodal Code Intelligence, examining how models generate and reason with code grounded in visual inputs such as screenshots and videos. It categorizes tasks and proposes verification-driven directions to enhance correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Programming tasks that involve visual artifacts (e.g., screenshots or charts) require integrating visual perception with code execution, which goes beyond text-only code synthesis approaches.

Method: The authors introduce a framework categorizing how code operates in various tasks, then organize benchmarks and methods into four domains based on task characteristics: GUI, scientific visualization, structured graphics, and frontier areas.

Result: The taxonomy enables connections between established tasks (e.g., artifact generation) and newer, more advanced areas, while offering insight into differing notions of correctness across these tasks.

Conclusion: Future multimodal code systems could advance through verification-focused methods, integrating multi-signal validation, cross-task transfer, and tests across execution states to ensure evidence-grounded results.

Abstract: While LLMs have substantially advanced text-to-code synthesis, many real programming tasks specify intent through visual artifacts such as screenshots, charts, documents, vector drawings, videos, and interactive states. These tasks require models to connect visual perception to executable programs, because correctness depends not only on syntax but also on layout, geometry, data semantics, editability, interaction behavior, and domain-specific constraints that apply after execution. This survey examines Multimodal Code Intelligence, covering systems that generate, edit, refine, execute, or reason with code under visually grounded inputs and outputs. We first formulate the field by the role that code plays in each task, distinguishing code as a rendered artifact, an editable symbolic structure, a scientific representation, an intermediate reasoning trace, or an executable policy or tool interface. We then organize benchmarks and methods into four domains: Graphical User Interface, Scientific Visualization, Structured Graphics, and Frontier Tasks and Frameworks. This taxonomy connects mature artifact-generation problems to emerging agentic and unified settings and allows us to compare how different tasks treat evidence of correctness. Looking ahead, we argue that future research may benefit from four verification-centered directions. Multi-signal validation can combine complementary evidence of correctness, multi-state verification can test behavior across execution trajectories, cross-task transfer testing can probe reusable visual-code skills, and verifiable agent traces can reveal whether agent actions are grounded in visual evidence. Together, these directions may move multimodal code generation from single-output imitation toward evidence-grounded executable systems.

</details>


### [210] [FinBalance: A Multi-Document Accounting Reconciliation Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15949)
*Sasank Tumpati,Devansh Agarwal,Ayush Kedia,Arjun Neekhra,Murari Mandal,Krishna Garg,Yash Sinha,Suman Gupta,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces FinBalance, a benchmark for multi-document accounting reconciliation, highlighting challenges faced by language models in generating accurate financial reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the gap in financial NLP evaluations, which often focus on prepared artifacts while neglecting the complexities of reconciling raw documents and balancing financial statements.

Method: A deterministic generator creates business scenarios, documents, and accounting data with inconsistency labels. Models' abilities are tested on reconstructing and verifying balance sheets across different difficulties and scenarios.

Result: Six language models were evaluated, achieving a maximum accuracy of 46% in generating balance sheets. Discrepancies were found between reported and reconciled balances, with limited improvement using citation-based prompting.

Conclusion: Current language models struggle with document linking, consistency checking, and reconciling financial data. Improvements require better integration of feedback mechanisms and handling of financial inconsistencies.

Abstract: Existing financial-NLP benchmarks mostly evaluate prepared artifacts such as filings, tables, or extracted values. Real accounting begins earlier: source documents must be reconciled into cited journal entries, aggregated into a balance sheet, and checked for contradictions. We introduce FinBalance, a multi-document accounting reconciliation benchmark built from source-document bundles across eight industries, three period types, and five difficulty levels. Human-authored business scenarios, accounting policies, tax/FX treatments, document schemas, distractors, and inconsistency templates are composed by a deterministic generator whose ledger produces journal entries,balance sheets, and 23 inconsistency-code labels. On a 710-record evaluation split, six contemporary LLMs reach at most 46% exact final-balance-sheet accuracy. Four models show a 26-41 pp gap between BS_exact, the model's reported balance sheet, and BS_recon, the balance sheet obtained by replaying its entries through our ledger. Models often recover numerically plausible entries but fail to bind them to supporting documents and aggregate them consistently. Citation-pressure prompting barely changes document-linking errors, while ledger-feedback ablations substantially improve reported balance sheets and expose inconsistency-detection trade-offs. Expert finance reviewers validate the benchmark design and labels.

</details>


### [211] [SAG: SQL-Retrieval Augmented Generation with Query-Time Dynamic Hyperedges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15971)
*Yuchao Wu,Junqin Li,XingCheng Liang,Yongjie Chen,Yinghao Liang,Linyuan Mo,Guanxian Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SAG (SQL Retrieval Augmented Generation) improves multi-hop reasoning and external knowledge retrieval for language models by using event-based SQL joins instead of static graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing retrieval methods struggle with structured constraints, multi-hop reasoning, and maintaining global static graphs.

Method: SAG dynamically links semantic events using SQL join queries and relies on database infrastructure for incremental updates and scalability.

Result: SAG outperforms on multi-hop benchmarks, achieving up to 80% Recall@5. It scales to production with low latency.

Conclusion: SAG offers a scalable, efficient solution for external knowledge retrieval with significant multi-hop reasoning improvements.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) offers an effective approach for large language models to access external knowledge. However, existing methods rely on dense similarity retrieval and face inherent limitations in handling structured constraints and multi-hop reasoning. Incorporating knowledge graphs partially alleviates these issues, but at the cost of semantic fragmentation, high maintenance overhead, and difficult incremental updates. This paper introduces SAG (SQLRetrieval Augmented Generation), a structured architecture for retrieval and agent systems. Instead of pre-building a global static graph, SAG converts each chunk into one semantically complete event and a set of indexing entities, then uses SQL join queries to dynamically link events that share entities into local hyperedges,constructing, at query time, a dynamically instantiated local index structure. This design avoids the need for global graph rebuilding and ongoing maintenance; the system naturally supports incremental writes, concurrent processing, and continuous scaling through its reliance on standard database infrastructure. Across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHop, and MuSiQue, three standard multi-hop benchmarks,SAG achieves the best results on 8 out of 9 Recall@K metrics, reaching 80.0% Recall@5 on MuSiQue, the benchmark with the highest multi-hop reasoning demands.SAG has also been deployed at a production scale of hundreds of millions of data items, with online retrieval latency kept within seconds. Project site and code are available at https://github.com/Zleap-AI/SAG-Benchmark.

</details>


### [212] [Formalize Once, Edit the Rest: Efficient Lean-Based Answer Selection for Math Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15972)
*Ji Feng,Zhouxing Shi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BASE, a system designed to lower the cost and improve the accuracy of using Lean for formal proof verification by reusing components of formalized statements across multiple candidate answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to mitigate the high computational cost associated with existing methods that individually formalize each candidate answer when leveraging Lean-based mathematical reasoning systems.

Method: The proposed BASE pipeline generates a single base formalization per problem and reuses it by editing localized parts of the answer expressions for the remaining candidates. A rewriter model, LEANSCRIBE, is trained to automate this editing process.

Result: BASE achieves improved selection accuracy while significantly reducing formalization costs, with up to 5x fewer autoformalization calls at K=8 across diverse benchmarks, and the cost reduction scales with higher K.

Conclusion: The approach provides a Pareto improvement over previous methods, offering both cost and accuracy advantages when scaling LLM applications with multiple candidate answers, demonstrating an impactful innovation in mathematical reasoning workflows.

Abstract: With large language models (LLMs) increasingly applied to mathematical reasoning, formal proof assistants such as Lean can be leveraged to verify reasoning outputs with machine-checkable rigor, enabling use cases such as answer selection in test-time scaling with K sampled candidate answers. However, employing Lean requires that LLM outputs, originally in natural language, first be formalized. Existing Lean-based answer-selection work uses an autoformalization model to generate a formal statement in Lean for each candidate answer independently, incurring a significant computational cost. We propose BASE, a base-and-edit pipeline that formalizes a single base candidate per problem and derives the remaining K-1 statements by editing the answer expression in place. To facilitate this, we train a rewriter model LEANSCRIBE to localize the answer in the base formalization and generate a reusable edit function for the other K-1 candidates. BASE simultaneously improves selection accuracy and reduces formalization cost - a Pareto improvement that holds on all 12 (dataset, solver) configurations across four benchmarks and three solvers, cutting autoformalizer calls by about 5x at K=8, with the reduction expected to become larger as K grows. Code is available at https://github.com/ucr-rai/base-and-edit.

</details>


### [213] [A Large-Scale Multi-Dimensional Empirical Study of LLMs for Conversation Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15974)
*Weixiao Zhou,Gengyao Li,Xianfu Cheng,Junnan Zhu,Feifei Zhai,Zhoujun Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces OmniCSEval, a benchmark containing 1,800 diverse conversations with varied context lengths, aiming to address limitations of current LLM evaluation methods. It uses innovative evaluation frameworks to assess 28 LLMs empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in LLM summarization, current evaluations are limited in their scenarios, inputs, and fine-grained analysis. The paper aims to address these gaps by creating a robust and comprehensive evaluation benchmark.

Method: The authors developed OmniCSEval, a benchmark with six scenarios and conversations up to 32k tokens. They implemented a bidirectional fact-checking framework for fine-grained evaluations and used a human-LLM collaborative pipeline for reliable assessments.

Result: The study evaluated 28 LLMs grouped by reasoning capabilities and scale, revealing critical insights into their challenges, reasoning impact, and efficiency. It also provides guidelines for LLM deployment in real-world settings.

Conclusion: OmniCSEval offers a unified and comprehensive evaluation tool for LLMs, highlighting current limitations while providing critical insights and practical guidance for their application.

Abstract: Despite the significant advancement of LLMs in conversation summarization, their evaluation remains limited by insufficient scenarios, input lengths, and sample sizes. Furthermore, existing benchmarks often omit frontier reasoning systems and efficient small models, or lack fine-grained, multi-dimensional assessments. To bridge these gaps, we propose OmniCSEval, a unified benchmark comprising 1,800 diverse conversations across six real-world scenarios, featuring context lengths ranging from 128 to 32k tokens. For fine-grained evaluation, we employ a bidirectional fact-checking framework that integrates key fact matching to assess completeness and conciseness, alongside summary fact verification to evaluate faithfulness. To ensure reliable assessment, we establish a human-LLM collaborative pipeline for key fact extraction and a multi-LLM consensus verifier for summary fact decomposition. Leveraging this framework, we evaluate 28 LLMs across four distinct categories grouped by reasoning capability and model scale. Our extensive empirical study reveals critical insights regarding the cross-scenario challenges current LLMs continue to face, the impacts of reasoning and scale, and the efficiency and adaptability of reasoning models. We also provide guidance for system selection in real-world deployments.

</details>


### [214] [ROMPAR: Morphological Completion and Demographic Unlearning for Romanian-Accented Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15984)
*Andrei-Marius Avram,Aureliu-Valentin Antonie,Ştefan-Bogdan Badea,Andrei Florea,Robert-Nicolae Zaharoiu,Dumitru-Clementin Cercel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tackles challenges in automated transcription of Romanian and Moldavian parliamentary speech by introducing the ROMPAR dataset and a robust ASR system with multi-task adversarial training and LLM-guided decoding, achieving significant improvements in performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in parliamentary speech transcription, including demographic bias, dialectal variation, and technical issues like truncated utterances, which hinder transcription accuracy.

Method: The paper introduces the ROMPAR dataset with annotated ground truth and proposes a multi-task adversarial training framework to ensure demographic invariance. It also employs an LLM-guided decoding strategy to handle morphological completion of truncated words.

Result: The proposed framework lowers WER (Word Error Rate) and achieves a high F1-score of 96.6% in morphological reconstruction.

Conclusion: The methods and dataset presented tackle core challenges in parliamentary speech transcription effectively, offering improvements in transcription accuracy and robustness to variability in speaker demographics and dialect.

Abstract: Automated transcription of parliamentary proceedings faces significant hurdles due to demographic bias, dialectal variation, and technical artifacts such as utterance truncation during segmentation. This paper introduces the ROManian PARliamentary Speech Corpus (ROMPAR) dataset, a 17.80-hour corpus of Romanian and Moldavian parliamentary speech, featuring double-annotated ground truth and explicit labels for reconstructed word fragments. To build a robust ASR system, we propose a multi-task adversarial training framework that enforces demographic invariance across age, gender, and dialect. We address the inherent instability of adversarial objectives in generative architectures by introducing an exponential decay mechanism for the adversarial coefficients. Furthermore, we implement an LLM-guided decoding strategy with position-dependent weighting to facilitate morphological completion of truncated terminal words. Our results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces WER and achieves an F1-score of 96.6% in morphological reconstruction.

</details>


### [215] [GRACE-DS: a Guarded Reward-guided Agent Correction Environment in Data Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16000)
*Aleksandr Tsymbalov,Danis Zaripov,Artem Epifanov,Anastasya Palienko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GRACE-DS is a new evaluation framework for testing LLM-powered AutoML agents in a controlled, realistic environment for organizational ML tasks, demonstrating its robustness through substantial testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of evaluating LLM-powered AutoML agents before deployment, particularly their capabilities to handle realistic machine learning workflows specific to organizational requirements.

Method: The authors created GRACE-DS, an isolated evaluation environment with hidden validators that measure agent performance across workflow stages, including metrics like leakage avoidance, reproducibility, correction behavior, and reward alignment. They compared flexible iterative interaction with other baseline methods (e.g., single-shot, restart-based interactions).

Result: Flexible iterative interaction in GRACE-DS achieved better end-to-end predictive performance and protocol adherence compared to other methods across over 7,000 evaluation episodes.

Conclusion: GRACE-DS provides a robust tool for assessing how well LLM-based AutoML agents can perform machine learning workflows in realistic, production-like settings while meeting organizational needs.

Abstract: We introduce GRACE-DS, a Guarded Reward-guided Agent Correction Environment in Data Science for pre-deployment evaluation of LLM-powered AutoML agents. GRACE-DS is a set of evaluation metrics in an isolated environment that can be applied to tabular ML tasks specific to a particular organization. It exposes agents to realistic workflow stages, from planning and data inspection through feature engineering, model development, validation, and code repair to final submission, while hidden executable validators measure not only final predictive performance but also leakage avoidance, reproducibility, protocol validity, correction behavior, and reward alignment. The strongest structured regime, flexible iterative interaction (our approach), achieves higher end-to-end normalized hidden-test quality than single-shot generation, unstructured interaction, and restart-based baselines, while also improving protocol-valid completion. Validated across more than 7,000 episodes, these results establish GRACE-DS as a robust platform for assessing the capacity of LLM-based AutoML agents to execute machine learning workflows under production-like conditions and in accordance with organization-specific requirements.

</details>


### [216] [Bridging the Usability Gap: Lessons from Interpreting Studies for Machine Interpreting Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16009)
*Claudio Fantinuoli*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the inadequacies of current machine interpreting (MI) systems, proposing a shift from textual fidelity metrics to communicative effectiveness for evaluation, along with three new design priorities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current advances in machine interpreting show high textual fidelity but fail to replicate interpreter-mediated communication's user experience, leading to what the authors call the 'accuracy illusion.'

Method: The authors analyzed the limitations of MI systems by incorporating insights from interpreting studies and defined critical dimensions overlooked by these systems.

Result: They outlined three design priorities for future MI: agency, grounding, and experience, aimed at achieving genuine multilingual communication in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: By addressing these priorities, MI systems could overcome the usability gap, allowing for smoother real-time multilingual interaction that aligns better with professional interpreting practices.

Abstract: Machine interpreting (MI), the live, real-time branch of speech translation, has achieved remarkable progress on standard benchmarks, with some systems approaching human parity on textual fidelity. Yet the user experience remains far inferior to interpreter-mediated communication, revealing what we term the \emph{accuracy illusion}: systems that appear accurate on paper but fail in practice to support smooth, goal-oriented interaction. This paper defines MI as a distinct subfield of speech translation, with its own characteristics and the need for evaluation methods grounded in communicative effectiveness rather than isolated fidelity metrics. Drawing on insights from interpreting studies, we identify critical dimensions of professional interpreting practice that are overlooked by current systems, and consolidate them into three interdependent design priorities for future MI: \emph{agency} (context-sensitive initiative and repair), \emph{grounding} (multimodal and discourse-level situational awareness), and \emph{experience} (adaptive improvement through real interaction). Together, these priorities chart a path toward closing the usability gap and enabling systems that can sustain authentic multilingual communication in real time.

</details>


### [217] [Who Flips? Self- and Cross-Model Counterarguments Reveal Answer Instability in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16011)
*Nafiseh Nikeghbal,Amir Hossein Kargaran,Shaghayegh Kolli,Jana Diesner*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the stability of large language models' (LLMs') answers against plausible counter-arguments rather than solely relying on standard accuracy benchmarks. Researchers propose a controlled protocol to systematically evaluate this stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation that standard accuracy benchmarks cannot assess whether LLMs remain consistent when their correct answers are challenged by plausible counter-arguments.

Method: The authors designed a protocol that challenges LLMs' correct answers with coherent but incorrect arguments, measuring flip rates under varying conditions such as argument length, self-attribution, and source of arguments. They also developed a curated challenge set, MaxFlip, to intensify these flips.

Result: The study revealed significant variations in LLMs' answer stability (flip rates ranging from 17.5% to 97.3%) that are not reflected in accuracy alone. Self-attribution and pooling arguments from various models proved to increase the flip rates. MaxFlip further amplified flipping by up to 23.6 percentage points.

Conclusion: Current accuracy metrics are insufficient for evaluating LLMs' answer stability. The introduced protocol, along with the MaxFlip dataset, provides a new benchmark for investigating the robustness of LLMs against adversarial challenges, addressing a critical aspect beyond correctness.

Abstract: Standard accuracy benchmarks are designed to test how closely large language models (LLMs) approach correct answers, but are not suitable for testing whether LLMs stick with a correct answer when that answer is challenged by a plausible counter-argument. We introduce a controlled protocol for evaluating answer stability: after a model answers a multiple-choice question correctly, we challenge the model's answer with a coherent argument for an incorrect option and measure whether the model flips. The setup a) isolates argumentative content from overt social pressure and b) varies argument length, self-attribution, and cross-model source. Across seven frontier models and 57 MMLU subjects, flip rates range from 17.5% to 97.3%, revealing large differences in stability that are not captured by accuracy metrics alone. We find that self-attribution consistently increases flip rates (mean +7.1pp, up to +18.7pp). Also, pooling wrong-answer arguments across models and selecting the most effective one per question yields stronger adversarial challenges than relying on any single source model. We further construct MaxFlip, a curated challenge set that amplifies flips by up to +23.6pp over standard self-generated challenges. We release the protocol, challenge records, and MaxFlip to support stability evaluation alongside standard accuracy benchmarks. Materials are available at https://github.com/nafisenik/WhoFlips and https://hf.co/datasets/nafisehNik/WhoFlips.

</details>


### [218] [Scaling Human and G2P Supervision for Robust Phonetic Transcription](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16019)
*Alexander Metzger,Aruna Srivastava,Ruslan Mukhamedvaleev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study evaluates the effectiveness of Grapheme-to-Phoneme (G2P) models versus human annotation for phonetic transcription, revealing thresholds at which G2P offers diminishing benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The high cost of expert phonetic annotation necessitates exploring automated alternatives, particularly for diverse dialects and atypical speech.

Method: The research employs an 80-hour curated benchmark of various English speech types and assesses the scalability of automatic phonetic transcription using G2P models, alongside ASR pretraining techniques.

Result: It finds that G2P supervision only aids transcription when human annotation is limited (below 20-30 hours), and ASR pretraining significantly improves phonetic transcription, especially for complex speech varieties.

Conclusion: G2P scaling has limited benefits beyond certain annotation thresholds, with ASR pretraining offering a more effective solution for robust phonetic transcription across diverse speech types.

Abstract: Expert phonetic annotation is costly, especially for non-standard dialects and atypical speech. A common alternative is using Grapheme-to-Phoneme (G2P) models to auto-generate phonetic labels from text transcripts at scale. We study how automatic phonetic transcription performance scales with human and G2P supervision in English. Using a curated 80-hour benchmark spanning native, non-native and post-stroke speech, we identify a supervision quality threshold: G2P supervision helps only when fewer than 20-30 hours of human annotation are available. Beyond this threshold, it provides no significant benefit and can reduce cross-dialect robustness. What is effective after this threshold is ASR pretraining which we use to achieve a 2.3x reduction in weighted phone feature error rate over prior systems, with strong gains on non-native and aphasic speech. These results suggest that quantity-driven G2P scaling may yield diminishing returns for robust generalization.

</details>


### [219] [In-Domain Supervised Pathology Report Classification: A Reproducible Pipeline from Data Curation to Production-Matched Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16026)
*Isaac Hands,Bin Huang,Adam Spannaus,John Gounley,Heidi Hanson,Eric Durbin,Sally R. Ellingson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a supervised pipeline to address the drop in out-of-distribution performance for biomedical NLP models, using reproducible methods for model training and evaluation on cancer registry data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to tackle the significant performance drop observed in supervised biomedical NLP models when they transition between cancer registries. This is particularly crucial for ensuring effective model deployment across different real-world registries.

Method: The authors propose a supervised pipeline including facility-stratified sampling, curation of in-domain training sets, creation of production-matched holdouts, and a blinded manual audit to estimate label noise and case prevalence.

Result: The pipeline demonstrated significant improvements, with the Kentucky model showing FNR 0.003 and FPR 0.097, compared to the baseline Seattle-trained model with FNR 0.010 and FPR 0.183. It also improved F1 scores from 0.860 to 0.922.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline effectively addresses the out-of-distribution performance drop, reduces label noise, and enhances model reliability for cancer registry data. The methodology is claimed to be robust and reproducible.

Abstract: We introduce an in-domain supervised pipeline designed to counter the out-of-distribution performance drop that hampers supervised biomedical NLP models, a problem observed when models trained on pathology reports are moved across cancer registries. Our contribution is a reproducible recipe for training a supervised classifier from routinely collected cancer registry data. It describes how to build the in-domain training set and a production-matched holdout, and to choose operating points that keep the false-negative rate (FNR) very low while keeping reviewer workload manageable. The pipeline standardizes data curation with facility-stratified sampling and separate handling of reports linked to registry cases, and includes a blinded manual audit to estimate positive-case prevalence and label noise. On a 418k-report holdout set, the Kentucky model achieved FNR 0.003 and false-positive rate (FPR) 0.097, improving over the Seattle-trained MOSSAIC OncoID baseline (FNR 0.010, FPR 0.183) and raising F1 from 0.860 to 0.922. In a blinded manual review of 600 reports, estimated positive prevalence declined from 0.500 to 0.398, indicating substantial label noise with errors concentrated in rare primary sites.

</details>


### [220] [From Argument Components to Graphs: A Multi-Agent Debate with Confidence Gating for Argument Relations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16047)
*Jakub Bąba,Jarosław A. Chudziak*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores extending a multi-agent paradigm for Argument Relation Identification and Classification (ARIC), using debates on uncertain cases to achieve better training-free performance and interpretability compared to traditional and fine-tuned methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing limitations of Large Language Models in Argument Mining tasks, such as lack of sophisticated reasoning and issues with self-correction mechanisms.

Method: The paper reformulates ARIC as a debate using the Proponent-Opponent-Judge architecture, introduces a confidence gating mechanism for selective debates, and evaluates performance on a corpus.

Result: Selective debates improved Macro F1 scores compared to all training-free methods, but debating all samples led to degraded performance. Generative approaches outperformed fine-tuned RoBERTa models, showing adaptability to argument under-representation.

Conclusion: Selective debating of uncertain cases shows promise for improving training-free methods in Argument Mining, achieving both high-performance metrics and interpretability.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly assessed and utilized in the field of Argument Mining (AM), thanks to their strong general reasoning capabilities. However, standard training-free models often miss sophisticated details, specifically in contexts where two parts of the text have to be analyzed together. Furthermore, self-correction mechanisms tend to reinforce initial hallucinations in reasoning. Overcoming these limitations typically requires expensive, domain-specific supervised fine-tuning. Recent work has shown that a multi-agent paradigm can address such weaknesses for the component classification task through dialectical refinement with a Proponent-Opponent-Judge architecture, setting a promising direction for training-free approaches in the field. In this paper, we extend and evaluate this framework on the Argument Relation Identification and Classification (ARIC) task, reformulating it as a debate over component pairs. Besides that, we introduce a confidence gating mechanism that enables debating only on the uncertain cases and accepting the initial prediction when confidence is high. On the UKP Argument Annotated Essays v2 corpus, we demonstrate that the selective debate achieves the highest Macro F1 among all training-free methods, while debate over all samples degrades performance below that of one of the baselines. All generative approaches also outperform fine-tuned RoBERTa models on Macro F1, suggesting that the under-representation of the Attack class was more damaging to supervised fine-tuning than to inference-only models. Additionally, our framework produces human-readable debate transcripts, offering interpretability absent from both single-agent and supervised classifiers.

</details>


### [221] [PVminerLLM2: Improving Structured Extraction of Patient Voice via Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16074)
*Samah Fodeh,Linhai Ma,Ganesh Puthiaraju,Srivani Talakokkul,Afshan Khan,Elyas Irankhah,Sreeraj Ramachandran,Ashley Hagaman,Sarah Lowe,Aimee Roundtree*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes PVminerLLM2, a model improving the extraction of structured information from patient-generated text using advanced optimization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Patient-generated texts hold valuable insights for patient-centered research but remain hard to analyze due to their unstructured nature. Existing models face challenges managing fine-grained, rare, and imbalanced data errors.

Method: PVminerLLM2 employs preference optimization with a token-level gated stabilization term, confusion-aware preference pair construction, token-importance weighting, and inverse-frequency reweighing.

Result: PVminerLLM2 significantly outperforms prior models and baselines in extracting structured information, showing gains of up to 4.43% in 'Code,' 3.50% in 'Sub-code,' and 1.55% in 'Span.'

Conclusion: Preference optimization and tailored methods address prior shortcomings in structured text extraction from patient data, enhancing outcomes and usability for healthcare research.

Abstract: Motivation: Patient-generated text contains critical information on patients' lived experiences, social context, and care engagement, but remains largely unstructured, limiting its use in patient-centered outcomes research. Prior work introduced the PV-Miner benchmark and PVMinerLLM models for structured extraction. However, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) alone struggles with rare, fine-grained, and unevenly distributed errors, particularly in token-critical structured outputs.
  Results: We present PVminerLLM2, an improved set of LLMs for structured patient voice extraction that applies preference optimization to address token-critical errors beyond the reach of supervised fine-tuning. Our method introduces (i) a preference objective with token-level gated stabilization term that prevents degradation of absolute token likelihood under preference optimization, and (ii) confusion-aware preference pair construction to better capture low-separation distinctions. We further incorporate token-importance weighting and inverse-frequency reweighing to address token imbalance and class skew. Across multiple model sizes, PVMinerLLM2 consistently outperforms strong baselines, achieving gains of up to 4.43% (Code), 3.50% (Sub-code), and 1.55% (Span), and outperforms baseline LLM trained with existing preference optimization methods.
  Availability and Implementation: The supplementary material, code, evaluation scripts, and trained models for PVminerLLM2 are publicly available at: https://github.com/Data-Mining-Lab-Yale/PVminerLLM2

</details>


### [222] [Long-Context Modeling via GSS-Transformer Hybrid Architecture with Learnable Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16093)
*Kuzey Torlak,Hüseyin Arda Arslan,Anıl Dervişoğlu,Beyza Nur Deniz,Onur Boyar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Parallel Hybrid Architecture (PHA), a model that combines Gated State Spaces, Grouped Query Attention, and Feed-Forward Networks for improved performance and efficiency, reaching near-Transformer level perplexity for long-context language modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the tradeoff between efficiency and performance in modeling long-range dependencies, where Transformers are powerful but computationally expensive, and State Space Models are efficient but struggle with detailed recall.

Method: The proposed Parallel Hybrid Architecture runs three independent parallel branches—GSS for global context, GQA for selective retrieval, and FFNs for additional processing—fused via a learnable mixing mechanism, allowing specialization across tasks.

Result: On WikiText-103, PHA achieves 16.51 PPL at 125M parameters, surpassing other hybrid and attention-based methods. At 180M parameters, it achieves comparable perplexity to Transformers but with higher throughput and memory efficiency. Results on OpenWebText similarly outperform standard Transformers and hybrid baselines.

Conclusion: Separating and combining the strengths of different sequence modeling paradigms into specialized parallel components allows PHA to achieve Transformer-level perplexity while significantly improving computational efficiency for long-context tasks.

Abstract: Modeling long-range dependencies remains a central challenge in natural language processing. Transformer architectures achieve strong performance via self-attention but scale quadratically ($O(N^2)$) with sequence length, while State Space Models (SSMs) scale linearly ($O(N)$) but suffer from a selective recall bottleneck, struggling to retrieve precise information from compressed states. This creates a fundamental tradeoff between efficiency and perplexity. To tackle these challenges, we propose the \textit{Parallel Hybrid Architecture (PHA)}, which runs Gated State Spaces (GSS), Grouped Query Attention (GQA), and Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) as independent parallel branches fused by a learnable mixing mechanism. Instead of forcing SSMs to approximate attention or serializing the two paradigms, PHA allows each branch to specialize: GSS captures global context, while attention performs selective retrieval, with FFN providing complementary processing. On WikiText-103, PHA achieves 16.51 PPL at 125M parameters, outperforming Hedgehog (16.70) and H3-125M (23.70). Scaling to 180M parameters yields 16.42 PPL, which gives comparable results with the pure attention baseline while delivering 24\% higher throughput and up to 40\% lower memory usage at long contexts. On OpenWebText, our 125M model achieves 19.72 PPL, outperforming standard Transformers (20.60) and GSS hybrid baselines (19.80). These results demonstrate that separating sequence modeling paradigms into parallel specialists enables Transformer-level perplexity with substantially improved efficiency for long-context language modeling.

</details>


### [223] [Towards Pareto-Optimal Tool-Integrated Agents with Pareto Ranking Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16111)
*Junyi Li,Xiaowei Qian,Yingyi Zhang,Wenlin Zhang,Guojing Li,Sheng Zhang,Xiao Han,Yichao Wang,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ParetoPO, a framework for aligning tool-using language models under conflicting objectives, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current alignment methods prioritize task accuracy at the expense of objectives like tool-use efficiency, which are crucial for practical deployment.

Method: The approach consists of a two-stage framework: (1) Hypervolume-guided dynamic scalarization adjusts reward weights based on Pareto frontier progress, (2) Pareto-ranking-based advantage computation promotes nondominated trajectories across conflicting objectives.

Result: Experimental results on reasoning tasks demonstrate superior policies in terms of accuracy-efficiency trade-offs achieved by ParetoPO compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: ParetoPO provides an effective solution for fine-grained multi-objective optimization, enhancing practical deployment of tool-integrated language models.

Abstract: Recent advances in tool-integrated language agents have significantly improved their ability to solve complex reasoning tasks. However, existing alignment methods predominantly focus on maximizing task accuracy, while overlooking auxiliary objectives such as tool-use efficiency, which are essential for practical deployment. To address this gap, we introduce ParetoPO, a two-stage multi-objective optimization framework for aligning tool-using large language models (LLMs) under competing objectives. In the first stage, ParetoPO leverages hypervolume-guided dynamic scalarization to adapt reward weights based on global Pareto frontier progress. In the second stage, it replaces scalarized learning signals with Pareto-ranking-based advantage computation, promoting nondominated trajectories through dominance-aware credit assignment. This design enables fine-grained, action-level optimization across multiple conflicting objectives. Experimental results on mathematic reasoning and multi-hop QA tasks show that ParetoPO consistently discovers policies with superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to static and heuristic baselines.

</details>


### [224] [AuAu: A Benchmark for Auditing Authoritarian Alignment in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16127)
*Andreas Einwiller,Max Klabunde,Florian Lemmerich*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "AuAu," a benchmark to evaluate the extent to which large language models (LLMs) exhibit or promote authoritarian attitudes. Key insights show that authoritarian tendencies can be manipulated and differ with context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing global prevalence of authoritarianism and the central role of LLMs in day-to-day life prompt the need to assess whether LLMs exhibit or propagate authoritarian attitudes.

Method: The authors developed a benchmark called AuAu that combines three evaluation approaches: (i) psychometric tests based on validated instruments, (ii) contextual behavior evaluation using scenario-based vignettes, and (iii) analysis of responses to realistic user prompts. They also evaluated 17 LLMs and manipulated their output using authoritarian system prompts.

Result: The study finds that all tested models exhibit significant authoritarian tendencies under psychometric evaluations, though these tendencies diminish in more realistic downstream tasks. 15 out of 17 models could be manipulated to promote authoritarianism using tailored prompts.

Conclusion: The research highlights the need for systematic auditing and mitigation strategies to address the risk of LLMs generating outputs with authoritarian tendencies.

Abstract: The worldwide surge of authoritarianism, combined with the increasing central role in users' everyday lives, raises the question of to what extent specific models exhibit or promote authoritarian attitudes and characteristics. We introduce AuAu, a comprehensive benchmark that aims to assess the risk of LLMs generating responses with authoritarian tendencies. This benchmark combines three evaluation approaches: (i) psychometric questions from an extensive pool of 15 human validated instruments; (ii) contextual behavior vignettes probing intended actions in concrete situations; and (iii) responses to realistic user prompts. Unlike prior work, AuAu evaluates not only a general closeness towards authoritarianism but also the established sub-concepts Authoritarian Aggression, Authoritarian Submission, and Conventionalism. Evaluating 17 models from China, the EU, Russia, and the USA, we find that all tested models exhibit substantial authoritarian response rates under the psychometric evaluation, though rates drop significantly in increasingly more realistic downstream task. We further find that an authoritarian system prompt easily manipulates 15 out of 17 models to promote increased authoritarianism. Our results underscore the need for continued, systematic auditing of LLM-based AI systems to detect and ultimately mitigate undesired authoritarian tendencies in generated output. Our code and data are available at: https://github.com/andreaseinwiller/AuAu

</details>


### [225] [XAI-Grounded Explanation Generation for Speech Deepfake Detection with Training-Free Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16137)
*Yupei Li,Qiyang Sun,Xiaoliang Wu,Chenxi Wang,Berrak Sisman,Björn W. Schuller*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework combining traditional explainable AI (XAI) with multimodal large language models (LLMs) to improve explanations for speech deepfake detection (SDD) systems. Testing shows improvements in explanation accuracy and faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for explaining SDD are either low-level and difficult for humans to interpret (traditional XAI) or too generic and ungrounded, due to a lack of task-specific datasets.

Method: The authors introduced a training-free framework that integrates heuristic XAI evidence with multimodal LLMs. They also constructed a new grounded explanation dataset using PartialSpoof.

Result: By leveraging XAI evidence, their method improved explanation accuracy inside the system by over 45%, as validated through human and faithfulness evaluations.

Conclusion: Integrating XAI evidence with multimodal LLMs offers a more effective way to generate specific and grounded explanations, enhancing the trustworthiness and interpretability of SDD systems.

Abstract: Speech deepfake detection (SDD) systems require trustworthy explanations for reliable decision-making. Existing explanation ways mainly fall into two categories. Traditional explainable AI (XAI), such as gradient-based attribution, produces low-level attribution signals tightly coupled with model decisions, and harder to be understood by human than natural language explanations. Meanwhile, large language model (LLM)-based explanation generation often produces generic and ungrounded descriptions due to the lack of heuristic evidence and task-specific supervision, stemming from limited grounded explanation datasets for SDD. We therefore propose a training-free explanation framework that integrates XAI evidence with multimodal LLMs to generate grounded and specific explanations. Using the PartialSpoof dataset, we construct a grounded explanation dataset and show that methods with XAI increase inside accuracy by over 45\%, verified through human evaluation and faithfulness checks.

</details>


### [226] [GRACE: Step-Level Benchmark for Faithful Reasoning over Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16151)
*Hoang Pham,Dong Le,Anh Tuan Luu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces GRACE, a human-annotated benchmark for detecting step-level faithfulness errors in reasoning tasks with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods fail to detect where and what types of errors occur in the intermediate steps of reasoning traces produced by CoT models.

Method: A benchmark, GRACE, is developed with human annotations assessing faithfulness, error categories, and natural language explanations of errors within CoT traces. A bottom-up data-driven taxonomy categorizes errors into two tracks: deductive (inference) and factual grounding errors.

Result: Human annotations show significant headroom for improvement in current reasoning models. Additionally, incorporating faithfulness signals improves the accuracy and reliability of model outputs.

Conclusion: GRACE provides valuable insights into reasoning model errors, aiding in their diagnostics and improvement. Its integration into training pipelines enhances reasoning capabilities and result faithfulness.

Abstract: Many reasoning tasks require models to reason over input context, from document-grounded question answering to rule-based deduction. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting produces traces that appear transparent, yet individual steps can silently deviate from the source evidence, even when the final answer is correct. Existing methods detect hallucinations at the response level but fail to identify where in the chain a failure occurs or what type it is. We introduce GRACE, the first human-annotated step-level faithfulness benchmark with a data-driven error taxonomy for context-grounded textual reasoning. GRACE covers CoT traces from 10 models across 4 source datasets, with each step annotated for faithfulness, error category, and natural language explanation. A data-driven taxonomy, discovered bottom-up via unsupervised clustering, organizes failures into two tracks: GRACE-Inference (deductive errors) and GRACE-Grounding (factual grounding errors), with four categories each. The evaluation set is human-annotated and challenging by design. Our experiments reveal substantial headroom for current models. In addition, integrating step-level faithfulness signals into reinforcement learning pipelines improves both downstream accuracy and reasoning reliability.

</details>


### [227] [Weaving Multi-Source Evidence for Biomedical Reasoning: The BioMedHop Benchmark and BioWeave Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16211)
*Xingyu Tan,Shiyuan Liu,Xiaoyang Wang,Qing Liu,Xiwei Xu,Xin Yuan,Liming Zhu,Wenjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces BioMedHop, a biomedical question answering benchmark focused on reasoning over multi-source structured evidence, and proposes BioWeave, a framework that achieves state-of-the-art results on the benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing biomedical QA benchmarks mainly focus on specific types of reasoning, such as exam-style knowledge or short-range inferences, but fail to address the challenges of reasoning over multi-source and structured evidence.

Method: The paper presents BioMedHop, a benchmark containing 10,045 instances for source-conditioned graph reasoning over evidence from knowledge graphs, documents, and web resources. It also introduces BioWeave, a reasoning framework that integrates evidence paths from these sources into a unified graph for answer verification.

Result: BioWeave achieved the best performance on the BioMedHop benchmark, outperforming a strong hybrid baseline (ToG-2) by 10.5% in overall average performance, and enabling smaller models to match reasoning capabilities of much larger ones like GPT-4-Turbo.

Conclusion: BioMedHop fills a research gap for evaluating multi-source biomedical reasoning, and BioWeave proves to be a strong framework, advancing the performance of biomedical QA systems.

Abstract: Biomedical question answering (QA) increasingly requires reasoning over interacting entities, where supporting evidence is scattered across biomedical knowledge graphs, literature documents, and web-accessible resources. However, existing biomedical QA benchmarks mainly focus on exam-style knowledge, literature comprehension, or short-range multi-hop inference, leaving source-conditioned graph reasoning and evidence topology construction underexplored. To fill this gap, we introduce BioMedHop, a multi-source graph-grounded benchmark for evaluating biomedical reasoning over structured evidence topologies. BioMedHop contains 10,045 instances across KG, document, web, and hybrid evidence settings, covering shared-neighbor matching, intersection reasoning, path-based reasoning, and counting, with option-based, open-ended, and numeric count renderings. To support this benchmark, we further propose BioWeave, a source-aware reasoning framework that retrieves biomedical KG paths, gathers supporting clues from documents and web sources, assembles them into a unified evidence graph, and verifies answers through entity-level evidence support. Comprehensive experiments show that BioWeave achieves the best overall performance among compared methods on BioMedHop, outperforming the strong hybrid baseline ToG-2 by 10.5% in the overall average. Moreover, BioWeave consistently improves different LLM backbones and enables smaller models, such as Qwen3-4B, to achieve reasoning performance comparable to GPT-4-Turbo.

</details>


### [228] [PACT: Privileged Trace Co-Training for Multi-Turn Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16215)
*Zhenbang Du,Jun Luo,Zhiwei Zheng,Xiangchi Yuan,Kejing Xia,Dachuan Shi,Qirui Jin,Qijia He,Shaofeng Zou,Yingbin Liang,Wenke Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes PACT, a training framework for multi-turn tool-use agents utilizing expert traces to optimize training without constraining trajectories during rollout.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in post-training multi-turn tool-use agents, such as sparse rewards in RL and over-constrained trajectories in supervised learning.

Method: Introduce PACT, which uses expert traces for training optimization through a trace-conditioned RL surrogate, component-aware SFT loss, and prompt-only anchoring.

Result: Experiments show PACT significantly outperforms RL- and SFT-based baselines on multi-turn agent tasks like FTRL, BFCL, and ToolHop.

Conclusion: PACT effectively leverages expert traces during training to balance optimization and flexibility, improving multi-turn tool-use learning models.

Abstract: Multi-turn tool-use agents must reason, call tools, and adapt to observations across several interaction turns. Post-training such agents is challenging, as reinforcement learning often suffers from sparse rewards and weak credit assignment despite matching the prompt-only inference setting, while supervised fine-tuning on expert traces provides dense process supervision but can over-constrain the model to fixed trajectories. To tackle this, we propose PACT, a Privileged trAce Co-Training framework for multi-turn tool-use agents. The key idea is to use expert traces only as training-time optimization signals rather than rollout-time hints. PACT keeps rollout generation prompt-only, then uses expert traces to guide optimization through two complementary signals: a trace-conditioned RL surrogate that evaluates prompt-only rollouts under expert-trace context, and a component-aware SFT loss that supervises reasoning prefixes and tool-calls with annealed strength. To reduce over-reliance on the training-only trace context, PACT further introduces a prompt-only anchoring.
