Motivation: To address the trade-offs between utility, privacy, efficiency, and stability in existing privacy-preserving split learning methods for resource-constrained users training LLMs.

Method: MIXGUARD employs token-level obfuscation, representation-level obfuscation, and adaptive gradient perturbation mechanisms, in combination with a calibration model trained on a public dataset.

Result: MIXGUARD demonstrates strong results in maintaining model utility, enhancing privacy protection against data reconstruction attacks, and achieving robustness under adaptive attacks across various tasks and LLM scenarios.

Conclusion: MIXGUARD effectively combines privacy and utility in split learning, overcoming limitations of previous methods and providing a stable, efficient solution for LLM training.

Abstract: Split learning provides a practical paradigm for resource-constrained users to train Large Language Models (LLMs) by offloading computation-intensive layers to a server while keeping raw data local. However, existing privacy-preserving split learning methods still face a difficult trade-off among utility, privacy, efficiency, and stability. Specifically, these methods often suffer from substantial utility degradation, remain vulnerable to advanced data reconstruction attacks, incur prohibitive computational and communication overhead, or exhibit unstable performance across different tasks. In this paper, we propose MIXGUARD, a novel mixup-based privacy-preserving split learning framework for LLMs. MIXGUARD introduces token-level obfuscation, representation-level obfuscation, and adaptive gradient perturbation mechanisms, which operate jointly to preserve useful learning signals while preventing privacy leakage to the server. Technically, MIXGUARD first constructs a lightweight calibration model on a public dataset to refine the approximated target representation, and then applies this model during privacy-preserving fine-tuning on private data. We conduct extensive experiments on four classification tasks and four text generation tasks across multiple LLM families, model sizes, architectures, and fine-tuning strategies. The results show that MIXGUARD preserves model utility comparable to non-split training baselines, consistently achieves stronger privacy protection than existing split learning defense methods against state-of-the-art data reconstruction attacks, and remains robust under adaptive attack settings.

</details>


### [260] [LLM-based Visual Code Completion for Aerospace Geometric Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16806)
*Hau Kit Yong,Robert Marsh,Edmar A. Silva,András Sóbester,Stuart E. Middleton*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a Large Language Model-based visual programming copilot application for aerospace design, introducing a custom plugin and dataset, and evaluates its utility through user trials, finding it helpful but limited by slow inference times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in LLM-based geometric design copilot tools in the aerospace industry, which demands safety and explainability, by creating a domain-specific solution.

Method: The study employs a variant of the ReAct methodology tailored for visual programming with GPT 5.4, introduces the Wingbuilder plugin and the Aerospace Visual Programming Dataset (AVPD), and evaluates the tool via user trials.

Result: The tool provided helpful suggestions appreciated by aerospace engineers, though its slow inference time made it more suitable for complex design tasks rather than frequent, simpler ones.

Conclusion: The proposed copilot system is a promising tool for aerospace engineering design, receiving positive user feedback despite practical limitations, paving the way for further refinement and adoption.

Abstract: Recent advances in both Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) have seen a step change in their ability to perform visual code completion, but the aerospace industry, which prioritizes safety and explainabilty over rapid LLM adoption, currently has no publicly announced LLM-based geometric design copilot systems in commercial use by aerospace Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs). This paper presents a LLM-based visual programming copilot application for aerospace engineering design tasks, using a visual programming variant of the ReAct methodology and GPT 5.4. In addition to the copilot, we describe Wingbuilder, a new Grasshopper plugin library with custom components for aerospace-specific geometry abstraction, and an associated Aerospace Visual Programming Dataset (AVPD) with 18 aerospace expert designed tasks at different levels of difficulty alongside ground truth solutions. We evaluate our copilot application with a user trial involving two experienced aerospace engineers from a large aircraft manufacturing company. We find our copilot visual programming ReAct methodology was successful in generating suggestions that participants found helpful, but slow ReAct inference times limit its usefulness to more complex time-consuming tasks where waiting for good copilot solution suggestion was worthwhile. Participants reported they liked the tool and would be willing to use it in the future.

</details>


### [261] [Connecting Speech to Words through Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16807)
*Gabriel Pirlogeanu,Dan Oneata,Horia Cucu,Herman Kamper*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a model to link spoken words to written counterparts using only images and their spoken descriptions, outperforming neural baselines in retrieval tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of mapping written words to spoken words in settings where textual supervision is unavailable.

Method: The method combines image captioning to build a vocabulary of written words, associates them with spoken utterances, and employs unsupervised word discovery to align the two.

Result: The approach surpasses a strong neural baseline in spoken word retrieval and keyword spotting tasks, showcasing its effectiveness in English.

Conclusion: It validates the feasibility of building spoken-written word mappings without text supervision and advocates extending this work to low-resource languages.

Abstract: How can we learn the mapping between written words and their spoken counterparts in the absence of explicit textual supervision? We present a visually grounded method for building a vocabulary of spoken words using only images and their spoken descriptions. First, image captioning systems are used to build a vocabulary of written words representing salient visual concepts in the images. For each word, we then find utterances whose image captions contain that word. Then we use an unsupervised word discovery technique to align these utterances to locate instances of the target word. The result is spoken word segments that are linked to written words -- all accomplished without any text supervision. In spoken word retrieval and keyword spotting experiments, the proposed approach outperforms a strong neural baseline while being more interpretable. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the approach in English and motivate future work on low-resource languages without transcripts.

</details>


### [262] [Understanding the Behaviors of Environment-aware Information Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16817)
*Ruifeng Yuan,Chaohao Yuan,David Dai,Yu Rong,Hong Cheng,Hou Pong Chan,Chenghao Xiao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines how Large Language Models (LLMs) can adapt their query strategies for different retrievers using reinforcement learning, revealing the need for retriever-specific optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge that various retrievers require distinct query formulation strategies to achieve optimal performance in retrieval-augmented generation tasks.

Method: The authors employ reinforcement learning to teach LLMs how to tailor their query formulation strategies to specific retrievers and introduce a branching-based rollout technique to improve multi-retrieval-step training stability.

Result: Empirical findings show that LLMs can effectively adapt to retriever-specific query styles, and performance improves with human guidance and larger model sizes.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for retriever-aware query adaptations in retrieval-augmented generation systems and provides actionable insights for further development.

Abstract: Recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches have demonstrated strong capability in handling complex queries, yet current research overlooks a critical challenge: different retrievers require fundamentally different query formulation strategies for optimal performance. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of how LLMs can learn to adapt their query formulation strategies for different retrievers via reinforcement learning (RL). Our empirical study reveals that RL effectively teaches an LLM to tailor its queries to specific retriever characteristics. We discover that different retrievers exhibit surprisingly distinct optimal query styles (e.g., descriptive vs. question-like), suggesting strategies learned for one retriever ineffective for another. We further show that performance can be enhanced by incorporating retriever-specific human guidance and by scaling model size. To facilitate learning over multi-retrieval-step trajectories, we introduce a branching-based rollout technique that improves training stability. Our work provides the first empirical evidence and actionable insights for building truly retriever-aware RAG systems. Code and resources are available at https://github.com/LCO-Embedding/Envs-aware-Information-Retrieval.

</details>


### [263] [How Much Can We Trust LLM Search Agents? Measuring Endorsement Vulnerability to Web Content Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16821)
*Yimeng Chen,Zhe Ren,Firas Laakom,Yu Li,Dandan Guo,Jürgen Schmidhuber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposed SearchGEO, a framework to evaluate endorsement corruption risks in LLM-based web-search agents, revealing varying vulnerabilities across 13 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the risk of LLM-based web search agents endorsing manipulated web content that could mislead users.

Method: Developed SearchGEO, a framework including a manipulation pipeline, attack taxonomy, and evaluation metrics, applied to analyze 13 LLMs on 308 cases each.

Result: Discovered varied attack success rates across models, ranging from 0.0% to 31.4%, and identified significant behavior differences in assessing adversarial content.

Conclusion: LLM web search agent safety should prioritize reliability when dealing with adversarial content, highlighting the need for improved evaluation metrics and model robustness.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based search agents synthesize open-web content into actionable recommendations on behalf of users, creating a risk that attacker-published pages are transformed into endorsed claims. We introduce SearchGEO, a controlled evaluation framework for measuring endorsement corruption in LLM-based web-search agents, combining a web-evidence manipulation pipeline, a five-mode attack taxonomy, and multiple output-level metrics. We evaluate 13 LLM backends on 308 cases each. Results show that vulnerability patterns vary across backends: overall attack success rate (ASR) ranges from 0.0% on Claude-Sonnet-4.6 to 31.4% on Gemini-3-Flash, the strongest attack mode differs by model family, and the same deployment scaffold could amplify or decrease ASR on different backends. An auxiliary agent-skill probe, where endorsement becomes an install command, exposes a sharp split among otherwise robust backends: Claude over-rejects while GPT over-trusts. These findings argue for treating recommendation reliability under adversarial search content as a first-class dimension of backend safety evaluation.

</details>


### [264] [Tying the Loop -- Tied Expert Layers in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16825)
*Martin Jaggi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Expert Tying to reduce memory usage in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures by sharing expert parameters across layers, achieving up to 2x memory savings with minimal performance degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) with Mixture-of-Experts architectures is efficient, but their memory requirements remain high, driven by the storage of expert parameters. Reducing memory overhead while maintaining performance is a pressing challenge.

Method: The researchers propose Expert Tying, a method that shares expert parameters across consecutive transformer layers while keeping routing and attention functions independent within each layer.

Result: Experiments show that Expert Tying can cut memory usage by almost half with virtually no impact on perplexity or the quality of downstream tasks in models like OLMoE, Qwen3, and DeepSeek MoEs.

Conclusion: Expert Tying is an effective architectural modification for improving the memory efficiency of MoE-based LLMs, striking a balance between computation and memory that supports scaling.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures efficiently scale Large Language Models (LLMs) by activating only a small fraction of their experts per token, yet the full parameter count - dominated by the expert parameters - must be held in training and inference memory. To address this, we introduce Expert Tying, an architectural modification that shares expert parameters across consecutive transformer layers while preserving independent, layer-wise routing and attention.
  We evaluate this approach across common, state-of-the-art architectures, including OLMoE, Qwen3, and DeepSeek-style MoEs. Our pretraining experiments demonstrate that tying experts can reduce memory footprint by almost 2x at virtually no degradation in perplexity or downstream quality. By exploiting the parameter redundancy inherent in MoE pathways, our method provides a highly favorable compute-to-memory trade-off, advancing efficient training and scaling of next-generation LLMs.

</details>


### [265] [Does Traversal Order Matter? A Systematic Study of Tree Traversal Methods in Transformer Grammars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16836)
*Zongru Liu,Pengyu Ji,Pengcheng Wang,Kewei Tu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores and expands linearization strategies for Transformer Grammars (TGs) by introducing new traversal strategies and evaluates their impact on various linguistic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Transformer Grammars largely rely on Depth-First Traversal (DFT) for linearizing syntactic trees, which may be suboptimal for certain tasks. The authors aim to explore alternative traversal strategies to enhance language modeling performance.

Method: The authors introduce Breadth-First Traversal (BFT) and Production-Rule Traversal (PRT) strategies. They integrate these traversals with several syntactic tree configurations and masking strategies, then evaluate their efficacy across tasks like language modeling, syntactic generalization, and summarization.

Result: The study highlights trade-offs between nested composition (DFT) and global lookahead (BFT/PRT), offering insights into the strengths and weaknesses of each strategy for different tasks.

Conclusion: The paper provides actionable recommendations for designing Transformer Grammars to suit specific tasks by leveraging appropriate traversal strategies and tree configurations.

Abstract: Transformer Grammars (TGs) enhance language modeling by incorporating syntactic tree structures. Despite the potentially significant impact on model performance of how syntactic trees are linearized in TGs, existing studies rely solely on Depth-First Traversal (DFT) for linearization. In this paper, we expand the traversal design space by exploring Breadth-First Traversal (BFT) and a novel hybrid traversal strategy, Production-Rule Traversal (PRT), which combines the structural lookahead of BFT with the early lexical generation of DFT. We integrate these traversal methods with varying tree configurations and masking strategies, and empirically evaluate their performance on language modeling, syntactic generalization and summarization. We reveal the inherent trade-offs between nested composition and global lookahead, providing actionable recommendations for designing task-aware Transformer Grammars.

</details>


### [266] [Data-Driven Decoding of Russell's Circumplex Model of Affect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16843)
*Amdjed Belaref,Samir Sadok,Zineb Noumir,Renaud Seguier*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines whether Transformer-based models encode emotional relationships in line with Russell’s circumplex model. Results show high alignment, especially with multimodal fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models excel in emotion representation but often lack interpretability. The research aims to validate whether these models naturally encode psychological theories like Russell's circumplex model.

Method: The study evaluates embeddings from RoBERTa and wav2vec 2.0 and their multimodal fusion using datasets like MSP-Podcast and generated stimuli. The alignment of these embeddings with psychological emotion models was analyzed.

Result: Multimodal fusion of text and speech showed perfect topological alignment with Russell’s circumplex model, and text embeddings reproduced fine-grained emotion mappings in zero-shot scenarios.

Conclusion: The findings illustrate that emotion-related psychological theories, such as Russell's model, are inherently encoded in Transformer-based embeddings, bridging psychological insights and machine learning.

Abstract: Affective computing increasingly relies on deep learning to represent emotions, yet latent spaces often remain opaque, high-dimensional black boxes. This paper investigates whether Transformers' embeddings recover the geometric regularities of Russell's circumplex model. We unify two complementary experiments testing the hypothesis that, after training models on text and speech, their resulting latent spaces encode a topology consistent with valence-arousal and reproduce human-like neighborhood relations. Specifically, we evaluate deep representations extracted from Transformer-based text (RoBERTa) and speech (wav2vec 2.0) encoders, along with a multimodal Transformer fusion architecture, across naturalistic datasets like MSP-Podcast and controlled LLM-generated stimuli. Our analysis reveals that multimodal fusion of text and audio yields perfect topological alignment with Russell's primary emotion ordering. Furthermore, in a zero-shot setting using generic text embeddings, projected fine-grained emotion terms fall close to their established human-mapped coordinates. Our contribution is a novel, data-driven framework for validating emotion models, demonstrating that Russell's circumplex structure is intrinsically encoded in the embeddings of these modalities rather than being solely an artifact of human labeling, thereby bridging the gap between psychological theory and representation learning.

</details>


### [267] [Robust Dual-Signal Fusion: Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Gating with Compressed Chain-of-Thought Refinement for Irony Detection in Social Media Texts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16845)
*Ankit Bhattacharjee,Krityapriya Bhaumik*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Robust Dual-Signal (RDS) framework for zero-shot irony detection, achieving competitive results without supervised fine-tuning, outperforming benchmarks in certain tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of zero-shot irony detection in Large Language Models (LLMs) that natively rely on literal semantic interpretations.

Method: Proposed the RDS framework, a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture, incorporating Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning and symbolic processing without supervised fine-tuning. Evaluated on benchmark datasets.

Result: RDS achieved 78.1% accuracy and 0.777 Macro F1 on TweetEval, and outperformed benchmarks with a zero-shot F1 of 0.6726 on iSarcasm.

Conclusion: The RDS framework effectively leverages a combination of neural and symbolic reasoning, and its full integration delivers a statistically significant advantage for sarcasm detection tasks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) natively default to literal semantic interpretations, making zero-shot irony detection a persistent challenge. We introduce the Robust Dual-Signal (RDS) Fusion framework, a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture that compresses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning trajectories without Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Evaluated on a strictly held-out TweetEval test set (N=734), RDS achieves 78.1% accuracy and a Macro F1 of 0.777, matching the absolute performance ceiling of the fine-tuned BERTweet. On the heavily imbalanced iSarcasm dataset, the frozen CoT pipeline filters 22.5% of out-of-distribution hallucinations, yielding a zero-shot Macro F1 of 0.6726 and Ironic F1 of 0.4821, outperforming multiple heavily supervised SemEval transformer ensembles. A statistical ablation confirms this structural synergy: adding the symbolic prior to the neural baseline yields no significant gain (p = 0.242), and the marginal benefit of adding the CoT pipeline to that prior is heavily compressed (p = 0.149). Only the complete, concurrent fusion of all three signals achieves a statistically validated improvement over the baseline (p = 0.005).

</details>


### [268] [Follow the Latent Roadmap: Navigating Revocable Decoding for Diffusion LLMs with Anchor Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16847)
*Yizhen Yao,Qinglin Zhu,Runcong Zhao,Xiangxiang Dai,Yanzheng Xiang,Yulan He,Lin Gui*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ASRD, a training-free framework that enhances the performance of diffusion-based large language models by mitigating recurring decoding errors and improving generation quality without sacrificing speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion large language models face a critical trade-off between decoding speed and generation quality due to error propagation and local error reinforcement in revocable decoding processes.

Method: ASRD decouples the decoding context into trusted 'Anchor Tokens' and uncertain candidates, using anchor-guided generation and anchor-perturbed verification to rectify and destabilize errors.

Result: ASRD achieved up to 6.4% accuracy improvements and a 7.2x acceleration in inference throughput on math and coding benchmarks compared to existing remasking baselines.

Conclusion: ASRD effectively addresses the dual challenges of decoding quality and speed in diffusion large language models, offering a robust and efficient decoding framework.

Abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) offer a promising avenue for parallel generation but face a trade-off between decoding speed and quality. While revocable decoding strategies attempt to mitigate errors by verifying and remasking tokens, they typically operate within a mixed-quality context. This leads to two critical failures: \textit{Error Propagation}, where new tokens absorb toxic information from erroneous context, and \textit{Local Error Reinforcement}, where errors mutually reinforce each other to evade detection. To alleviate these challenges, we propose ASRD (Anchor Supervised Revocable Decoding), a training-free framework that operates within the embedding space. ASRD explicitly decouples the decoding context into trusted \textit{Anchor Tokens}, which are identified via temporal consistency, and uncertain candidates. Leveraging a dynamic Anchor Tokens Cache, we introduce two complementary mechanisms: (1) Anchor-Guided Generation, which injects entropy-weighted anchor signals into masked positions to implicitly rectify attention toward the reliable global skeleton; and (2) Anchor-Perturbed Verification, which applies orthogonal perturbations to uncertain candidate tokens, destabilizing and remasking errors driven by fragile local consensus. Extensive experiments on math and coding benchmarks demonstrate that ASRD outperforms recent remasking baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of up to 6.4\% while accelerating inference throughput by up to 7.2$\times$.

</details>


### [269] [Revisiting the Systematicity in Negation in the Era of In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16867)
*Hitomi Yanaka,Taisei Yamamoto*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs struggle with fully understanding negation in sentences but can partially succeed with demonstrations and in-context learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate how well LLMs comprehend and process negated sentences.

Method: The paper explores both behavioral and representational systematicity in understanding negation, conducting experiments on negation cue extraction and scope recognition.

Result: LLMs show partial success in recognizing negation cues and scope, with performance affected by output format. Function vectors are robustly constructed for cue extraction but less so for scope recognition.

Conclusion: While LLMs can handle negation to some extent, achieving robust understanding of negation remains challenging.

Abstract: Understanding the meaning of negated sentences remains one of the challenges for language models, even in the era of large language models (LLMs). We analyze systematicity regarding LLM understanding of negation from two perspectives: behavioral systematicity and representational systematicity. For behavioral systematicity, we confirm that through demonstrations and in-context learning, LLMs can recognize negation expressions and scope within sentences to some extent, but they fail to achieve perfect performance. In particular, the difficulty of the negation scope recognition for models varies depending on the output format. For representational systematicity, we analyze the extent to which function vectors can be robustly constructed from in-context examples for tasks that are essential to understanding negation. The experiments suggest that while function vectors can be composed for negation cue extraction tasks, extracting function vectors for recognizing scope is more challenging.

</details>


### [270] [Understanding Scam Trends and Rail Paths from Reddit Self-Disclosure Narratives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16874)
*Yangjun Zhang,Mirko Bottarelli,Mark Hooper,Carsten Maple*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a dataset and analysis of online scam trends, paths, and community behavior on Reddit from 2023-2025, highlighting multi-rail scams and evolving support dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research lacks tracking of scam trends across years and examination of relations between scam rails, partly due to insufficient open-source datasets.

Method: The authors collected and heuristically annotated 21,304 Reddit posts from scam-related subreddits for trend analysis, labeled 1,800 posts with scam chains using an LLM-assisted approach, and used a topic model to analyze community support behavior.

Result: Scams predominantly follow multi-rail patterns, with yearly variations in rail types and path complexity. Community support behaviors on Reddit became more detailed over time.

Conclusion: This work enhances understanding of multi-stage scam behaviors and trends, aiding AI-related scam risk assessment, though findings may not generalize to other platforms.

Abstract: Online scam behavior is inherently multi-stage, and the lifecycle includes temporally ordered rails and events rather than isolated signals. Existing works analyze characteristics of scam types and rails, but they do not track scam trends across years. Moreover, the work on the relations between rails is hampered due to the lack of open-source datasets with annotations and coverage of different scam types. To address these gaps, we build a dataset to analyze the yearly trend of scam characteristics and rail paths using Reddit self-disclosure narratives from 2023 to 2025. We collect 21,304 posts from scam-related subreddits with at least one rail among identity, communication, platform, and payment for trend analysis by heuristic annotation. Then, we label 1,800 posts containing explicit or recoverable scam chains by an LLM-assisted method for scam path analysis. The method is evaluated with human annotation. Lastly, we run a topic model on the comments of the posts to analyze the community support behavior. The results reveal that scam processes are predominantly multi-rail. Across years, different scam types and rail components dominate. Different scam types vary systematically in path complexity. Reddit support behaviors have become more detailed over time. This work supports synthetic scam chain data simulation and AI-related scam risk assessment, though findings may not generalise to other platforms.

</details>


### [271] [Compositional Reasoning Depth Predicts Clinical AI Failure: Empirical Evidence Consistent with Transformer Compositionality Limits in Electronic Health Record Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16890)
*Sanjay Basu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates why large language models (LLMs) struggle with answering complex EHR questions that require multiple reasoning steps, concluding that errors increase with reasoning complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is driven by the need to understand and predict errors made by LLMs in clinical EHR question-answering tasks, given theoretical constraints on their reasoning capabilities.

Method: A hop-count taxonomy was used to categorize the reasoning complexity of 313 annotated questions. Three language models (Claude-Sonnet-4-6, GPT-4o, GPT-5.4-2026-03-05) were tested to analyze accuracy across different reasoning levels.

Result: All models showed declining accuracy as the number of reasoning steps (hops) increased, with significant statistical support. The decline appears driven by compositional reasoning challenges rather than data truncation.

Conclusion: Hop count is a reliable indicator of where models might fail in EHR tasks, highlighting compositional reasoning limitations and informing AI deployment risk in clinical settings.

Abstract: Aggregate accuracy benchmarks conceal a systematic structure in how large language models fail at electronic health record (EHR) question answering: questions requiring more inferential steps produce disproportionately more errors. Motivated by theoretical results on transformer compositionality limits, we introduce a pre-specified hop-count taxonomy -- the number of distinct reasoning steps required to answer a clinical question from an EHR -- as a principled predictor of model failure. We annotate 313 clinician-generated MedAlign EHR question-answer pairs across four hop levels and evaluate 301 questions in a within-model ablation (claude-sonnet-4-6, zero-shot vs. extended thinking) and cross-architecture replications (gpt-4o and gpt-5.4-2026-03-05, zero-shot). All three models, spanning two providers and two OpenAI generations (GPT-4 and GPT-5), show monotone accuracy decline with hop count: Claude Sonnet zero-shot falls from 30.6% (hop=1) to 17.6% (hop=4) (Cochran-Armitage z=-2.30, p=0.011; OR per hop 0.72, 95% CI [0.56,0.92], p=0.008); GPT-4o replicates this (37.8% to 14.7%; OR 0.58 [0.45,0.75], p<0.001); and gpt-5.4-2026-03-05 confirms it (37.8% to 23.5%; OR 0.80 [0.66,0.98], p=0.027). A pre-specified context-sufficiency audit shows higher-hop questions are not differentially disadvantaged by EHR truncation (answerability 93-95% at hops 2-4 vs. 79% at hop=1), so the decline reflects compositional reasoning difficulty. Extended thinking did not significantly flatten the accuracy-depth curve across three reasoning conditions, and thinking-token usage scaled with hop count (r=0.31, p<0.0001), consistent with the predicted O(k) computational requirement. Hop count is thus a theory-motivated, cross-architecture predictor of large-language-model error on EHR question answering, with direct implications for deployment risk stratification of clinical AI.

</details>


### [272] [Contrastive-Difference CKA Reveals Concept-Specific Structural Alignment Across Language Model Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16897)
*Xueping Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores how high-level concepts are encoded across different LLM architectures, finding moderate geometric alignment but strong functional transfer using a new diagnostic, CKA_Delta.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether different large language model (LLM) architectures encode concepts in a structurally compatible manner, and to develop methods to evaluate this compatibility.

Method: The paper introduces contrastive-difference CKA (CKA_Delta), a training-free diagnostic to measure concept-specific geometric and functional convergence across LLMs, analyzing six concept domains.

Result: Moderate geometric convergence coexists with near-perfect functional transfer across LLM architectures, with CKA_Delta outperforming standard CKA in identifying concept-specific convergence.

Conclusion: CKA_Delta serves as an effective tool for detecting concept universality across architectures and could aid in cross-architecture monitoring and architectural anomaly detection.

Abstract: Do different LLM architectures encode high-level concepts in structurally compatible ways? We systematically characterize a geometric-functional universality dissociation: across multiple concept domains and architectural families, moderate geometric convergence coexists with near-perfect functional transfer. Using contrastive-difference CKA (CKA_Delta), a training-free diagnostic that computes kernel alignment on per-sample contrastive differences, we isolate concept-specific convergence from generic similarity -- achieving significant discrimination where standard CKA cannot. The dissociation replicates across all six concept domains we test (five with p <= 0.017 geometric discrimination and safety as a converging-functional trend, p = 0.08), including two non-instruction concepts (code-vs-NL, reasoning-vs-recall) validated without system prompts; a single 70B--70B pair provides an observational note that universality may strengthen with scale, requiring replication with additional >=70B models. We position CKA_Delta as a practical regime classifier and architectural outlier detector (Gemma: d = 1.08, AUC = 0.79) rather than an absolute transfer-accuracy predictor, providing a training-free diagnostic for cross-architecture concept monitoring.

</details>


### [273] [Speaking the Language of Science: Toward a General-Purpose Generative Foundation Model for the Natural Sciences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16905)
*Mingyang Li,Yurou Liu,Jieping Ye,Bing Su,Ji-Rong Wen,Zheng Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LOGOS, a unified scientific generative language model for heterogeneous tasks across natural sciences, by encoding spatial interactions as token sequences. It performs better than domain-specific models and underlines the potential synergy between scientific models and large language models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of handling diverse scientific tasks with a unified framework and to explore the feasibility of integrating scientific foundation models with large language models (LLMs).

Method: A sequence-based autoregressive framework is developed, encoding scientific objects and their spatial interactions as discrete token sequences, without requiring explicit coordinates or geometric neural networks. Models are trained at various sizes (1B, 3B, 8B parameters).

Result: LOGOS achieves competitive or superior performance compared to domain-specific baselines across tasks. Larger models consistently yield better results, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness.

Conclusion: LOGOS exemplifies the potential for a unified approach in AI for Science (AI4S). The study advocates aligning scientific foundation models with LLMs to leverage shared architectures and training paradigms, pushing for integrated AI frameworks in natural sciences.

Abstract: In this report, we present LOGOS (Language Of Generative Objects in Science), a scientific generative language model that unifies heterogeneous tasks across the natural sciences within a single autoregressive framework based on a shared scientific grammar. It encodes diverse scientific objects and their spatial interactions as token sequences over a common vocabulary. By representing spatial contact and constraint patterns as discrete tokens, the model captures complex structural interactions in a purely sequential manner, without relying on explicit coordinates or geometric neural networks. This unified representation enables a wide range of downstream tasks to be formulated consistently as next-token prediction in the same grammar space, creating strong alignment between continued multi-domain pre-training and downstream objectives. Across diverse tasks, LOGOS consistently matches or outperforms domain-specific baselines, providing preliminary evidence for the feasibility of "one model fits all" in the natural sciences. We train LOGOS models at different scales (1B, 3B, and 8B parameters) and find a consistent positive correlation between model size and performance. This suggests that the future of AI for Science (AI4S) may not lie in building an independent technical stack that is separated from large language models (LLMs). Instead, it may depend on deeply aligning scientific foundation models with LLMs through shared architectures, shared training paradigms, and shared inference infrastructure, so that LLMs can truly become a new entry point for AI4S. We release the model weights and associated resources to facilitate further research.

</details>


### [274] [LESS Is More: Mutual-Stability Sampling for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16908)
*Amr Mohamed,Guokan Shang,Michalis Vazirgiannis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new sampling strategy, LESS, to improve the efficiency of diffusion-based language models, reducing reverse steps and computational costs without a loss in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address inefficiencies in current diffusion large language models (dLLMs) caused by fixed reverse denoising steps that waste computation and sometimes make premature commitments during the decoding process.

Method: LESS is a training-free, model-agnostic adaptive sampling approach that dynamically determines when to unmask tokens. It uses a joint stability rule based on token prediction confidence, persistence, and stability of the predictive distribution.

Result: LESS was evaluated on multiple dLLMs across seven benchmarks and showed improved accuracy over current adaptive samplers while achieving a 72.1% reduction in reverse denoising steps. This significantly lowers inference latency and computational requirements.

Conclusion: LESS demonstrates a practical and efficient method to optimize decoding in dLLMs, making them more computationally and time-efficient without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) offer a promising alternative to autoregressive decoding by iteratively refining masked sequences, enabling parallel token updates and bidirectional conditioning. Their practical efficiency, however, is limited by sampling procedures that execute a fixed number of reverse denoising steps selected before decoding, spending computation on already-stable positions and sometimes committing unstable ones too early. We present \textsc{LESS}, a training-free, model-agnostic adaptive sampler that treats token commitment as an online stopping problem. \textsc{LESS} implements mutual-stability sampling through a joint stability rule that makes a masked position eligible for unmasking only when its top-1 prediction has high confidence, its top-1 token persists across recent reverse steps, and its predictive distribution is stable under top-$K$ inter-step Jensen--Shannon divergence. We evaluate \textsc{LESS} on Dream-7B, LLaDA-8B, and LLaDA-1.5-8B, covering full-sequence diffusion and semi-autoregressive blockwise sampling regimes, across seven benchmarks spanning general knowledge, math, and code. \textsc{LESS} improves average accuracy over strong training-free adaptive samplers while using $72.1\%$ fewer reverse steps than fixed-budget decoding. Since each reverse step requires a Transformer forward pass, these step-count reductions translate into fewer forward evaluations, lower measured wall-clock latency, and lower estimated inference compute.

</details>


### [275] [IMPACTeen: Intentions, Manipulation, Persuasion, Annotations, and Consequences in Teen Communication Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16910)
*Aleksander Szczęsny,Wiktoria Mieleszczenko-Kowszewicz,Maciej Markiewicz,Beata Bajcar,Tomasz Adamczyk,Jolanta Babiak,Grzegorz Chodak,Przemysław Kazienko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IMPACTeen is a Polish-English dataset designed for analyzing textual social influence in youth contexts, backed by 5,100 annotations from diverse perspectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the need for research data focusing on social influence in adolescent settings by creating a specialized, multi-annotated, and linguistically diverse dataset.

Method: The dataset was constructed using constrained language model (LLM) generation, followed by human editing, annotation, and validation from five diverse perspectives.

Result: IMPACTeen consists of 1,021 texts and 5,100 individual annotation records, covering dimensions like influence techniques, intentions, consequences, and reactions.

Conclusion: IMPACTeen is a multi-dimensional, linguistically-diverse dataset useful for studying social influence detection, annotator dynamics, and language model evaluation.

Abstract: IMPACTeen is a dataset of textual social influence scenarios spanning interpersonal, media-based, and digital settings in an adolescent context. It contains 1,021 texts, 5,100 individual annotation records, and gold labels for social influence techniques, with each text annotated from five distinct perspectives: teenagers, parents, psychologists, communication experts, and teachers. The resource was constructed through constrained LLM generation, followed by a two-step human editing and validation phase aimed at ensuring youth-context realism. A multi-dimensional annotation covered influence presence, techniques, intentions, consequences, resistance, reactions, and annotation confidence. The dataset supports research on social influence detection, annotator disagreement, cross-lingual modeling, and the training and evaluation of language models. The dataset was created in Polish and is accompanied by a corresponding English version.

</details>


### [276] [Exploring Extrinsic and Intrinsic Properties for Effective Reasoning with Code Interpreter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16934)
*Patomporn Payoungkhamdee,Napat Laosaengpha,Jenta Wonglertsakul,Pittawat Taveekitworachai,Pume Tuchinda,Panjapong Poobanchuen,Ekapol Chuangsuwanich,Can Udomcharoenchaikit,Samuel Cahyawijaya,Peerat Limkonchotiwat,Sarana Nutanong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores the behavioral properties that enable effective code reasoning in large language models (LLMs) via code interpreters and identifies methods to enhance performance both during inference and training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study seeks to address the underexplored behavioral properties underlying code reasoning abilities in LLMs, despite the proven effectiveness of code interpreters in augmenting reasoning.

Method: The authors analyze crucial tokens (extrinsic) and code-specific cognitive behaviors (intrinsic) across multiple LLMs, and investigate their effects on reasoning by augmenting inference and training processes.

Result: The study finds a correlation between stronger CI reasoning models and higher occurrences of crucial tokens and cognitive behaviors like verification and backtracking. It demonstrates how incorporating these properties during inference and training can improve performance in reasoning tasks while reducing errors.

Conclusion: The paper provides a systematic characterization of code interpreter reasoning in LLMs, noting its potential in enhancing reasoning tasks, while also pointing out limitations and factors that affect performance gains.

Abstract: Reasoning with a Code Interpreter (CI) has emerged as an effective paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through executable computation and iterative verification. Despite its growing adoption, the behavioral properties underlying effective code reasoning remain largely underexplored. In this work, we investigate code reasoning from two distinct perspectives inspired by prior studies of natural language reasoning: extrinsic properties, represented by crucial tokens, and intrinsic properties, represented by code-specific cognitive behaviors. Across multiple LLMs, we find that stronger CI reasoning models consistently exhibit a higher prevalence of crucial tokens and cognitive behaviors, particularly verification, backtracking, and backward chaining. Building on these observations, we examine how these properties can be leveraged during both inference and training. At inference time, appending code-specific crucial tokens improves performance on several reasoning capabilities, including mathematical, ordering, and optimization, while yielding limited benefits elsewhere. At training time, augmenting a state-of-the-art framework with code-specific cognitive behaviors improves supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning performance in two of three evaluated models. Further analysis shows that these behaviors reduce overthinking in incorrect responses and improve token efficiency, while also revealing factors that limit gains in a certain model. Our findings provide the first systematic characterization of effective reasoning with CI and demonstrate both the potential and limitations of leveraging key properties to improve CI-based reasoning.

</details>


### [277] [TokenPilot: Cache-Efficient Context Management for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17016)
*Buqiang Xu,Zirui Xue,Dianmou Chen,Chenyang Fu,Chiyu Wu,Caiying Huang,Chen Jiang,Jizhan Fang,Xinle Deng,Yijun Chen,Yunzhi Yao,Xuehai Wang,Jin Shang,Gong Yu,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TokenPilot is a framework to optimize context management in long-horizon large language model (LLM) sessions by reducing inference costs while maintaining competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the high inference costs associated with context accumulation in session-based LLMs, which is amplified by unconstrained text mutations in existing approaches.

Method: TokenPilot employs a dual-granularity approach: globally, it stabilizes prompt prefixes via Ingestion-Aware Compaction, and locally it uses Lifecycle-Aware Eviction to manage segment utility and offload irrelevant content.

Result: Experiments on PinchBench and Claw-Eval showed that TokenPilot reduced inference costs significantly by up to 61% in isolated mode and 87% in continuous mode, while maintaining performance levels comparable to previous systems.

Conclusion: TokenPilot effectively minimizes inference costs through a structured context management framework and provides a viable solution for improving the efficiency of session-based LLMs.

Abstract: As LLM agents are deployed in long-horizon sessions, context accumulation drives up inference costs. Existing approaches utilize text pruning or dynamic memory eviction to minimize token footprints; however, their unconstrained sequence mutations alter layouts, introducing prefix mismatches and cache invalidation. This reveals a critical trade-off between text sparsity and prompt cache continuity. To address this, we present TokenPilot, a dual-granularity context management framework. Globally, Ingestion-Aware Compaction acts as a framework harness to stabilize prompt prefixes and eliminate open-world environmental noise at the ingestion gate. Locally, Lifecycle-Aware Eviction monitors the ongoing residual utility of context segments, enforcing a conservative batch-turn schedule to offload content segments only when task relevance expires. Experiments on PinchBench and Claw-Eval under both isolated and continuous modes demonstrate that TokenPilot reduces costs by 61% and 56% in isolated mode, and 61% and 87% in continuous mode, while maintaining competitive performance compared to prior systems. TokenPilot has been integrated into LightMem2 at https://github.com/zjunlp/LightMem2.

</details>


### [278] [DEEPRUBRIC: Evidence-Tree Rubric Supervision for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17029)
*Minghang Zhu,Chuyang Wei,Junhao Xu,Yilin Cheng,Zhumin Chen,Jiyan He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Deep research agents can create reports using reinforcement learning (RL) based on rubric-based rewards, but these rubrics can be suboptimal if they don't fully capture the task's evidence needs. DeepRubric proposes a new approach to better construct query-rubric pairs for training, leading to significantly more efficient RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reinforcement learning (RL) methods for training research agents depend on rubric-based rewards. However, the quality of the rubrics is highly dependent on an accurate understanding of the task's evidence needs, which is often not reliable when inferred directly from queries.

Method: DeepRubric proposes a framework that starts by defining evidence-backed evaluation criteria through an evidence tree. It then uses these criteria to generate aligned query-rubric pairs, ensuring that RL training rewards align well with the task's information needs.

Result: By constructing 9K query--rubric samples, DeepRubric allows training of an agent called DeepRubric-8B. This agent performs comparably to state-of-the-art models while requiring 13x fewer GPU hours for reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: DeepRubric demonstrates that reversing the process of query-rubric generation—by focusing on evidence-backed evaluation criteria first—can significantly improve RL efficiency and performance of deep research agents.

Abstract: Deep research agents synthesize long-form reports by searching and reasoning over retrieved evidence. Reinforcement learning with rubric-based rewards improves these agents by optimizing them against checkable criteria that translate report quality into reward signals, but its efficiency depends on whether those criteria reliably capture the task scope and evidence needs. Most existing studies ask an LLM to generate rubrics for a given query, but when the model fails to infer the underlying information needs, the generated rubrics may be incomplete and reduce RL efficiency. To obtain more reliable query--rubric supervision, we introduce DeepRubric, a data construction framework that reverses this process: instead of inferring evaluation criteria for a given query, it first determines what an evidence-backed report should be evaluated on and then synthesizes aligned query--rubric pairs from those evaluation targets. Starting from a sampled seed topic, DeepRubric builds an evidence tree by recursively expanding evidence-backed sub-questions, whose leaves serve as atomic and verifiable evaluation targets. It then uses the evidence tree to synthesize the training query and rubrics, ensuring that the reward evaluates exactly the information requested by the query. Using DeepRubric, we construct 9K query--rubric supervision examples and train DeepRubric-8B with rubric-based GRPO, achieving comparable performance to prior open state-of-the-art deep research models across three benchmarks with roughly 13x fewer RL GPU-hours.

</details>


### [279] [KVEraser: Learning to Steer KV Cache for Efficient Localized Context Erasing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17034)
*Mufei Li,Shikun Liu,Dongqi Fu,Haoyu Wang,Yinglong Xia,Hong Li,Hong Yan,Pan Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces KVEraser, a learned method for locally editing tokens in the KV cache of long-context LLMs without global recomputation. This approach achieves comparable accuracy to full recomputation but at a significantly reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the challenge of post-hoc edits in the KV cache of long-context language models, where local changes propagate globally and conventional methods demand high computation costs proportional to the length of the suffix.

Method: KVEraser replaces only the cached key-value states of an erased span with learned steering states, avoiding global recomputation. The method uses a two-stage training process: a pre-training phase with generic span-neighbor data and a fine-tuning phase for specific tasks.

Result: KVEraser nearly matches the performance of full recomputation while offering a significant latency reduction (24% compared to 17.6x). It also generalizes effectively to unseen tasks and provides a 3--4x speedup over full recomputation in long-document QA tasks.

Conclusion: KVEraser provides an efficient and effective solution for localized context erasing in LLMs, drastically reducing computational costs while maintaining strong post-erasure performance in both in-domain and generalization tasks.

Abstract: Post-hoc context erasing over the KV cache is challenging because a local edit has a global consequence: once a span has been processed, its influence propagates into the cached states of all subsequent tokens. This issue arises naturally in long-context LLM applications, where stale retrieved facts, incorrect tool observations, retracted user preferences, or harmful prompt injections may be identified only after prefill. Exact erasing must then recompute all tokens after the deleted span, making its computational cost depend on suffix length rather than erased-span length. We introduce KVEraser, a learned KV-cache editing method for efficient localized context erasing. Given a processed context and a span to remove, KVEraser replaces only the KV states of the erased interval with learned steering states while reusing the remaining cache unchanged. To learn a transferable erasing mechanism, we build a two-stage training pipeline: generic span-neighbor pre-training teaches the eraser to suppress the influence of the erased span, while task-specific fine-tuning adapts this capability to downstream scenarios. Experiments show that KVEraser nearly matches full recomputation in post-erasure performance on in-domain tasks across 1K--32K context lengths, while its latency increases by only 24% compared with a 17.6x increase for full recomputation. KVEraser also generalizes to unseen long-document QA tasks with harmful factual distractors, achieving the best performance among approximate baselines with a 3--4x speedup over full recomputation.

</details>


### [280] [Benchmarking LLM Agents on Meta-Analysis Articles from Nature Portfolio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17041)
*Anzhe Xie,Weihang Su,Yujia Zhou,Yiqun Liu,Qingyao Ai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MetaSyn, a dataset for testing meta-analysis pipelines and finds that existing systems struggle significantly in identifying eligible studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of ground-truth benchmarks accommodating the full retrieval, screening, and synthesis pipeline in meta-analysis.

Method: MetaSyn was created using 442 expert-curated meta-analyses paired with a retrieval corpus, eligible and ineligible studies, and other detailed data. It benchmarks pipeline configurations, including RAG variants and a protocol-driven agent.

Result: No system reviewed achieved more than 52.7% recovery of eligible literature, showing critical limitations in screening despite retrieval recall up to 90.9%.

Conclusion: Existing systems and LLMs struggle with the meta-analysis pipeline, especially in differentiating eligible from ineligible studies, suggesting that current approaches need improvement.

Abstract: Meta-analysis is a demanding form of evidence synthesis that combines literature retrieval, PI/ECO-guided study selection, and statistical aggregation. Its structured, verifiable workflow makes it an ideal substrate for evaluating systematic scientific reasoning, yet existing benchmarks lack ground truth across the full retrieval-screening-synthesis pipeline. We introduce MetaSyn, a dataset of 442 expert-curated meta-analyses from Nature Portfolio journals. Each entry pairs a research question with PI/ECO criteria, a retrieval corpus of 140k PubMed articles, verified positive studies, hard negatives that are topically similar but PI/ECO-ineligible, and complete search strategies and date bounds.
  Benchmarking twelve pipeline configurations (nine RAG variants and a protocol-driven agent) reveals a critical screening bottleneck: despite a retrieval ceiling of 90.9% recall at K=200, no system recovers more than 52.7% of ground-truth included literature. Current LLMs fail to reliably separate eligible studies from PI/ECO-failing distractors in pools of comparable topical relevance. Stage-attributed metrics capture where systems succeed and fail; a single end-to-end score does not.

</details>


### [281] [Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17053)
*Peiyang Xu,Bangzheng Li,Sijia Liu,Karthik R. Narasimhan,Pramod Viswanath,Prateek Mittal,Xingyu Fu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Large language models struggle with identifying specific evidence in long or complex contexts. ContextRL, a novel reinforcement learning method, enhances reasoning by rewarding identification of supportive contexts, showing improvements over standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often fail to pinpoint critical details within long or complex contexts, undermining their reasoning capabilities in such scenarios.

Method: ContextRL uses an indirect RL objective by presenting a query, answer, and two similar contexts, rewarding models for selecting the correct supportive context. It leverages contrastive context data built for coding and multimodal reasoning domains.

Result: ContextRL yields +2.2% improvement over standard GRPO on long-horizon benchmarks and +1.8% on diverse visual question answering tasks. Baseline comparisons confirm the gains stem from the context-selection objective.

Conclusion: The proposed context-selection objective in ContextRL effectively enhances long-horizon and multimodal reasoning by enabling fine-grained evidence identification, surpassing traditional data-augmentation approaches.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often fail when answering requires identifying a small but decisive piece of evidence within a long or complex context, such as a single line in a tool trace or a subtle detail in an image. We propose ContextRL, a context-aware reinforcement learning (RL) method that improves long-horizon reasoning and multimodal performance through an \emph{indirect} auxiliary objective. Instead of supervising only the final answer, ContextRL presents the model with a query, an answer, and two highly similar contexts, and rewards it for selecting the context that supports the query--answer pair, thereby encouraging fine-grained grounding. We construct contrastive context data in two domains: for coding agents, trajectories serve as contexts, yielding 1k pairs built via condition filtering; for multimodal reasoning, images serve as contexts, yielding 7K pairs built via generative editing and similarity search. ContextRL achieves average gains of +2.2% over standard GRPO on 5 long-horizon benchmarks, and +1.8% across 12 diverse visual question answering benchmarks. To disentangle the effect of the proposed objective from that of additional data, we compare against data-augmentation baselines that repurpose the same contrastive contexts as standard query--context--answer examples. These baselines provide little to no improvement, showing that the gains arise from the proposed context-selection objective rather than from the contrastive data alone.

</details>


### [282] [The Value Axis: Language Models Encode Whether They're on the Right Track](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17056)
*Nick Jiang,Isaac Kauvar,Jack Lindsey*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates if language models track a 'value' axis related to goal achievement likelihood, finding connections to confidence and behavioral patterns like backtracking and steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding whether language models track an internal 'value' related to success likelihood in their strategies, and how this impacts their behavior and decision-making.

Method: Synthetic in-context data to construct a 'value' axis for Qwen3-8B; experiments involving steering behavior and applying preference optimization.

Result: Identified that activations along the 'value' axis correlate with behavioral traits like confidence and backtracking and that fine-tuning affects this axis in models.

Conclusion: Language models encode an internal estimate of the probability of goal success, influencing their confidence and behavior.

Abstract: We investigate whether language models internally track the value of their current trajectory, defined as the likelihood that their ongoing strategy will achieve their goals. Using synthetic, in-context reinforcement learning data, we construct a "value" axis for Qwen3-8B. We find that activations along this axis distinguish between high vs. low verbalized confidence, rollouts without and with backtracking, and correct vs. corrupted code. Steering towards high value causally suppresses self-correction and reduces explanatory verbosity, while steering towards low value induces backtracking and exploration. We demonstrate that direct preference optimization (DPO) can increase the internal value of rewarded behaviors (e.g. use a certain word), causing the model to act more confidently after exhibiting them. Finally, we apply the value axis to study in-the-wild settings. For example, we find that Qwen assigns low value to politically sensitive chat queries after post-training and that supervised fine-tuning increases internal confidence within the training domain. Our results suggest that language models linearly encode an estimate of expected goal success that modulates their confidence in pursuing a direction.

</details>


### [283] [From Static Inference to Dynamic Interaction: A Survey of Streaming Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04592)
*Junlong Tong,Zilong Wang,YuJie Ren,Peiran Yin,Hao Wu,Wei Zhang,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the fragmented understanding of streaming Large Language Models (LLMs) by proposing a unified definition, systematic taxonomy, and exploring applications, while maintaining an updated research repository.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLMs are not optimized for real-time, dynamic scenarios, which necessitates the development of a clear framework for streaming LLMs that can handle dynamic interaction and data flow.

Method: The authors provide a unified definition, propose a systematic taxonomy, perform in-depth methodological discussions, and explore real-world applications of streaming LLMs.

Result: A clarified understanding of streaming LLMs with a systematic taxonomy and a repository of relevant research to guide advancements in this area.

Conclusion: This work establishes a foundational framework and guidelines to enhance the development and application of streaming LLMs, addressing ambiguities and fostering continuous advancements in the field.

Abstract: Standard Large Language Models (LLMs) are predominantly designed for static inference with pre-defined inputs, which limits their applicability in dynamic, real-time scenarios. To address this gap, the streaming LLM paradigm has emerged. However, existing definitions of streaming LLMs remain fragmented, conflating streaming generation, streaming inputs, and interactive streaming architectures, while a systematic taxonomy is still lacking. This paper provides a comprehensive overview and analysis of streaming LLMs. First, we establish a unified definition of streaming LLMs based on data flow and dynamic interaction to clarify existing ambiguities. Building on this definition, we propose a systematic taxonomy of current streaming LLMs and conduct an in-depth discussion on their underlying methodologies. Furthermore, we explore the applications of streaming LLMs in real-world scenarios and outline promising research directions to support ongoing advances in streaming intelligence. We maintain a continuously updated repository of relevant papers at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Awesome-Streaming-LLMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.CV'></div>

# cs.CV [[Back]](#toc)

### [284] [RAMS: Resource-Adaptive and Detection-Conditioned Model Switching for Embedded Edge Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14716)
*Kushal Khemani,Evan Leri,George Xu,Amit Hod*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces RAMS, a runtime controller for dynamic model selection among YOLOv8 tiers on edge devices, optimizing latency and accuracy under resource pressure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the need for real-time edge object detection systems that maintain a balance between inference latency and detection quality, especially under fluctuating resource availability.

Method: RAMS dynamically switches between YOLOv8 tiers (NANO, SMALL, MEDIUM) using five switching policies. It monitors device pressure and calibrates thresholds in real-time, incorporating new safety-focused metrics like SWAS and its oracle variant for policy evaluation.

Result: On multiple hardware platforms, the proposed system achieves significant improvements in latency and accuracy retention. For instance, on Jetson Orin TensorRT under load, it achieves 3.41 ms mean latency with selective tier locks, improving SWAS by up to 47.3%. However, switching performance is fundamentally limited by the baseline detector's recall.

Conclusion: RAMS effectively balances detection latency and quality, especially under heavy pressure. Detection-conditioned switching policies and metrics like SWAS offer meaningful advancements, though hardware and detector recall limitations remain challenges.

Abstract: Edge object detection on embedded hardware requires balancing inference latency and detection quality under changing resource pressure. We present RAMS, a lightweight runtime controller that monitors device pressure, calibrates switching thresholds from idle behavior, and dynamically selects among three resident YOLOv8 tiers (NANO/SMALL/MEDIUM at 320/416/640 px) without model-reload latency. RAMS defines five switching policies, including two detection-conditioned variants that prevent aggressive downgrades after recent vulnerable-road-user (VRU) detections. We further introduce the VRU-Weighted Accuracy Score (SWAS), a scalar metric for offline policy comparison without ground-truth annotations, together with an oracle-bounded variant that separates detector circularity from genuine tier-retention benefit. Across Raspberry Pi 5, x86 laptops, and Jetson Orin ONNX/TensorRT deployments, the same controller equations operate over a 37x latency range. On Jetson Orin TensorRT under heavy load, the safety2 policy achieves 3.41 ms mean latency, 5.6x faster than fixed-MEDIUM inference, while retaining 74% of its proxy accuracy through near-NANO operation with selective SMALL and MEDIUM locks during VRU-positive windows. Detection-conditioned switching improves SWAS by 25.4% under oracle scoring and 47.3% under detector-derived scoring relative to threshold-only policies under heavy load. Live KITTI evaluation reports per-tier VRU recall of 24.2%, 41.2%, and 59.0%, showing that reactive overrides are fundamentally limited by baseline detector recall.

</details>


### [285] [AI for Maritime Security: Comparative Evaluation of CNN and Vision Transformer Architectures for Maritime Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14720)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiayn,Shakeel Ahmad,Raza Hasan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates deep learning models for detecting ships in varied maritime environments, with the Vision Transformer (ViT) outperforming others, reaching 100% accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance maritime security through advanced AI and CV for better ship detection capabilities in varied real-time seascapes.

Method: Used a maritime image dataset of 6,468 images under varying weather conditions and evaluated six deep learning architectures, including CNN, transfer learning models, and Vision Transformer (ViT), against metrics like accuracy and error rates.

Result: Lightweight models are fit for resource-limited devices, but ViT achieved the best accuracy (100%), with minimal errors and fastest processing time.

Conclusion: AI and CV techniques can greatly improve maritime surveillance and autonomous navigation, with ViT being highly effective for accurate ship detection.

Abstract: This study aims to enhance maritime security by using advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computer Vision (CV) techniques. For this purpose, it was designed and assessed intelligent object detection systems that can detect the presence of ships on the sea surface under different real-time environments. To achieve this goal, a maritime image dataset with 6,468 images was used, covering different weather conditions like cloudy, foggy, rainy, and sunny environments. Six deep learning architectures were evaluated, including a base Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model, four transfer learning models (Xception, VGG16, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNetV2L), and a Vision Transformer (ViT) model. The models were compared using multiple performance indicators, including accuracy, Type I and Type II errors, model size, and video processing time. The results show that model performance varies depending on computational constraints and deployment conditions. While lightweight architectures are suitable for resource-limited devices, the ViT achieved the best overall performance, reaching 100% accuracy with the lowest error rates and the fastest video processing time. The findings highlight the potential of AI-driven computer vision systems for maritime surveillance, border protection, and autonomous navigation.

</details>


### [286] [Disagreement-Based Cross-Model Routing for Implicit Video Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14723)
*Durga Sandeep Saluru*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper addresses multiple-choice video question answering on the ImplicitQA benchmark by proposing a novel inference-time method, improving accuracy particularly in questions requiring cross-shot reference resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the challenge that existing video language models (LLMs) struggle with questions requiring inference beyond explicit information, such as unseen events and spatial reasoning, with traditional strategies like majority voting failing to improve performance.

Method: The proposed method, disagreement-based cross-model routing, uses triple-sampling from a primary video model to identify questions with sample disagreement. These subsets are then routed to a secondary model with complementary strengths for improved inference.

Result: The method improves accuracy significantly on the ImplicitQA benchmark, achieving +1.43 AvgAcc on a 1001-question validation set and +1.81 AvgAcc on a 172-question held-out test set, with notable gains in certain complex question categories.

Conclusion: Disagreement-based cross-model routing enhances video question answering accuracy by leveraging complementary model strengths, showing promise for adapting LLMs to hard inference tasks without requiring additional training.

Abstract: We study multiple-choice video question answering on the ImplicitQA benchmark, where the correct answer is never explicitly shown but must be inferred from off-screen events, line-of-sight cues, causal structure, and cross-shot spatial layout. On this benchmark a single frontier video LLM already operates near its accuracy ceiling, and we observe that conventional self-consistency strategies -- majority voting across repeated samples of the same model -- can hurt rather than help, because the model's errors on hard questions are correlated. We propose disagreement-based cross-model routing, a pure inference-time procedure that requires no labels and no training. We triple-sample a native-video model (Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview) at temperature zero, exploit the genuine sample-to-sample variance of its video-processing pipeline to identify the roughly 20% subset of questions where the three samples disagree, and route only that subset to a second model from a different family (Claude Opus 4.8) that consumes uniformly sampled frames with adaptive thinking. On the 1001-question validation set with public ground truth -- our main evaluation -- the method improves AvgAcc by +1.43 over the best single sample of the primary model, with per-category gains concentrated on Motion & Trajectory (+5.49), Inferred Counting (+3.45), and Vertical Spatial Reasoning (+1.82) -- the categories most dependent on cross-shot reference resolution. The same pipeline applied to the held-out 172-question CVPR 2026 ImplicitQA challenge test set achieves 82.03 AvgAcc / 79.71 MacroAvgAcc (+1.81 over the best single sample of the primary model), confirming the validation result on an independent split.

</details>


### [287] [VigilFormer: Deformable Attention for Video Anomaly Detection with Causal Risk Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14724)
*Xinze Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces VigilFormer, a real-time video anomaly detection framework that achieves high accuracy and speed via deformable attention and causal temporal modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for video anomaly detection struggle to balance detection accuracy and real-time processing, requiring an approach that merges efficient architectures with strong feature extraction.

Method: The method includes a Deformable Spatio-Temporal Encoder (DSTE) for sparse attention, a Causal Anomaly Classifier (CAC) using contrastive learning, and an Adaptive Confidence Scheduler (ACS) to skip redundant frames.

Result: VigilFormer achieves high AUC scores (87.83%, 97.21%, and 89.74%) on major datasets like UCF-Crime and ShanghaiTech, while maintaining real-time processing speeds (41.5 FPS on a single GPU).

Conclusion: VigilFormer successfully balances anomaly detection accuracy with computational efficiency, making it suitable for practical surveillance applications.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection in surveillance settings must balance detection accuracy against real-time throughput, a tension that existing methods address either through stronger feature extractors or more efficient architectures, but rarely both. We present VigilFormer, a unified framework that combines deformable spatio-temporal attention with causal temporal modeling to detect anomalies in untrimmed surveillance video. The proposed Deformable Spatio-Temporal Encoder (DSTE) attends to a sparse set of informative locations across frames, avoiding the quadratic cost of dense attention while retaining the ability to capture irregular motion patterns. A Causal Anomaly Classifier (CAC) applies dilated causal convolutions over snippet-level features and optimizes a contrastive multiple-instance learning objective that separates anomalous and normal representations without frame-level labels. To meet deployment constraints, an Adaptive Confidence Scheduler (ACS) dynamically skips low-information frames at inference time, reducing redundant computation in static scenes. Evaluated on UCF-Crime, ShanghaiTech, and CUHK Avenue, VigilFormer achieves AUC scores of 87.83%, 97.21%, and 89.74% respectively, at 41.5 FPS on a single GPU, outperforming recent weakly-supervised methods in both accuracy and speed.

</details>


### [288] [Interpolation between Convolution and Attention via K-Nearest Neighbors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14725)
*Mingi Kang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors propose "ConvNN," a unified framework that bridges the gap between Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers by showing that both convolution and self-attention are special cases of neighbor selection and aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Convolutional Neural Networks and Transformers are currently seen as fundamentally different methods in computer vision. The paper intends to unify them under one framework to facilitate a systematic exploration of their strengths.

Method: The authors introduce "ConvNN," which formulates both convolution and self-attention using a k-nearest neighbor aggregation process that selects neighbors based on either spatial proximity or feature similarity.

Result: ConvNN successfully recovers standard CNN operations and transformer self-attention as specific cases, while also enabling exploration of hybrid operations lying between the two methods by tweaking neighbor selection and aggregation strategies.

Conclusion: The study bridges the theoretical divide between convolution and self-attention, offering a flexible framework (ConvNN) for advancing computer vision by combining aspects of both neural network families.

Abstract: The shift from Convolutional Neural Networks to Transformers has reshaped computer vision, yet these two architectural families are typically viewed as fundamentally distinct. Convolutional Neural Networks are defined by spatially local convolution operations, while Transformers rely on global self-attention. We argue that convolution and self-attention, despite their apparent differences, can be unified within a single k-nearest neighbor aggregation framework. The critical insight is that both operations are special cases of neighbor selection and weighted aggregation. Convolution selects neighbors by spatial proximity while self-attention selects by feature similarity, revealing that they lie on a continuous spectrum rather than representing categorically different computations.
  We introduce Convolutional Nearest Neighbors (ConvNN), a unified framework that formalizes this connection. ConvNN exactly recovers standard and depthwise convolution by restricting neighbor selection to normalized spatial coordinates, and exactly recovers self-attention and its sparse variants, including KVT-attention, by replacing spatial proximity with scaled dot-product similarity.
