Conclusion: By introducing techniques such as sub-stochastic kernel-based contractions and occupation-measure frameworks, the paper provides viable solutions to the inverse reinforcement learning problem in MFGs with average rewards, enabling accurate recovery of expert policies.

Abstract: We study inverse reinforcement learning for discrete-time, infinite-horizon mean-field games (MFGs) under an average-reward criterion. Expert demonstrations are assumed to arise from a stationary mean-field equilibrium under an unknown reward, and the goal is to recover a policy explaining the observed behaviour via the maximum causal entropy principle. We formulate the inverse problem by enforcing consistency with the expert mean-field term and long-run feature expectations, treating two reward classes within a unified occupation-measure framework. For finite-dimensional linear rewards, we give a convex dual reformulation with an explicit log-partition objective, and prove smoothness and curvature properties justifying constant-step-size gradient descent. For infinite-dimensional RKHS rewards, we develop a Lagrangian relaxation whose inner-maximising policy is characterised by a soft Bellman equation. The main obstacle is the absence of a discount-factor contraction. We resolve this by introducing a minorisation-based sub-stochastic kernel that yields a strict contraction of the soft Bellman operator. We establish Fréchet differentiability and Lipschitz smoothness of the log-likelihood score, leading to a gradient ascent algorithm with convergence guarantees. Two numerical examples, a malware-spread MFG and an RKHS-based consumer-choice model, show that the recovered policies closely match expert behaviour.

</details>


### [747] [A Validated LBM Dataset and Pipeline for Surrogate Modeling of Turbulent 3D Obstructed Channel Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16765)
*Lukas Schröder,Shubham Kavane,Harald Köstler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a validated data pipeline to train neural operators for 3D turbulent flows and proposes benchmarks to compare surrogate models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of validated datasets and benchmarks necessary for training and evaluating neural operators for 3D turbulent flow prediction.

Method: Developed pipeline generating training data for 3D channel flows using lattice Boltzmann solver verified against experimental measurements and grid resolution studies. Planned to evaluate Fourier Neural Operators and U-Net variants.

Result: Proposed a reproducible pipeline that is validated and enables standardized comparison of neural operators with physical benchmarks.

Conclusion: The research establishes a framework to evaluate the performance, accuracy, and computational efficiency of neural operators in modeling turbulent flows and invites community feedback for further improvement.

Abstract: Evaluating neural operators for 3D turbulent flow requires validated datasets with physical benchmarks. We present a reproducible pipeline generating training data for 3D channel flows around generated geometries at Re=1,000-10,000. Our lattice Boltzmann solver with cumulant collision operators is rigorously verified against experimental measurements (Strouhal number, drag coefficients, turbulent fluctuations) with comprehensive grid convergence studies at resolution 1024x512x512. Building upon an established framework, this validated pipeline enables standardized surrogate model comparison. We outline planned systematic evaluation of Fourier Neural Operator and U-Net variants on forecasting, super-resolution, and error correction tasks, using physics-informed metrics to assess turbulent energy cascade representation. Future work will compare computational efficiency between numerical solvers and neural surrogates, exploring practical application. We seek community feedback on our validation approach, planned benchmark methodology, and evaluation priorities for neural operators in turbulent flows.

</details>


### [748] [Taming Curvature: Architecture Warm-Up for Stable Transformer Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16768)
*Sameera Ramasinghe,Ajanthan Thalaiyasingam,Hadi Mohaghegh Dolatabadi,Chamin Hewa Koneputugodage,Gil Avraham,Violetta Shevchenko,Yan Zuo,Karol Pajak,Alexander Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes methods to stabilize the training of billion-parameter Transformers by controlling curvature using an efficient curvature estimator and architectural warm-up techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large-scale Transformers often faces instability issues such as loss spikes and training divergence, which waste computational resources. Understanding and controlling curvature can help mitigate these problems, but existing curvature-controlling methods are complex and impractical for large-scale models.

Method: The paper introduces a fast online estimator for tracking the largest preconditioned Hessian eigenvalue based on a warm-start variant of power iteration. Using insights from curvature growth, the paper proposes a new technique called architecture warm-up, which incrementally increases network depth during training to stabilize curvature.

Result: The proposed curvature estimator is efficient and accurate even at the billion-parameter scale. Training instabilities correlate with surges in curvature, and architecture warm-up successfully stabilizes training. The method outperforms existing stabilization techniques without delaying convergence.

Conclusion: Efficient curvature tracking and architecture warm-up are effective strategies to manage training instabilities in large-scale Transformers, offering computational benefits and better training stability compared to current methods.

Abstract: Training billion-parameter Transformers is often brittle, with transient loss spikes and divergence that waste compute. Even though the recently developed Edge of Stability (EoS) theory provides a powerful tool to understand and control the stability of optimization methods via the (preconditioned) curvature, these curvature-controlling methods are not popular in large-scale Transformer training due to the complexity of curvature estimation. To this end, we first introduce a fast online estimator of the largest (preconditioned) Hessian eigenvalue (i.e., curvature) based on a warm-started variant for power iteration with Hessian-vector products. We show theoretically, and verify empirically, that the proposed method makes per-iteration curvature tracking feasible at billion parameter scale while being more accurate. Using this tool, we find that training instabilities coincide with surges in preconditioned curvature and that curvature grows with depth. Motivated by these observations, we propose architecture warm-up: progressively growing network depth to carefully control the preconditioned Hessian and stabilize training. Experiments on large Transformers validate that our approach enables efficient curvature tracking and reduces instabilities compared to existing state-of-the-art stabilization techniques without slowing down convergence.

</details>


### [749] [GD$^2$PO: Mitigating Multi-Reward Conflicts via Group-Dynamic reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16771)
*Haotian Liu,Yihao Liu,Jingwei Ni,Siyuan Huang,Xinpeng Liu,Pengyu Cheng,Jiajun Song,Ruijin Ding,Junfeng Li,Zhechao Yu,Mengyu Zhou,Hongteng Xu,Xiaoxi Jiang,Guanjun Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GD$^2$PO, a reinforcement learning algorithm designed to handle multi-dimensional reward conflicts by employing conflict-aware filtering and query-level reweighting to improve training efficiency and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As reinforcement learning for large language models increasingly relies on multi-dimensional rewards, there is a need for algorithms that can handle diverse and often conflicting objectives during training.

Method: The proposed method, GD$^2$PO, uses a conflict-aware filtering mechanism to exclude rollouts with severe reward-wise disagreements and introduces query-level reweighting to dynamically adjust update intensity based on reward consensus.

Result: Experiments on multi-reward scenarios, such as tool calling and human preference alignment, show that GD$^2$PO significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of learning efficiency and performance.

Conclusion: GD$^2$PO effectively addresses the challenges of multi-dimensional reward conflicts in reinforcement learning, providing a valuable improvement over existing algorithms in terms of both training efficiency and result optimization.

Abstract: As LLMs advance, post-training reinforcement learning (RL) increasingly relies on multi-dimensional rewards to cultivate comprehensive capabilities. This shift demands new algorithms capable of optimizing diverse and potentially competing objectives simultaneously. To address this, existing methods such as Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (GDPO) decompose the overall score into independent reward groups, then compute the RL loss separately within each group. However, this strategy still encounters multi-reward conflicts: a single rollout can yield positive advantages on certain reward dimensions but negative ones on others, causing opposing signals to cancel each other out during aggregation, further hindering RL training efficiency. Inspired by Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization (DAPO), which improves RL training efficiency by filtering out ineffective rollouts with near-zero advantages, we propose Group-Dynamic reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (GD$^2$PO). Specifically, GD$^2$PO employs a conflict-aware filtering mechanism to mask out rollouts suffering from severe reward-wise disagreement. By preventing conflicting signals from canceling each other out, this masking strategy preserves and enhances the magnitude of effective RL advantages, thereby significantly accelerating learning efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce query-level reweighting to dynamically adjust the update intensity of each query based on its overall reward consensus. Experiments on various multi-reward scenarios, including tool calling and human preference alignment, demonstrate that GD$^2$PO consistently and significantly outperforms existing baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/GD2PO.

</details>


### [750] [We Need Explanation Cards to Connect Explanation Algorithms to the Real World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16786)
*Eric Günther,Balázs Szabados,Kristof Meding,Gunnar König,Sebastian Bordt,Ulrike von Luxburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Algorithmic explanations often fall short of aiding users due to complexity and lack of transparency. The authors propose "Explanation Cards" to enhance clarity, robustness, and validity of algorithmic explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between what algorithmic explanations appear to convey and their actual meaning or utility, especially when they are uninformative or require expert interpretation.

Method: Introduction of "Explanation Cards" that augment algorithmic explanations with information on robustness, validity, and clear interpretation guidelines. These were demonstrated using examples like counterfactual explanations and SHAP.

Result: The proposed Explanation Cards provide users with the necessary guidance for correctly interpreting algorithmic explanations, while alleviating the burden on users by shifting responsibility to providers.

Conclusion: Explanation Cards make algorithmic explanations more practical and transparent, potentially operationalizing explainability requirements such as those in the EU AI Act.

Abstract: Algorithmic explanations are intended to help stakeholders understand opaque algorithmic decisions, but in practice, they often fall short. First, the meaning of algorithmic explanations is often not what one might intuitively expect, so expert knowledge is required to interpret them correctly. Second, recent work has shown that popular explanation algorithms are uninformative about the behavior of complex decision functions. Together, these issues create a gap between what explanations appear to convey and what they actually provide. In this work, we propose Explanation Cards for Explanation Algorithms, which augment standard explanations with complementary information about robustness and validity, as well as clear instructions for interpretation. The complementary information can render otherwise uninformative explanations practically useful, while also helping to detect cases where they are not. Importantly, the interpretation instructions in explanation cards shift responsibility from users to providers: Rather than expecting users to recognize what can and cannot be concluded from an explanation, providers must make this explicit upfront. Using counterfactual explanations and SHAP as examples, we demonstrate how providers can construct explanation cards and that these cards provide users with the guidance needed for sound interpretation. We further argue that explanation cards offer a practical means of operationalising the explainability provisions of the EU AI Act. Overall, explanation cards are a significant step toward making explanation algorithms fit for real-world use cases.

</details>


### [751] [Decision-Weighted Flow Matching for Contextual Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16790)
*Jize Xie,Haomiao Wu,Qiang Chen,Xiu Su,Yi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Decision-Weighted Flow Matching (DW-FM), a training framework for conditional generative models to reduce decision regret in stochastic optimization scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training objectives focus on uniform distributional fit but fail to minimize downstream decision errors in stochastic optimization tasks. This misalignment motivates the need for a regret-sensitive training approach.

Method: DW-FM reweights the velocity-regression objective in generative models using decision-sensitive endpoint information. It bridges decision regret with velocity mismatch through theoretical loss-induced metrics, offering practical regret-aligned training objectives.

Result: DW-FM demonstrated reduced downstream regret in three CVaR-based benchmarks: synthetic portfolio management, semi-real financial tasks, and traffic-CVaR problems, outperforming standard training baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed DW-FM method aligns generative model training with decision-sensitive objectives, optimizing performance for downstream stochastic optimization while maintaining simplicity.

Abstract: Conditional generative models are increasingly used as scenario generators for stochastic optimization, but standard training objectives emphasize uniform distributional fit rather than the downstream decisions induced by generated scenarios. This creates an objective mismatch: errors in statistically common regions may have little effect on decision regret, whereas errors in decision-sensitive regions can substantially change the optimal action. We propose Decision-Weighted Flow Matching (DW-FM), a regret-aligned training framework that preserves the simplicity of standard flow matching while reweighting its velocity-regression objective using decision-sensitive endpoint information. Theoretically, we connect downstream regret to pathwise velocity mismatch through a loss-induced decision discrepancy and an adjoint transport argument, yielding an ideal regret-aligned surrogate and practical endpoint-weighted objectives with regret guarantees. Empirically, we demonstrate the effectiveness of DW-FM on three CVaR-based contextual stochastic optimization benchmarks spanning synthetic portfolio, semi-real financial, and traffic-CVaR tasks, where DW-FM improves downstream regret over standard baselines.

</details>


### [752] [Deep Q-Learning on Hölder Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16846)
*Qian Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the mathematical foundation of Q-learning in continuous-time stochastic control, focusing on the regularity and complexity of Bellman updates under specific conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve Q-learning in continuous action and state spaces by analyzing the regularity and computational complexity of Bellman optimality targets.

Method: The analysis isolates Bellman updates in a diffusion framework and studies their regularity, proposing a DeepONet architecture suited to the problem's mixed regularity. Explicit approximation bounds and complexity metrics are derived.

Result: Bellman updates yield anisotropic smoothing, compact iterate families, and explicit stiffness-complexity trade-offs as time-step approaches zero.

Conclusion: The study advances Q-learning theory by providing insights into Bellman target regularity and approximation but does not deliver convergence results for practical implementations.

Abstract: We study the operator-theoretic core of Q-learning in continuous-time stochastic control with continuous states and actions. In value-based reinforcement learning, each Q-learning or DQN update is built from a Bellman optimality target; our analysis isolates this target in a diffusion setting and studies its regularity and approximation complexity. Under uniform ellipticity and Hölder-regular coefficients, we show that a Bellman update maps bounded inputs into an anisotropic regularity class, smoothing the state variable while leaving only Lipschitz dependence on the action variable. This yields a compact family of Bellman iterates and motivates a tensor-product DeepONet architecture adapted to the mixed regularity of the problem. We then derive explicit approximation and resource bounds, together with a stiffness--complexity trade-off as the time step $δ\to 0$. The resulting theory makes a direct contribution to Q-learning theory at the level of Bellman target regularity and approximation in continuous stochastic control. At the same time, we do not claim a full convergence theorem for practical sampled Q-learning with exploration, replay, and stochastic gradient updates.

</details>


### [753] [HawkesNest: A Multi-Axis Synthetic Benchmark for Spatiotemporal Pattern Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16863)
*Yahya Aalaila,Sumantrak Mukherjee,Gerrit Großmann,Sebastian Vollmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces HawkesNest, a benchmark tool for testing spatiotemporal point process (STPP) models by simulating controlled complexity variations using a Hawkes-process backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: STPP models are difficult to evaluate due to their reliance on complex real-world data where the underlying generative structures are unknown, making it hard to diagnose model failures.

Method: HawkesNest tests STPP models by varying four defined complexity axes (space-time entanglement, background heterogeneity, cross-type interaction, and domain topology) while keeping global rates, stability, and simulation budgets consistent, and uses deterministic indices to quantify complexity.

Result: The indices used in HawkesNest were shown to be monotone and nearly orthogonal. Using the benchmark, Hawkes-family and neural-based baseline models showed performance degradation as complexity increased in specific axes.

Conclusion: HawkesNest provides a diagnostic framework to analyze the robustness of STPP models under varying structural complexities, revealing specific weaknesses in models like Hawkes-family baselines and AutoSTPP.

Abstract: Evaluation of spatiotemporal point process (STPP) models relies heavily on opaque real-world datasets, where latent generative structure is unknown and model failures are difficult to attribute. We introduce HawkesNest, a generator-aligned benchmark for controlled spatiotemporal pattern complexity built on a multivariate Hawkes backbone. HawkesNest defines four complexity axes: space--time entanglement, background heterogeneity, cross-type interaction, and domain topology. Each axis is associated with a deterministic index computed from the latent data-generating mechanism. By varying these axes while holding global rate, stability, and simulation budget fixed, HawkesNest enables diagnostic stress tests of STPP models under known structural difficulty. We verify that the indices are monotone and nearly orthogonal under controlled sweeps. We illustrate its use by showing that Hawkes-family baselines degrade under joint heterogeneity--entanglement complexity, even though they are structurally aligned with the Hawkes data-generating backbone. We further show that HawkesNest exposes neural-model sensitivity: AutoSTPP remains vulnerable under isolated increases in space--time entanglement. Code. Available at https://github.com/YahyaAalaila/HawkesNest

</details>


### [754] [Integrated Marketing Attribution: A Bayesian Framework for Privacy-Safe Granular Measurement Anchored in MMM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16878)
*Meghana R. Bhat,Ankit Umare,Utsav Aggarwal,Richard Vecsler,Arunkumar Mani,Karthik Nair,Chandhu Nair*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Integrated Marketing Attribution (IMA) to provide granular and privacy-safe campaign-level marketing insights by combining Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) and Bayesian attribution models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current marketing measurement techniques, MMM and MTA, lack an integrated framework. MMM is privacy-safe but not granular enough, while MTA offers detailed insights but struggles with reliability due to privacy restrictions.

Method: The authors introduce IMA, a framework that integrates MMM and Bayesian attribution models, using MMM-informed priors to balance granularity and privacy.

Result: IMA delivers campaign-level effects from aggregated data, maintaining privacy safety and remaining consistent with MMM insights.

Conclusion: IMA addresses the limitations of both MMM and MTA, providing a unified, secure, and granular solution for marketing attribution.

Abstract: Retail marketing measurement increasingly requires granular campaign-level insights without relying on user-level tracking. However, the two dominant approaches, Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM) and Multi-Touch Attribution (MTA), often produce fragmented insights. MMM is privacy-safe and robust for channel-level planning but is too coarse for campaign optimization, while MTA provides granular attribution but has become less reliable under increasing privacy restrictions. We propose Integrated Marketing Attribution (IMA), a unified framework that combines MMM with channel specific Bayesian attribution models to derive campaign-level effects from aggregated data. By leveraging MMM-informed priors, IMA delivers granular, privacy-safe attribution while preserving consistency with MMM.

</details>


### [755] [Upper Bounds on the Generalization Error of Deep Learning Models via Local Robustness and Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16883)
*Abdul-Rauf Nuhu,Parham M. Kebria,Vahid Hemmati,Mahmoud N. Mahmoud,Edward Tunstel,Abdollah Homaifar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the common issue of overly loose generalization bounds in robustness studies, proposing a refined approach to tighter and more meaningful error analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robustness-based generalization bounds are often data-dependent but fail in practical applications due to their vacuousness, largely stemming from overly broad treatment of robustness terms.

Method: The authors introduce a novel bound that scales the robustness term by considering stable and unstable samples in different sub-regions, incorporating data- and model-dependent factors.

Result: With experiments on ImageNet-trained models, the proposed bounds consistently provide tighter, non-vacuous estimates that align well with empirical performance in robust neural networks.

Conclusion: The refined approach improves the practical applicability of robustness generalization bounds, offering a closer alignment with real-world model error rates.

Abstract: Generalization is a critical property of data-driven models, particularly deep learning models deployed in safety-critical applications. Robustness-based generalization bounds have gained attention as a principled way to link robustness properties to generalization performance, often in a data-dependent manner. However, most existing bounds suffer from vacuousness in practical settings, yielding loose upper bounds that greatly exceed the actual error rates and limiting their usefulness for real-world evaluation. While this issue is often attributed to the uncertainty term, a substantial part of the problem originates from the robustness term itself, particularly for the 0-1 loss. Existing approaches typically treat the robustness term as a global measure, ignoring its variation across different sub-regions of the input space. In this work, we propose a generalization bound that addresses this limitation by scaling the robustness term according to the number of stable and unstable samples within each sub-region. Our bounds incorporate both data- and model-dependent factors while maintaining practical relevance (yielding tighter upper bounds on true error). Experiments on models trained on the ImageNet dataset show that our bounds remain consistently non-vacuous and achieve the tightest estimates among existing methods, closely aligning with empirical performance across a range of robust deep neural networks.

</details>


### [756] [Beyond Weights and Gradients: A Taxonomy of Federated Learning Messages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16891)
*Alvaro Javier Vargas Guerrero,Xinguang Wang,Quang Manh Doan,Guy Nagels*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides a formal definition and taxonomy for federated messages, addressing modern payloads beyond model weights and gradients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in defining diverse federated learning payloads and understand their trade-offs for utility and privacy.

Method: Proposes a mathematical framework and taxonomy to classify federated message types and evaluates them based on computational, communication, and privacy perspectives.

Result: Analyzed 202 recent publications, identifying a trend toward specialized messaging methods since 2021, beyond traditional deep learning updates.

Conclusion: The framework offers a structured approach to optimize federated systems for diverse hardware and security considerations.

Abstract: Federated Learning is rapidly evolving beyond the exchange of traditional model weights and gradients, yet existing definitions fail to capture the full scope of modern payloads like synthetic data and federated analytics. This paper addresses the gap by proposing a formal mathematical definition of a federated message that accounts for both utility and privacy. We introduce a taxonomy that organizes these exchanges into three categories: model structures, statistical summaries, and data-conditioned representations. By evaluating these groups based on computational demands, communication costs, and privacy risks, we provide a clearer understanding of the trade-offs involved in decentralized training. Our review of 202 recent publications highlights a significant shift since 2021 toward diverse messaging paradigms, signaling a move away from standard deep learning updates toward more specialized information sharing. This framework provides a structured path for future research to optimize federated systems for varying hardware and security requirements.

</details>


### [757] [Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them II: Hyperball Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16899)
*Kaiyue Wen,Xingyu Dang,Kaifeng Lyu,Tengyu Ma,Percy Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperball, an optimizer wrapper, improves efficiency and learning rate transfer for matrix-based optimizers like Muon by fixing Frobenius norms of weights and updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the diminishing gains of Muon over AdamW in larger models with standard weight decay, leveraging theoretical insights about equilibrium weight norms.

Method: Hyperball adjusts the Frobenius norms of weight matrices and optimizer updates to constant values, applied as a wrapper around base optimizers.

Result: Muon Hyperball achieves 20–30% token equivalent speedup on models up to 1.2B parameters and improves learning rate transfer across model dimensions.

Conclusion: Hyperball enhances the efficacy and generalizability of matrix-based optimizers, providing performance gains in larger-scale language model pretraining.

Abstract: Matrix based optimizers such as Muon can substantially speed up language model pretraining, but their gains over AdamW are observed to shrink as model size and data scale grow when using standard constant decoupled weight decay. We propose Hyperball, a simple optimizer wrapper that addresses this issue. Given a base optimizer such as Adam or Muon, Hyperball sets the Frobenius norms of weight matrices and their corresponding optimizer updates to fixed constants. On Qwen3 style models up to 1.2B parameters, Muon Hyperball achieves 20--30% token equivalent speedup over weight decay baselines. Hyperball also improves learning rate transfer across widths and depths compared to decoupled weight decay. This method is motivated by prior theory showing that training with weight decay leads to an equilibrium weight norm that only depends on the training hyperparameters. Through this mechanism, the weight decay then decides the angular learning rate, i.e. how fast the direction of the weight matrix changes.

</details>


### [758] [Factorized Neural Operators Decompose Dynamic and Persistent Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16900)
*Hao Tang,Yuechen Duan,Jiongyu Zhu,Zimeng Feng,Hao Li,Chao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Factorized Neural Operators (FaNO), a new framework designed to tackle multiscale physical systems by decomposing spectral representations into two distinct roles: handling dynamic and persistent physical responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural operators struggle to effectively model multiscale physical systems, as they rely on a single inductive bias and conflate distinct physical mechanisms into shared representations.

Method: The authors develop FaNO within a Unified Green's Function Framework. FaNO employs two specialized operator branches—one focusing on rapidly changing dynamics (equivariant branch) and the other on persistent structures (invariant branch).

Result: FaNO demonstrates improved interpretability, prediction accuracy, parameter efficiency, and cross-scale generalization in physical modeling tasks. It consistently performs well in long-horizon scenarios, cross-resolution settings, and under physical-regime shifts.

Conclusion: FaNO’s factorized approach offers a significant advancement over traditional single-inductive-bias methods by better reflecting the heterogeneous mechanisms of physical systems, potentially accelerating the use of machine learning in scientific discovery.

Abstract: Physical systems often exhibit heterogeneous mechanisms, where rapidly evolving dynamics coexist with persistent structures. Capturing such multiscale physical behavior remains challenging for existing neural operators, which typically rely on single dominant inductive bias and therefore couple distinct physical responses into a shared representation. We introduce the Unified Green's Function Framework across domains and propose the Factorized Neural Operators (FaNO), which decompose spectral representations into equivariant dynamic responses and invariant persistent responses, leading to better interpretability and generalization. Mechanistically, we show that the two operator branches spontaneously specialize into distinct physical roles that remain consistent across scales and domains: the equivariant branch captures rapidly varying transient dynamics, whereas the invariant branch extracts coherent persistent structures. This factorized mechanism of FaNO improves prediction accuracy, parameter efficiency and cross-scale generalization across physical systems and domains. In particular, it maintains consistent predictions under long-horizon autoregressive rollout, cross-resolution extrapolation and physical-regime shifts. These findings suggest that scalable physical modeling may benefit from moving beyond single-inductive-bias formulations toward factorized operator representations that better reflect the heterogeneous organization of physical systems, accelerating the reliable deployment of machine learning for scientific computing and discovery.

</details>


### [759] [Demystifying Variance in Circuit Discovery of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16920)
*Frank Zhengqing Wu,Francesco Tonin,Volkan Cevher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses variability issues in current circuit discovery methods for mechanistic interpretability, proposing a new method (CEAP) that improves upon existing techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the reliability of circuit discovery methods in mechanistic interpretability by addressing variability issues that affect their outcomes.

Method: The authors introduced a new circuit discovery method named CEAP, which enhances EAP-IG with a theoretical guarantee and analyzes the sources of different types of variance in circuit discovery.

Result: CEAP significantly reduces resampling variance. It also identifies reasons for rephrasing variance and concludes that finding comprehensive circuits for multiple templates in LLMs may be inherently challenging. Sparsity fails to resolve these problems, and sample-wise variance is found to be less problematic than initially assumed.

Conclusion: While CEAP improves on prior methods, variability in interpreting model circuits under different prompts or samples persists, posing challenges to fully steering LLMs for specific tasks. This indicates an inherent complexity in understanding and controlling LLM behaviors.

Abstract: Circuit discovery is a key technique in mechanistic interpretability to pinpoint the model components that are crucial for performing a given task. Although the current state-of-the-art method (EAP-IG) performs well on the metric of (un)faithfulness, it suffers from substantial variability. This includes resampling variance, where the circuit changes when we probe with a new batch of data from the same distribution; rephrasing variance, where the discovered circuit shifts when the prompts are rephrased; and sample-wise variance, where a circuit with low population unfaithfulness exhibits large fluctuations in unfaithfulness across individual samples.
  This paper studies the roots of these variances. We demonstrate that CEAP, our new circuit discovery method that improves upon EAP-IG with a theoretical guarantee, can substantially lessen resampling variance. We further show that rephrasing variance arises because prompts with different templates tend to activate different circuits in the model. This leads us to argue that it may be challenging to find a comprehensive circuit that explains and controls the model's behavior on a task, which can be expressed in countless templates, suggesting that LLMs may be inherently hard to steer. We show that sparsity, which has been claimed to form more compact and interpretable task circuits, fails to solve this problem. Regarding sample-wise variance, we argue that it is largely benign: extremely poor unfaithfulness scores often stem from how unfaithfulness is defined, rather than from defects in the measured circuits. We show that the magnitude of unfaithfulness is affected by selective contribution scaling, a neural mechanism that accounts for the extremely poor scores sometimes observed.

</details>


### [760] [A Unified Causal-Origin Taxonomy of Distributional Shifts in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16933)
*Ardianto Wibowo,Paulo E Santos,Amer Baghdadi,Matthew Stephenson,Karl Sammut,Jean-Philippe Diguet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a unified taxonomy to classify sources of distributional shift in reinforcement learning (RL), integrating In-Distribution (ID), Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization, and non-stationary settings using a causal framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL systems often deteriorate in unfamiliar conditions caused by distributional shifts, but existing research lacks a clear understanding of the causal origins and focuses more on mitigating these effects rather than addressing their roots.

Method: The authors propose a causal-origin taxonomy by adapting the dataset-shift principle from supervised learning to RL using a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). This model decomposes the interaction process into components (state distribution, observation, policy, reward, transition dynamics) and introduces the concept of a shifted-time boundary.

Result: The taxonomy categorizes shifts as internal (agent-driven) or external (environment-driven), and further distinguishes them into explicit, implicit, and hybrid shifts. It links ID/OOD generalization and non-stationarity as structured alterations in the data-generation process, proposing metrics for evaluating performance degradation and recovery.

Conclusion: This framework provides a systematic way to analyze and evaluate RL performance under different types of distributional shifts, advancing the understanding of robustness and adaptability in RL systems.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) systems often degrade when operating conditions differ from those previously encountered, reflecting distributional shifts in the underlying data-generating process. Such shifts may occur between training and evaluation, as in In-Distribution (ID) and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization, or within non-stationary settings where environment dynamics evolve over time. However, the formal relationship between these views remains unclear, and existing work mainly focuses on mitigation rather than the causal origin of shift within the agent-environment interaction. This work develops a unified causal-origin taxonomy that characterizes sources of distributional shift in RL and relates ID/OOD generalization to non-stationary settings. We transfer the classical dataset-shift principle from supervised learning to RL by reformulating distributional shift in terms of the generative interaction process. Using a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), we decompose the interaction into structural components, including the state distribution, observation process, policy, reward, and transition dynamics, together with the shifted-time boundary. The proposed taxonomy distinguishes internal, agent-driven, and external, environment-driven, distributional shifts. The shifted-time boundary perspective further characterizes explicit, implicit, and hybrid shifts. This formulation unifies ID/OOD generalization and non-stationarity as structured changes in the underlying process. We also introduce an evaluation framework for measuring shift impact and adaptation through performance degradation and recovery metrics. By grounding distributional shift in the causal-origin structure of RL, this work supports systematic analysis of robustness under distributional shift.

</details>


### [761] [Scalable Circuit Learning for Interpreting Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16939)
*Naiyu Yin,Dennis Wei,Tian Gao,Amit Dhurandhar,Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy,Yue Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces CircuitLasso, a scalable approach for learning interpretable circuits in LLMs, achieving comparable accuracy to prior methods at reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge is to interpret how LLM components produce behavior via sparse circuits, while overcoming issues like polysemantic neurons and computational inefficiencies in existing methods.

Method: CircuitLasso uses sparse linear regression to recover circuits and connect relationships among SAE features efficiently for improved interpretability.

Result: CircuitLasso matches the structural accuracy of state-of-the-art methods for circuit learning but at a significantly lower computational cost.

Conclusion: CircuitLasso offers an efficient, interpretable, and computationally light solution for understanding semantic feature propagation in LLMs, with practical utility in tasks like domain generalization.

Abstract: A prominent research direction in mechanistic interpretability is learning sparse circuits over LLM components to reveal how they jointly produce model behavior. However, raw neurons are polysemantic, making learned circuits hard to interpret. Sparse autoencoder (SAE) features alleviate this, but their high dimensionality makes existing intervention-based circuit learning methods computationally prohibitive. We propose CircuitLasso, a scalable circuit-learning approach based on sparse linear regression. CircuitLasso recovers circuits whose structural accuracy matches that of state-of-the-art intervention-based methods on the benchmark data, at a fraction of the computational cost. For interpretability, CircuitLasso efficiently uncovers relationships among SAE features, showing how human-interpretable semantic features propagate through the model and influence its predictions. Finally, we validate the utility of our learned circuits by leveraging their insights to achieve comparable performance at substantially lower cost on a domain-generalization task.

</details>


### [762] [Beyond the Smile: A Hybrid Convolutional VAE for Crypto Volatility Surfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16961)
*Sadanand Singh,Allam Reddy,Manan Chopra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a convolutional variational autoencoder and a hybrid predictor for cryptocurrency implied-volatility surfaces (BTC and ETH), demonstrating robust performance in completing missing surface data with low error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of precise surface completion for cryptocurrency implied-volatility datasets, which often have missing or sparse data points.

Method: The authors use a convolutional variational autoencoder trained on Binance Options data of BTC and ETH, combined with a deterministic quadratic smile re-fit for improved predictions.

Result: The hybrid model achieves an eightfold error reduction in masked data and identifies an optimal shared manifold for BTC and ETH volatility surfaces, outperforming other methods across a variety of conditions.

Conclusion: The method significantly improves surface reconstruction, identifies volatile market periods, ensures arbitrage-free predictions, and supports further research through released infrastructure.

Abstract: We present a convolutional variational autoencoder for cryptocurrency implied-volatility surfaces, together with a deployable predictor that combines it with a quadratic smile re-fit through a deterministic per-tenor routing rule. Trained on 6,034 fully-filled hourly Binance Options surfaces of BTC and ETH spanning May-October 2023 and parameterised on a common $6 \times 7$ tenor-delta grid, the model attains a hidden-cell surface-completion RMSE in the 0.94-1.56 vol-point range across both markets and mask rates 10-50%. The hybrid predictor attains 0.83 vol points at 50% masking against 7.00 for the smile re-fit alone, an eightfold reduction obtained at no additional inference cost. Under structurally-correlated hole patterns that emulate the withdrawal of an entire tenor of strikes, the smile re-fit incurs 9.6-13.1 vol points of error while the learned model remains at 1.5-1.9, isolating a regime in which the generative model is the only viable predictor. Joint training on BTC and ETH improves the in-distribution model on both markets by 9-27% relative to the better-performing single-symbol counterpart, indicating a substantially shared vol-surface manifold across the two largest cryptocurrencies over the observation window. The hybrid is calendar- and butterfly-arbitrage-free at the listed strikes, a property that the parametric smile re-fit alone fails at high mask rates. The per-snapshot reconstruction error of the trained model flags the late-October ETF-anticipation rally and the August $17$, $2023$ flash crash as elevated-error periods without supervision. All training and evaluation infrastructure is released to support reproducible follow-on work.

</details>


### [763] [Scalable Pairwise Kernel Learning with Stochastic Vec Trick](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16979)
*Napsu Karmitsa,Tapio Pahikkala,Antti Airola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPaiK introduces a scalable kernel learning method for pairwise learning, utilizing the stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT) for efficiency, and demonstrates its effectiveness on drug-target affinity datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of computational and memory demands in kernel-based pairwise learning methods, making large-scale datasets accessible for analysis.

Method: SPaiK employs the stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT)—a stochastic extension of the sparse Kronecker product multiplication algorithm—to maintain kernel expressiveness while significantly improving scalability for pairwise kernel learning.

Result: The proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art techniques on seven real-world drug-target affinity datasets, showcasing its scalability and efficiency.

Conclusion: SPaiK successfully combines the expressive power of kernel methods with enhanced scalability, enabling its application to previously intractable large-scale pairwise learning scenarios.

Abstract: Pairwise learning is a specialized form of supervised learning that focuses on predicting outcomes for pairs of objects. In this work, we introduce SPaiK, a new scalable kernel learning method tailored for pairwise settings. Our approach preserves the expressive power of kernel methods while substantially reducing computational and memory requirements. The key innovation is the stochastic generalized vec trick (sGVT), a stochastic extension of the sparse Kronecker product multiplication algorithm, which enables efficient large-scale training with pairwise kernels. By incorporating sGVT, SPaiK makes it possible to apply kernel-based pairwise learning to datasets of a size previously out of reach. We evaluate the performance of SPaiK on seven real-world drug-target affinity datasets and compare the results with state-of-the-art methods in pairwise learning.

</details>


### [764] [Analytic Torsion and Spectral Gap Capture Persistent-Laplacian Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16990)
*Jernej Grlj,Aaron D. Lauda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a compact spectral representation for persistent Laplacians (PL) that reduces dimensionality and preserves essential predictive signals while preventing noise from higher-frequency eigenvalues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Utilizing the full eigenspectrum of persistent Laplacians for learning tasks is challenging due to high dimensionality and varying length across filtration scales.

Method: They propose a compact representation by extracting mathematically grounded invariants—Betti numbers, spectral gap, and analytic torsion—from the persistent Laplacian's spectrum.

Result: The proposed invariants effectively capture predictive signals in benchmark datasets (MNIST, QM-3D, SKEMPI WT), outperforming the full spectrum in some cases while reducing computational complexity.

Conclusion: These spectral invariants provide a principled and efficient means to bridge spectral geometry with topological learning, offering a fixed-length representation for practical applications.

Abstract: While persistent Laplacians (PL) offer a richer geometric representation of data than persistent homology, utilizing their full eigenspectrum for learning tasks is often hampered by high dimensionality and the ``varying length'' problem across different filtration scales. We propose a compact spectral representation that distills the persistent Laplacian into three mathematically grounded invariants: Betti numbers, the spectral gap, and analytic torsion. Across benchmark datasets including MNIST, QM-3D, and SKEMPI WT, we demonstrate that this reduced feature space captures the essential predictive signal of the full spectrum, and in some cases outperforms it, while significantly reducing computational overhead and preventing the noise introduced by higher-frequency eigenvalues. Our results suggest that these invariants provide a principled, fixed-length interface between spectral geometry and topological learning.

</details>


### [765] [From Tokens to Policy: Causal and Interpretable Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17010)
*Riccardo Cadei,Frank Otchere,Nyasha Tirivayi,Gustavo Angeles Tagliaferro,Falco J. Bargagli-Stoffi,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper focuses on Heterogeneous Treatment Effect (HTE) identification in controlled experiments, proposing a method, NEXIS, to address unmeasured heterogeneity drivers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve HTE identification and address the limitations of existing approaches, which fail to account for unmeasured variables and make causal interpretations unreliable.

Method: The authors introduce NEXIS, an iterative procedure that identifies HTE by framing it as a Markov-blanket discovery problem on sufficient and aligned pre-treatment representations.

Result: When applied to anti-poverty programs in Africa, NEXIS successfully utilized additional data like satellite imagery to discover new, interpretable effect modifiers and provided prescriptive guidelines.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that NEXIS is a viable tool for causal HTE identification, yielding actionable insights and improving the design of intervention programs.

Abstract: Heterogeneous Treatment Effect (HTE) identification is crucial to explain the impact of an intervention and optimize our policies accordingly. Existing approaches trade expressivity for interpretability, but, if some active heterogeneity drivers are unmeasured, methods at both ends of this spectrum allow for spurious HTE characterization with no causal reading. In this work, we focus on controlled experiments and argue that an oracle HTE causal characterization via the latent interactors is now within reach, thanks to (i) more extensive pre-treatment measurements, i.e., multi-modal and multi-view, and (ii) scalable representations with minimal human supervision. We then re-frame HTE identification as a Markov-blanket discovery problem on a sufficient and aligned pre-treatment representation, and introduce Neural EXposure Interaction Search (NEXIS), an iterative procedure with provable and empirically validated consistent selection. We deploy NEXIS on two anti-poverty programs in Africa, augmenting each with satellite imagery capturing previously unmeasured environmental effect modifiers, leading to novel, interpretable and prescriptive guidelines to optimize the programs' next iterations.

</details>


### [766] [ExpRL: Exploratory RL for LLM Mid-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17024)
*Violet Xiang,Amrith Setlur,Chase Blagden,Nick Haber,Aviral Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes ExpRL, a reinforcement learning-based mid-training approach for improving reasoning skills in large language models (LLMs) using human-written question-answer data. ExpRL applies reference solutions as 'reward scaffolds' to judge reasoning traces rather than to imitate them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address shortcomings in existing sparse-reward reinforcement learning approaches and manual mid-training methods for LLMs, especially for harder problems. Manual mid-training, though effective, requires predefined skills and may fail to generalize to complex tasks requiring combination and adaptation of learned skills.

Method: ExpRL employs RL-based mid-training, where references are hidden from the policy and used to construct rubrics that provide dense rewards. These rewards guide the policy to improve reasoning traces. The approach emphasizes process-level or outcome-level reinforcement by comparing sampled solutions to reference answers with an LLM judge.

Result: ExpRL outperformed supervised fine-tuning (SFT), sparse-reward RL methods like GRPO, and self-distillation in challenging math reasoning tasks. Additionally, it delivered better initialization for subsequent sparse-reward RL and proved adaptable across mixed domains.

Conclusion: ExpRL demonstrates a more automated and effective alternative to manual mid-training, strengthening both primitive skill learning and broader reasoning strategy formation. The method is versatile and scalable, extending its applicability beyond specific domains like math.

Abstract: Sparse reward reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard tool for improving LLM reasoning, but its success depends critically on the coverage present in the base model. In practice, models are often primed for RL through \emph{mid-training} on curated reasoning traces that teach useful primitive skills such as decomposition, verification, or self-correction. Although effective, this strategy requires manually specifying what the model should learn, and it remains unclear whether such primitive coverage is enough for much harder problems, which require combining these skills into broader solution strategies. We study a more automated approach: \emph{RL-based mid-training} using large corpora of human-written question-answer data. Rather than treating reference solutions as targets to imitate, our method, ExpRL, uses them as \emph{reward scaffolds}: references are hidden from the policy and used only to construct problem-specific grading rubrics for judging on-policy reasoning traces. The policy samples from the original problem prompt, while an LLM judge compares the sampled reasoning trace against the reference solution and assigns outcome-level or process-level dense rewards. This lets ExpRL reinforce partial progress, useful intermediate reductions, and productive reasoning behaviors that sparse final-answer rewards often fail to upweight. On challenging math reasoning tasks, ExpRL yields stronger RL priming than SFT, sparse-reward GRPO, and self-distillation, and provides a better initialization for subsequent sparse-reward RL. Additional mixed-domain experiments further suggest that ExpRL can extend beyond the original math-only setting.

</details>


### [767] [Your Privacy My Cloak: Backdoor Attacks on Differentially Private Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17035)
*Xiaolin Li,Ning Wang,Ninghui Li,Wenhai Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper challenges the assumption that differential privacy inherently enhances the robustness of federated learning against backdoor attacks by showing that DP can conceal malicious activity, rendering existing defenses ineffective. A new attack method called RING exploits this vulnerability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to re-evaluate the common belief that differential privacy (DP) improves robustness against backdoor attacks in federated learning (FL) and demonstrate potential vulnerabilities in this assumption that could expose FL to serious security threats.

Method: The authors conduct empirical analysis of two baseline attack strategies to reveal weaknesses stemming from DP masking malicious updates' statistical characteristics. They then introduce RING, an attack method that exploits DP to collaboratively craft adversarial perturbations for a strong backdoor signal during aggregation. RING is composable with various backdoor techniques and effective against multiple defense mechanisms.

Result: RING achieved an average attack success rate of 90.3% across four datasets and six state-of-the-art defenses under non-iid distributions, significantly outperforming baseline strategies by up to 26.08x within a moderate privacy budget.

Conclusion: The study reveals a fundamental tension between differential privacy and backdoor defense mechanisms in federated learning, showing that DP inadvertently facilitates backdoor attacks while limiting defensive measures. The authors highlight significant security risks and suggest that mitigating this threat comes at a considerable utility cost, exposing a gap in DP-FL systems.

Abstract: Prior research suggests that differential privacy (DP) inherently enhances the robustness of federated learning (FL) against backdoor attacks. In this paper, we challenge this assumption. Through an empirical analysis of two baseline attack strategies, we uncover a fundamental tension in DP-FL: while bypassing DP allows state-of-the-art defenses to detect and filter malicious updates, complying with DP inadvertently masks their distinguishing statistical characteristics. Consequently, existing defenses become ineffective as DP reduces the raw backdoor signal. Building on this masking effect, we propose RING, a novel attack that explicitly exploits DP to conceal malicious contributions while maximizing attack impact. By collaboratively crafting adversarial perturbations, compromised clients reconstruct a strong backdoor signal during aggregation without triggering anomaly detection. RING operates as a perturbation layer that is agnostic to the underlying backdoor technique, making it broadly applicable and composable with existing attacks -- a property that significantly amplifies the threat it poses to DP-FL. Extensive evaluations across four image and text datasets under non-iid distributions show that RING achieves an average attack success rate of 90.3% against six state-of-the-art defenses under a moderate privacy budget, an improvement of up to 26.08x over baseline strategies. Finally, we evaluate potential countermeasures and find that mitigating this threat incurs significant utility trade-offs, exposing a fundamental security gap in the deployment of differentially private FL.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [768] [Test-Time Adaptation of Spiking Neural Networks for Intracortical Neural Decoding using Membrane Potential Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14866)
*Guangzhi Tang*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Membrane Potential Alignment (MPA), a lightweight test-time adaptation method for intracortical brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) to handle neural signal shifts without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing decoders for brain-computer interfaces degrade due to daily neural signal shifts. Current adaptation methods are computationally intensive and unsuitable for implantable hardware. This work aims to create a lightweight, efficient solution.

Method: The proposed MPA method adapts pretrained spiking neural networks by aligning membrane potential distributions via KL divergence and limiting updates to low-rank (LoRA) weights, thus keeping computational overhead low.

Result: In a month-long primate reaching task, MPA showed competitive performance to NoMAD, a state-of-the-art adaptation method, while using a simpler architecture and offering finer temporal resolution.

Conclusion: MPA presents an efficient and practical approach to long-term brain-computer interfaces by enabling recalibration-free adaptation while minimizing computational demands.

Abstract: Intracortical brain-computer interfaces suffer from day-to-day neural signal shifts that degrade pretrained decoders. Existing unsupervised adaptation methods rely on deep recurrent or adversarial architectures that are too computationally expensive for implantable hardware. We propose Membrane Potential Alignment (MPA), a test-time adaptation method for spiking neural networks that realigns a pretrained decoder to shifted recordings by only matching membrane potential distributions via KL divergence. By restricting updates to low-rank (LoRA) weights, MPA adapts fewer than 9% of parameters. On a non-human primate reaching task spanning over one month, MPA achieves performance competitive with the state-of-the-art NoMAD method, while using a simpler architecture and finer temporal resolution (4 ms vs. 20 ms). These results show that efficient SNN-based test-time adaptation is a practical path toward long-term, recalibration-free brain-computer interfaces.

</details>


### [769] [Harnessing cortical geometry, wiring, and function as inductive biases for recurrent neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14975)
*Mo Shakiba,Rana Rokni,Mohammad Mohammadi,Nima Dehghani*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper leverages a dataset from the MICrONS program to design biologically inspired recurrent neural networks using mouse visual cortex data, achieving superior task performance and highlighting the organizational benefits of cortical principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how the structural and functional organization of the cortex can inform the design of recurrent neural networks in neuroscience and machine learning.

Method: Recurrent neural networks were initialized with spatial coordinates, anatomical connectivity, and functional relationships derived from 12,000 coregistered neurons from the mouse visual cortex and constrained with communication-aware spatial considerations during learning across tasks.

Result: The biologically constrained networks consistently outperformed baseline models, demonstrated modular, small-world organization, and maintained strong performance even under restrictive weight conditions.

Conclusion: Cortical geometry, wiring, and functional structure provide valuable inductive biases for designing recurrent neural networks that align with biological organizational principles while enhancing task learning effectiveness.

Abstract: How the wiring and functional organization of cortex shape recurrent computation remains a central question in both neuroscience and machine learning. Here, we leverage data released through the Machine Intelligence from Cortical Networks (MICrONS) program--a functional connectomics resource spanning multiple areas of mouse visual cortex, in which dense calcium imaging is co-registered with high-resolution electron microscopy reconstruction from the same animal--to build biologically grounded recurrent neural networks. Using neuronal spatial coordinates, anatomical connectivity, and function-derived relationships from nearly 12,000 coregistered excitatory neurons, we initialize recurrent weights and impose communication-aware spatial constraints during learning. Across three cognitive decision-making tasks, networks constrained by cortical structure and function consistently outperform baseline and partially constrained models. Functional weight initialization provides the largest gain, while real spatial embedding yields robust additional improvements across conditions. These biologically grounded networks also develop low-entropy, modular, and small-world organization, and retain strong performance even when recurrence is restricted to positive weights. Together, our results show that the machinery of cortex--its geometry, wiring, and functional structure--can be harnessed as a powerful inductive basis for building recurrent networks that learn more effectively while converging toward key organizational principles of biological computation.

</details>


### [770] [Large Language Model-Driven Cooperative Operator Ensemble Evolution for Permutation Flow Shop Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15334)
*Rui Xu,Yufan Liao,Haoze Lv,Shengcai Liu,Yi Mei,Ke Tang*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-operator iterated greedy algorithm (IG-DOE) with a novel destruction operator ensemble to improve exploration in solving the NP-hard permutation flow shop scheduling problem (PFSP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical iterated greedy (IG) algorithms struggle with exploration and stagnation due to reliance on a single, fixed destruction operator.

Method: A multi-operator IG algorithm uses sequential switching of heterogeneous destruction operators and employs a large language model (LLM)-assisted framework (SCOE) for adaptive and effective construction of the operator ensemble.

Result: The IG-DOE algorithm significantly outperformed state-of-the-art algorithms on the VRF-hard-large benchmark and generalized well to real-world industrial instances and varying data distributions.

Conclusion: Combining LLM-driven design of destruction operators and multi-operator strategies enhances IG algorithm performance, providing a robust solution to PFSP in diverse and complex scenarios.

Abstract: The permutation flow shop scheduling problem (PFSP) is a classical NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem in intelligent manufacturing. In practice, PFSP is commonly addressed using metaheuristic algorithms, among which the iterated greedy (IG) algorithm is widely adopted due to its simplicity and strong empirical performance. However, classical IG relies on a single fixed destruction operator, which often limits exploration and leads to search stagnation on large and complex problem instances. To address this issue, this work proposes a multi-operator IG algorithm, termed IG-DOE, which enhances exploration by switching among heterogeneous destruction operators along a single search trajectory. The core mechanism, called stagnation-triggered sequential switching, activates the next destruction operator in an ordered destruction operator ensemble (DOE) when stagnation is detected, thereby enriching the perturbation behavior of classical IG. Moreover, to reduce reliance on expert-crafted operators, a large language model (LLM)-assisted framework, termed SCOE, is introduced to automatically construct a high-quality DOE through stagewise evolution, state-awareness, and cooperative evaluation. Experiments on the challenging \textit{VRF-hard-large} benchmark show that the DOE evolved from smaller problem instances generalizes well to larger unseen instances. Under the same CPU-time limit, IG-DOE obtained much better average performance than QIG, a state-of-the-art IG algorithm. Additional experiments on real-world industrial-data-derived instances further show that the evolved DOE can generalize effectively to different data distributions without additional adaptation.

</details>


### [771] [AQ4SViT: An Automated Quantization Framework with Search Gating Policy for Compressing Spiking Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15523)
*Rachmad Vidya Wicaksana Putra,Saad Iftikhar,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper presents AQ4SViT, an automated quantization framework for Spiking Vision Transformers (SViTs) aimed at efficiently deploying them on resource-constrained embedded AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenges of large SViTs in low-power scenarios and inefficiency in manual quantization approaches, which are time-consuming and power-intensive, particularly for multiple networks.

Method: AQ4SViT introduces an automated quantization process using a quantization search strategy and a search gating policy, providing Greedy and Beam search algorithms for different trade-offs in speed and performance.

Result: AQ4SViT-Greedy achieves up to 6.6x faster search time and 82.5% memory savings compared to current methods, while AQ4SViT-Beam saves up to 90% memory with longer search time but maintains high model accuracy (within 1.5% of non-quantized versions).

Conclusion: The AQ4SViT framework demonstrates significant potential to enable efficient SViT usage in embedded AI applications, offering promising trade-offs between accuracy, memory efficiency, and search speed.

Abstract: Spiking Vision Transformers (SViTs) have emerged as alternative low-power ViT models, but their large sizes hinder their deployments on resource-constrained embedded AI systems. To address this, state-of-the-art works proposed quantization techniques to compress SViT models, but their manual, human-guided approach needs a huge design time and power/energy consumption to find the appropriate quantization setting for each given network, making this approach not scalable for quantizing multiple networks. Toward this, we propose AQ4SViT, a novel automated quantization framework for SViTs that can provide quick quantization settings with good trade-offs between accuracy and memory. To achieve this, AQ4SViT employs the following key ideas: quantization search strategy that evaluates the quantization setting candidates while considering the accuracy constraint; and search gating policy that quickly evaluates and selects promising quantization candidates by leveraging membrane potential drift as a performance proxy. In the search gating policy, AQSViT employs two search algorithm variants to provide trade-off options: Greedy search, which performs fast but may lead to local optima; and Beam search, which performs slower but has better performance in finding global optima selection due to a wider search space. Experimental results show that AQ4SViT-Greedy quickly finds the appropriate quantization settings, achieving up to 6.6x faster search time and up to 82.5% memory saving compared to the state-of-the-art; while AQ4SViT-Beam further reduces the memory footprint by up to 90% compared to the state-of-the-art, but with 4.5x longer search time; all these results are obtained while maintaining high accuracy within 1.5% from the original/non-quantized models on the ImageNet dataset. These results highlight that AQ4SViT framework offers advancements toward SViT deployments on embedded AI systems.

</details>


### [772] [MSC-CMA-ES: Structure-Aware Restarts for CMA-ES via Cyclic Nearest-Better Basin Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15830)
*Dimitar Nedanovski,Svetoslav Nenov,Dimitar Pilev*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a multi-modal optimization approach, MSC-CMA-ES, which structures restarts using cluster-aware strategies and demonstrates significant improvement over existing approaches, especially in composition problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current restart strategies in CMA-ES for multimodal optimization do not utilize information from prior evaluations, leading to inefficiencies in landscapes with multiple basins of attraction. This paper aims to make better use of this information.

Method: MSC-CMA-ES alternates structured strategies using clustering (e.g., nearest-better clustering) to detect and seed basin-specific restarts. It combines this with Sobol sampling and adjusts parameters like step size dynamically. This is followed by an unbounded refinement phase for the best-so-far solutions.

Result: MSC-CMA-ES achieves improved performance on composition functions with 2.7x target coverage over BIPOP-CMA-ES. However, it has limitations in hybrid functions compared to leading DE algorithms and trades deep-target success for broader landscape discovery in basic functions.

Conclusion: MSC-CMA-ES provides a competitive and innovation-driven approach in multimodal optimization, particularly succeeding in composition functions where landscape discovery is critical. However, it faces limitations inherent to its CMA family origin.

Abstract: CMA-ES is, per run, a local optimizer; multimodal search relies on restart strategies such as IPOP and BIPOP, which draw every restart uniformly and reuse no information from previous evaluations. Multi-Start Clustering CMA-ES (MSC-CMA-ES) makes restarts structure-aware: in alternating cycles, a Sobol pre-sample is partitioned into approximate basins of attraction by nearest-better clustering, restarts are seeded basin by basin with locally scaled step sizes and population sizes, redundant basin visits are detected and excluded, and the remaining budget is spent on an unbounded local refinement of the best-so-far solution. We evaluate the method on four CEC suites (CEC2014, CEC2017, CEC2020, CEC2022) at their official budgets, across ten (suite, dimension) cells with dimensions 5-30, 51 runs per function, against BIPOP-CMA-ES and five differential-evolution algorithms (ARRDE, jSO, j2020, NL-SHADE-RSP, LSRTDE). Read per function class, MSC-CMA-ES leads on one class, is mixed on a second, and trails on the third. On composition functions, MSC-CMA-ES attains the best value on all four aggregate measures, with 2.7x the fixed-budget target coverage of BIPOP-CMA-ES - the highest composition coverage of any algorithm evaluated. On basic functions, it achieves the best (lowest) median error but exhibits a lower deep-target coverage - the measured price of spending budget on landscape discovery. On hybrid functions both CMA variants trail the leading DE algorithms; the deficit belongs to the CMA family, not to the restart mechanism. All results and scripts are publicly available.

</details>


### [773] [Runtime Analysis of Cartesian Genetic Programming in Evolving Boolean Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15923)
*Duc-Cuong Dang,Roman Kalkreuth,Andre Opris*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper analyzes Cartesian Genetic Programming (CGP) for evolving Boolean functions, showing theoretical time bounds and the impact of strict versus non-strict selection on performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for this study is to provide the first theoretical understanding of CGP's runtime in evolving Boolean functions, addressing a gap in the analysis which has been previously explored only empirically.

Method: The authors conducted a theoretical runtime analysis of CGP and derived time complexity bounds for evolving Boolean functions, complemented by experiments evolving conjunctions under different conditions.

Result: The paper shows CGP has an expected runtime of $O(n D^5)$ with strict selection and $O(n D^4)$ with non-strict selection for conjunctions, but requires exponential time for exclusive disjunctions. Experiments also suggest incomplete training sets improve efficiency while maintaining generalization.

Conclusion: CGP's efficiency depends on the selection mechanism and training set completeness. Non-strict selection and incomplete training sets significantly enhance performance for evolving conjunctions, but some functions like exclusive disjunctions remain computationally challenging.

Abstract: Cartesian Genetic Programming (CGP) is among the practical and popular forms of Genetic Programming as it uses a graph-based representation of programs. This paper presents a first runtime analysis of CGP in evolving Boolean functions using complete training sets. We prove an asymptotic bound $O(n D^5)$ for the expected number of fitness evaluations of CGP to construct a conjunction of $n$ inputs using at most $D \geq n-1$ binary gates, a minimal function set, and even with a strict survival selection. When the non-strict selection is used, the bound is improved to $O(n D^4)$. Our analysis reveals interesting characteristics of CGP induced search, which have been only observed empirically. In particular, enabling the acceptance of equally good solutions, including those with connected gates non-contributing to fitness, can lead to a speedup, and consequently a better asymptotic time bound. In contrast to conjunctions, we also prove a negative result which shows that CGP requires exponential time to evolve an exclusive disjunction. Experiments evolving conjunctions complement our theoretical findings. The use of incomplete training sets is found to further reduce the average number of fitness evaluations while maintaining a good level of generalisation.

</details>


### [774] [Effects of Objective Normalization on Regions of Interest in Preference-Based Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16382)
*Ryuichi Mogami,Ryoji Tanabe*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper investigates the challenges of defining and approximating the region of interest (ROI) in preference-based evolutionary multi-objective optimization (PBEMO), focusing on the use of normalized vs. unnormalized objective spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of standardization in defining the ROI in PBEMO, especially in cases where objective functions have different scales, which creates challenges in real-world applications.

Method: The authors analyze ROIs defined in both normalized and unnormalized objective spaces, highlighting their differences and assessing their impact on approximation quality for scaled and equally scaled objective problems.

Result: ROIs defined in normalized objective space are shown to be difficult to approximate due to poor ideal and nadir point approximations, whereas ROIs in unnormalized space are easier to approximate.

Conclusion: Defining the ROI in unnormalized objective space is more practical and effective for PBEMO problems than using normalized objective space.

Abstract: Preference-based evolutionary multi-objective optimization (PBEMO) aims to approximate a region of interest (ROI) defined by the preference information from a decision maker (DM). Although objective functions in real-world applications typically have different scales, the issue of how to define the ROI in such problems has been overlooked in the literature. In fact, it has not been standardized in the EMO community whether the ROI should be defined in the unnormalized objective space or in the normalized objective space. In this context, this paper investigates the effects of objective normalization on ROIs. First, this paper shows that two ROIs defined in the unnormalized and normalized objective spaces can differ significantly for problems with differently scaled objectives. Then, we demonstrate that ROIs defined in the normalized objective space are highly difficult to approximate even on problems with equally scaled objectives because of poor approximations of the ideal and nadir points. In contrast, we show that ROIs defined in the unnormalized objective space are much easier to approximate than those defined in the normalized objective space.

</details>


### [775] [Evolution & Foundation: AI Shares Creative Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16849)
*Dylan Banarse,Stephen Todd,William Latham,Frederic Fol Leymarie*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper explores how a multimodal AI model, integrated with an evolutionary system, creates aesthetically appealing 3D organic forms and supports artists in efficiently navigating creative possibilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To facilitate the evolution of aesthetically pleasing 3D organic designs and shift the human artist's role from direct curation to strategic system design using AI's multimodal capabilities.
