<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance robot control models with foresight into action-driven scene changes while reducing latency and pixel-level redundancy found in prior approaches.

Method: LaWAM trains a latent-action-conditioned world model within the latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model and uses predicted latent visual subgoals for robot action generation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art success rates in LIBERO (98.6%), RoboTwin (91.22%), and real-world tasks, with low-latency inference (187 ms per prediction and up to 24x faster than pixel-space methods).

Conclusion: LaWAM provides an efficient, foresight-enhanced solution for dynamics-aware robot control, superior in both performance and inference speed compared to previous models.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) leverage large-scale vision-language pretraining for semantic robot control, but often lack explicit foresight into how robot actions change the scene. World-Action Models (WAMs) address this limitation by conditioning policies on predicted futures, yet existing approaches typically rely on computationally expensive video generation with substantial pixel-level redundancy. We present LaWAM, a Latent World Action Model that exposes predictive dynamics to robot policies through compact latent visual subgoals instead of reconstructed future video. At the core of LaWAM is a latent-action-conditioned Latent World Model (LaWM). We obtain LaWM by training a latent action model in the latent space of a pretrained vision foundation model and repurposing its forward decoder to predict future observation features for scene evolution. LaWAM then conditions action generation on these predicted latent visual subgoals to enable dynamics-aware robot control. LaWAM achieves state-of-the-art or competitive success rates (SRs) across LIBERO (98.6% SR), RoboTwin (91.22% SR), and real-world manipulation tasks while retaining low-latency inference. LaWAM runs in 187 ms per action-chunk prediction and achieves up to 24x lower wall-clock latency than pixel-space WAMs.

</details>


### [834] [FlashNav: Ultra-Fast Policy Training for Robot Navigation within 20 Seconds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15846)
*Shanze Wang,Yiwei Qian,Xinming Zhang,Jun Xue,Siwei Cheng,Xianghui Wang,Qingyuan Hu,Xiaoyu Shen,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlashNav is a GPU-first framework for ultra-fast deep reinforcement learning-based robot navigation training, achieving seconds-level policy training and successful physical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome the limitation of long training duration in deep reinforcement learning for robot navigation by proposing a faster, GPU-based training framework.

Method: FlashNav aligns simulation with navigation MDP by removing unnecessary rendering and physics details, employs a batched bitmap simulator, and uses a fully GPU-resident training pipeline with FastDSAC learner.

Result: FlashNav achieves seconds-level training, demonstrating a 100% success rate on TurtleBot2 and Unitree Go2 robots, and successfully transferring trained policies to physical robots for indoor scenes.

Conclusion: This framework significantly accelerates DRL-based navigation training while maintaining deployable and effective obstacle-avoidance policies for robots.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning has shown strong potential for robot navigation, but its practical deployment is still limited by the long wall-clock cost of policy training. This paper presents FlashNav, a GPU-first framework for ultra-fast range-based robot navigation training. To the best of our knowledge, FlashNav is the first DRL-based robot navigation framework that reaches seconds-level policy training, with the fastest deployable policy trained in less than 20 seconds. The key idea is to align simulation with the navigation MDP: FlashNav preserves the essential components for velocity-level navigation, including occupancy geometry, range sensing, goal-conditioned control, robot motion dynamics, collision handling, termination, and reset, while removing unnecessary rendering and high-fidelity physical details from the training loop. Built on a batched bitmap simulator and a fully GPU-resident training pipeline with our FastDSAC learner, FlashNav generates massive parallel navigation transitions entirely on GPU. Experiments on TurtleBot2 and Unitree Go2 show that FlashNav achieves a 100\% success-rate below 20 seconds on an RTX 5090 and remains within tens of seconds across desktop GPUs. The learned policies further transfer to physical wheeled and legged robots in static and dynamic indoor scenes, demonstrating that DRL-based navigation can be trained at seconds-level speed while preserving deployable obstacle-avoidance behavior.

</details>


### [835] [LoComposition: Terrain-Adaptive Energy-Efficient Quadruped Locomotion without Gait Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15896)
*Loukas Kordos,Leonard T. Franz,Simon Rappenecker,Oliver Hausdoerfer,Angela P. Schoellig,Pavel Kolev,Georg Martius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new approach to quadrupedal locomotion by disentangling task, constraints, and terrain adaptation, leading to efficient, emergent behavior without explicit gait priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the complexity and inefficiencies in traditional learning-based locomotion methods that use entangled reward formulations.

Method: The framework separates task specification (via rewards), operational limits (via constraints), gait preference (via energy minimization), and terrain adaptation (via exteroceptive perception).

Result: This approach reduces cost of transport by 56%, operational-limit violations by 96%, and enables zero-shot policy transfer to a physical Unitree Go2 robot.

Conclusion: Distinct mechanisms for locomotion functions allow for efficient, adaptive, and emergent behavior, outperforming traditional complex-reward baselines while simplifying the design process.

Abstract: Learning-based quadrupedal locomotion typically relies on complex reward formulations that entangle task specification, operational limits, gait preference, and terrain adaptation within a single optimization objective. We instead treat these functions through distinct mechanisms: rewards for task specification, constraints for operational limits, energy minimization for gait preference, and exteroceptive perception for adapting energy use to terrain difficulty. We show that these components jointly enable efficient, terrain-adaptive locomotion, and that removing each component exposes a distinct failure mode. Our formulation removes explicit gait priors (including air-time, contact-count, and foot-clearance targets) in favor of emergent behavior. Compared to a conventional complex-reward baseline, our formulation achieves comparable terrain traversal while reducing cost of transport by 56% and operational-limit violations by 96%. The resulting policies transfer zero-shot to a physical Unitree Go2 using LiDAR-based elevation mapping. Project website with videos: https://tinyurl.com/locomposition.

</details>


### [836] [VL2Spike: Spike-driven Distillation from VLMs for Low-Power Visual Perception in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15898)
*Zinan Liu,Eric Zheng,Soumyaratna Debnath,Hao Shi,Ling Xiao,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents VL2Spike, a knowledge distillation framework that integrates Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and Spiking-Transformer (Spikformer) models for energy-efficient and enriched learning in resource-constrained AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to enhance the learning capacity and energy efficiency of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), leveraging the multi-modal knowledge of Vision-Language Models to overcome limitations associated with spike-based architectures.

Method: The paper introduces two technical contributions: spatial-temporal visual spike (SVS) distillation for aligning VLM image features with spike tokens, and spike prototype-guided linguistic (SPL) distillation to align Spikformer prototypes with VLM textual embeddings.

Result: VL2Spike achieves a 6.81% performance gain on three static datasets while reducing energy consumption to 15.7%. It also improves generalization in robotic visual place recognition by 6.63%.

Conclusion: VL2Spike successfully bridges Vision-Language Models and Spikformer systems to offer improved performance and low-power consumption, presenting a promising approach for low-power robotic perception tasks.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are brain-inspired, event-driven models that compute with sparse spikes, which enables highly efficient visual perception in resource-constrained embodied AI models. The emergence of Spiking-Transformer models with spike self-attention has substantially improved the learning capacity of pure SNNs. Although SNNs are energy efficient, their performance is still limited by the spike-based architecture and optimization challenges, as standard gradient descent rules cannot be directly applied. Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have shown rich multi-modal knowledge representation capabilities for visual perception. Thus, it is promising to leverage VLMs for better Spikformer training. To this end, we present VL2Spike, a novel spike-based knowledge distillation (KD) framework that bridges multi-modal knowledge from VLMs with compact Spikformer models. This design enhances the learning capacity of Spikformer models while preserving their energy-efficiency merits, thereby offering a practical pathway toward low-power robotic perception. Our VL2Spike brings two key technical contributions. To align with spiking dynamics, we first propose spatial-temporal visual spike (SVS) distillation, which achieves (1) shared manifold alignment between VLM image features and spike tokens, and (2) warm-started temporal consistency on membrane potentials and spike rates. We then design a novel spike prototype-guided linguistic (SPL) distillation strategy that aligns Spikformer's class prototypes and logits with promptable VLM text embeddings. Extensive experiments show that VL2Spike achieves 6.81% gain across three static datasets with only 15.7% energy consumption. It also exhibits strong generalization capacity on robotic visual place recognition (VPR) with a gain of 6.63%, highlighting its potential for low-power perception in embodied AI.

</details>


### [837] [GeoTLM: Geometry-aware Tactile-Language Models for Contact Motion Orientation Reasoning of Dynamic Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15909)
*Qiutian Li,Zinan Liu,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GeoTLM, a tactile-language model guided by geometric representation, improves understanding of dynamic object interactions in tasks such as rotation and sliding direction reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tactile-language models (TLMs) struggle with reasoning about dynamic physical properties, such as object rotation and sliding, limiting their usefulness in contact-rich robot learning tasks.

Method: GeoTLM introduces a lightweight Differentiable Geometric Representation (DGR) to structure tactile shear-field geometry based on antisymmetric patterns, instead of relying on low-resolution tactile tokens or fragile physics operators.

Result: GeoTLM improves rotation direction reasoning accuracy by +14.6% and sliding direction accuracy by +16.2% compared to a baseline without the geometric encoder.

Conclusion: GeoTLM demonstrates the advantages of incorporating physically grounded geometric priors into tactile-language models, enabling more reliable reasoning about dynamic object properties and advancing robotic manipulation capabilities.

Abstract: Modern tactile-language models (TLMs) have shown potential for robot learning tasks, such as material and texture recognition. However, for contact-rich scenarios, these TLMs struggle to understand the physical properties of dynamic objects, such as rotation and sliding directions. For instance, our preliminary experiments reveal that popular TLMs, such as Sparsh and AnyTouch2, exhibit weak performance on basic rotation direction reasoning from GelSight Mini tactile data. This surprising gap inspires us to explore a novel research question: Can we inject physically grounded geometric priors into TLMs to enable reliable contact orientation reasoning of dynamic object properties? To this end, we propose GeoTLM, a novel geometric representation-guided TLM for the perception of dynamic contact events. Our key idea is to preserve and structure tactile shear-field geometry before language-level reasoning, rather than forcing low-resolution tactile tokens into fragile closed-form physics operators. To achieve this, we propose a lightweight (only 14k parameters) yet novel Differentiable Geometric Representation (DGR). Specifically, DGR learns a contact-mask-guided representation in the shear field and aggregates it through an antisymmetric seven-region pooling design, motivated by the physical intuition that rotational contact produces antisymmetric deformation patterns. We conduct experiments on two representative tasks: rotation direction and sliding direction reasoning. Extensive experiments show that GeoTLM improves novel-object rotation accuracy by +14.6% and real-sensor sliding accuracy by +16.2% over the same backbone without the geometric encoder. Overall, our work paves a new way for physically grounded tactile-language reasoning, with strong potential for dynamic object understanding and contact-rich robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [838] [Identification of a Physics-Based Electrical Power Consumption Model for the Unitree G1 Humanoid Arm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15915)
*Nestor N. Deniz,Sebastian Vega,Simon Parsons,Fernando Auat Cheein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a physics-based model for predicting electrical power consumption in a humanoid robot's left arm, achieving high accuracy in both the training and validation datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable effective energy-aware motion planning, battery management, and thermal monitoring for humanoid robots.

Method: A linear-in-parameters model was developed combining actuator loss terms, baseline-torque correction, and pairwise interaction terms to predict electrical power consumption. Model parameters were identified using experimental data from onboard measurements.

Result: The model achieved $R^2 = 0.933$ with RMSE of 1.07 W on training data and $R^2 = 0.965$ on unseen validation data, demonstrating generalization and accuracy.

Conclusion: The identified model successfully captures power consumption variations and distinct characteristics across individual joints, enabling accurate predictions for a humanoid robot's arm.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of electrical power consumption is essential for energy-aware motion planning, battery management, and thermal monitoring in battery-powered humanoid robots. This letter presents a physics-based, linear-in-parameters model for the electrical power consumption of the seven-degree-of-freedom left arm of the Unitree~G1 humanoid robot. The proposed formulation combines actuator loss terms with a baseline-torque correction that captures changes in gravity-compensation load and enables accurate prediction of negative net power trajectories. Pairwise interaction terms are introduced to model power coupling during simultaneous multi-joint motion. Model parameters are identified from experimental data collected on a physical Unitree~G1 using onboard power measurements as the regression target. Across 897 trajectories covering single-joint and coordinated arm motions at multiple speed levels, the identified model achieves $R^2 = 0.933$ with an RMSE of 1.07 (W). Validation on 46 trajectories executed at previously unseen speeds yields $R^2 = 0.965$, demonstrating strong generalisation beyond the identification dataset. Analysis of the identified parameters reveals distinct power-consumption characteristics across the arm, with viscous friction dominating most joints (shoulder pitch and all three wrist joints), copper losses dominating shoulder yaw and the elbow, and shoulder roll uniquely dominated by Coulomb friction.

</details>


### [839] [Energy-Efficient Arm Reaching for a Humanoid Robot via Deep Reinforcement Learning with Identified Power Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15918)
*Nestor N. Deniz,Simon Parsons,Fernando Auat Cheein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces an energy-aware reinforcement learning framework for optimizing robotic arm movements of the Unitree G1 humanoid robot while minimizing energy consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy constraints limit the performance of humanoid robots in tasks like apple harvesting, requiring methods to optimize energy usage during manipulation.

Method: A Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) RL policy is trained using a physics-based power model and a hybrid reward system to optimize joint positions, combining energy efficiency and task success.

Result: The framework achieves a 69.9% success rate in simulation, performing tasks with less energy consumption. Experimental validation shows successful reaching motions with low errors and energy-efficient performance.

Conclusion: This study demonstrates the potential of energy-aware RL for humanoid robots, laying groundwork toward optimizing real-world robotic reaching tasks.

Abstract: Humanoid robots performing in-field manipulation tasks, such as robotic apple harvesting, face severe energy constraints that directly limit the number of reaching motions that can be executed per battery charge. This paper presents an end-to-end, energy-aware reinforcement learning framework for the 7-degree-of-freedom left arm of the Unitree~G1 humanoid robot, combining a physics-based, experimentally identified electrical power model with a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) policy trained in a Pinocchio-based rigid-body dynamics simulator. The RL policy operates on an incremental joint-position action space and is trained with a Hybrid Constellation Reward that combines a four-point end-effector constellation distance with a torque-norm energy proxy; after % $5\times10^6$ training it reaches a $69.9\%$ success rate over $1\,000$ random targets in kinematic simulation, at a mean energy of \SI{98.16}{\joule} on successful episodes. Finally, on the physical Unitree~G1, the policy is validated over three independent 10-target batches, achieving a mean energy of $71.5 \pm 48.3$\,J, an end-effector position error of $2.64 \pm 1.04$\,cm, and an orientation error of $6.92 \pm 1.33^\circ$ -- within the \SI{4}{\centi\metre}/$8.6^\circ$ training tolerance. These results constitute a first step toward energy-aware reinforcement-learning-based arm reaching for humanoid robots.

</details>


### [840] [ControlMap: Controllable High-Definition Map Generation for Traffic Scenario Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15930)
*Marwan Farag,Steffen Wäldele,Yu Yao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel, controllable pipeline for generating High Definition (HD) maps for autonomous driving systems using latent diffusion and ControlNet, addressing limitations in current map generation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving simulation pipelines are limited by the expensive, labor-intensive process of creating HD maps and lack sufficient scenario diversity. Moreover, existing generative map models lack fine control over specific road topologies.

Method: The authors propose a data-driven HD map generation method by injecting spatial guidance signals into a latent diffusion model combined with ControlNet. The method enables adjustable conditioning strength via classifier-free guidance and city-level style transfer using city label conditioning.

Result: The proposed method produces realistic and controllable HD maps that follow input road topologies and retain city-specific details. The paper also introduces two novel metrics for evaluating map adherence to control signals and similarity to ground-truth maps.

Conclusion: This approach offers a scalable and controllable way to generate HD maps for autonomous driving simulations, achieving both diversity and accuracy while reducing dependency on costly manual processes.

Abstract: Simulation is central to validating autonomous driving systems, yet current pipelines are limited by insufficient scenario diversity due to costly High Definition (HD) map creation. Scaling HD maps requires expensive data collection and manual processing. Moreover, existing generative models lack the fine-grained control necessary to target specific road topologies during generation.
  This paper presents a data-driven pipeline for controllable HD map generation using latent diffusion and ControlNet for spatial conditioning. To our knowledge, we are the first to inject spatial guidance signals into a diffusion model for HD map synthesis. Furthermore, our model supports adjustable conditioning strength through classifier-free guidance and city-level style transfer via city label conditioning. To complement existing metrics, we introduce two novel metrics to evaluate adherence to the control signal and similarity to ground-truth maps. Experiments demonstrate that our model generates realistic HD maps that faithfully follow input road topologies while accurately preserving city-specific details.

</details>


### [841] [Friction Characterization of a Cable-Driven Differential Actuation System for Lower-Limb Exoskeletons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15997)
*Alberto Maria Nobili,Fabio Salsedo,Alessandro Filippeschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel differential actuation method for hip-knee exoskeletons, eliminating torque sensors by using a model-based friction estimation strategy that reduces system complexity and weight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop lighter and less complex lower-limb exoskeletons by addressing the limitations of traditional systems that rely on independently actuated joints and torque sensors.

Method: Proposed a differential actuation system for cooperative torque sharing between two motors. Used a linear differential mapping and developed a friction estimation strategy to enable accurate torque estimation without requiring torque sensors.

Result: Demonstrated the feasibility of sensorless torque estimation in a physical prototype of a differentially actuated hip-knee exoskeleton module.

Conclusion: The novel differential actuation architecture simplifies the design of lower-limb exoskeletons by removing the need for torque sensors and maintaining accurate torque control, with potential to reduce system weight and complexity.

Abstract: Lower-limb exoskeletons require actuation systems that can provide accurate joint torque control while preserving low mass and encumbrance. Conventional architectures often rely on independently actuated joints and joint-level torque sensors, increasing system complexity and weight. This paper presents a novel differential actuation architecture for hip-knee flexion/extension, enabling cooperative torque sharing between two motors via a linear differential mapping between motor and joint. To compensate for transmission losses, a model-based friction estimation strategy is developed and experimentally implemented, allowing accurate joint torque estimation without the need for torque sensors. The proposed solution is validated on a physical prototype, demonstrating the feasibility of sensorless torque estimation in a differentially actuated hip-knee module of a lower-limb exoskeleton.

</details>


### [842] [$λ$-Reachability: Geometric-Horizon Safety Bellman Equations for Humanoid Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16022)
*Rui Chen,Shangtao Li,Yifan Sun,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces $λ$-Reachability, a novel approach to safety analysis for robotic systems, emphasizing scalability in high-dimensional settings and improving safety estimations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing safety analysis methods, particularly fixed one-step Bellman updates, in handling high-dimensional robotic systems.

Method: The method involves a stochastic multi-step safety value estimator with a geometrically distributed rollout horizon, interpolating between local updates and long-horizon safety targets using parameters $λ$ and $δ$.

Result: Experimental results show improved safe-set boundary classification and safety margin estimation for simulated and real humanoid robots, outperforming traditional single-step baselines.

Conclusion: $λ$-Reachability offers a scalable, effective framework for robotic safety analysis, supporting both theoretical reinforcement learning foundations and practical robotic system applications.

Abstract: We introduce $λ$-Reachability, a scalable approach to Hamilton--Jacobi safety analysis for high-dimensional robotic systems. Unlike prior discounted formulations that rely on fixed one-step Bellman updates, $λ$-Reachability employs a stochastic multi-step estimator of the safety value, using a geometrically distributed rollout horizon together with a randomly absorbed terminal. Conceptually analogous to TD($λ$), $λ$-Reachability interpolates between local self-consistency updates and long-horizon max-over-trajectory safety targets via an interpretable horizon-control parameter. Unlike TD($λ$), where the terminal value is always incorporated in learning targets, the terminal safety value in $λ$-Reachability is only used at a probability controlled by parameter $δ$. We formally show that for $δ<1$, the update induces a contraction mapping that allows temporal-difference learning; as $λ\to 1$, the estimator recovers the undiscounted reachability objective. We apply $λ$-Reachability to high-dimensional safety learning problems with both simulated and real humanoid robots under balance and collision avoidance constraints. Experimental results demonstrate that $λ$-Reachability significantly improves both safe-set boundary classification and safety margin estimation compared to single-step temporal-difference baselines.

</details>


### [843] [Leveraging Deep Learning for Object and Position Recognition of Load Carriers for Autonomous Logistics Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16042)
*Christoph Legat,Tobias Miller,Marco Riess*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a deep learning framework for mobile robotics to autonomously detect and estimate the pose of load carriers using RGBD data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the need for reliable autonomous detection and pose estimation of load carriers to enhance automated logistics and intralogistics operations.

Method: A convolutional neural network processes RGBD data to identify predefined landmarks on load carriers, which are then combined with geometric knowledge to compute poses.

Result: The proposed deep learning-based method achieves accurate load carrier detection and pose estimation, validated through extensive software and hardware experiments.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates effectiveness and reliability for industrial and autonomously operated intralogistics applications.

Abstract: This work explores the use of artificial intelligence in mobile robotics to achieve autonomous detection and pose estimation of load carriers for automated pickup. A deep neural network is designed to recognize predefined landmarks on the carrier from RGBD data; these landmarks are then used to compute the carrier's pose. The network operates directly on RGBD images to estimate landmark positions, which form the basis for determining the carrier's location. The approach is validated in extensive experiments and comprises both software and hardware implementations.
  A deep learning-based framework is presented to detect load carriers and estimate their pose for use with autonomous logistics vehicles. Our method uses a convolutional neural network to identify characteristic reference points on the carrier from RGBD input and computes its pose by combining these inferred landmarks with prior geometric knowledge. Experiments show that the resulting accuracy is sufficient for reliable load carrier detection in industrial environments, confirming the suitability of the method for autonomous intralogistics applications.

</details>


### [844] [A Smart-Scheduled Hybrid (SSH) EKF-FGO State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16057)
*Eric Levi,Soosan Beheshti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper explores the role of optimization scheduling as an independent design variable in hybrid state estimation systems, balancing accuracy and computation cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State estimation methods in robotics often trade estimation accuracy for computational efficiency, but the explicit consideration of optimization scheduling in this trade-off is under-explored.

Method: A Smart Scheduled Hybrid (SSH) EKF-FGO framework was created to isolate optimization scheduling experimentally. EKF handles state propagation, while batch optimization is periodically invoked. Simulations were conducted in a planar SLAM environment.

Result: Findings indicate that scheduling impacts pre-optimization drift, transient error behavior, and runtime. Certain scheduling regimes achieve much of the benefits of global optimization at reduced computational cost.

Conclusion: Optimization scheduling is a critical yet under-explored element in hybrid state estimation, with potential to improve trade-offs between accuracy and cost.

Abstract: Reliable state estimation in robotics and control re quires balancing estimation accuracy against computational cost. While filtering-based methods such as the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) provide efficient real-time updates, and optimisation based formulations using factor graphs improve global consistency, the role of optimisation scheduling is often treated implicitly rather than examined as an explicit design variable. This paper presents an experimental study that explicitly isolates optimisation scheduling using a Smart Scheduled Hybrid (SSH) EKF-FGO framework as a controlled testbed. By combining EKF-based state propagation with periodically invoked batch optimisation and holding solver structure and effort fixed, the main contribution of this work is the experimental characterisation of optimisation scheduling as an independent design variable governing the trade-off between intermediate estimation accuracy and computational cost. Simulation results in a planar SLAM environment show that scheduling strongly influences pre optimisation drift, transient error behaviour, and runtime. In particular, the results identify operating regimes in which most of the benefit of global optimisation can be retained at a fraction of the computational cost, highlighting optimisation scheduling as an under-explored yet critical consideration in hybrid state estimation systems.

</details>


### [845] [A Deployment Case Study in Robotic Apparel Automation: Digital Twin Integration, Interoperability, and Workforce Enablement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16078)
*Gokul Narayanan,Abhiroop Ajith,Jonathan Zornow,Carlos Calle,Auralis Herrero Lugo,Jose Luis Susa Rincon,Chengtao Wen,Eugen Solowjow*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a robotic sewing system for denim manufacturing and highlights the importance of system-level integration for adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apparel automation is challenging due to the deformable nature of fabrics, requiring innovative solutions for robotic manipulation.

Method: The approach involves integrating a digital thread module for parsing production drawings, a digital twin for validation, collaborative robotics, runtime monitoring, and operator training tools.

Result: The proposed system was successfully deployed in factory settings for denim shorts sewing tasks, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness.

Conclusion: A combination of digital twin validation, task generation from digital threads, interoperability, monitoring, and operator support is critical for advancing robotic apparel automation.

Abstract: Despite steady advances in flexible automation in sectors such as electronics and automotive manufacturing, apparel automation remains challenging because fabrics are deformable and difficult to manipulate with robots. This paper presents a deployment-oriented case study of a robotic sewing system for denim manufacturing, emphasizing the system-level integration required for practical adoption. At the engineering level, a digital thread module parses DXF production drawings into process parameters and executable robot trajectories, reducing manual programming effort and enabling rapid re-targeting across sewing operations. In parallel, a digital twin of the workcell is used during pre-deployment to validate reach and clearance, refine layout and sequencing, evaluate operator access, and assess cycle-time compatibility with upstream and downstream tasks, thereby reducing commissioning risk. At deployment, the system integrates a collaborative robot with conventional sewing equipment, welding, suction fixtures, and machine-level controllers through an interoperability layer. Runtime monitoring and verification, including seam monitoring, collision checking, and trajectory-level validation, improve robustness under environmental variability, while operator-facing training and guidance tools support setup, troubleshooting, and technology adoption. Two staged factory deployments on denim shorts, covering 2D pocket operations and 3D garment-shaping seams, show that digital-twin-based validation, digital-thread-driven task generation, interoperability, runtime verification, and operator training are important for scaling robotic apparel automation.

</details>


### [846] [Scaling Short-Term Memory of Visuomotor Policies for Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16178)
*Rutav Shah,Rajat Kumar Jenamani,Xiaohan Zhang,Lingfeng Sun,Roberto Martín-Martín,Yuke Zhu,Deva Ramanan,Karl Schmeckpeper*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents PRISM, a transformer-based visuomotor policy architecture addressing short-term memory in robotic tasks, and introduces ReMemBench, a new benchmark for evaluating memory in visuomotor control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most visuomotor policies today rely on immediate sensory inputs and fail to utilize past experiences, hindering their ability to perform tasks that require short-term memory, like retrieving occluded objects or delayed actions.

Method: PRISM combines a transformer-based architecture with two key components: (i) gated attention to suppress irrelevant details, and (ii) a hierarchical architecture to compactly encode and integrate temporally extended information. ReMemBench serves as a benchmark for systematic evaluation.

Result: PRISM outperforms alternative baselines by 5%–12% on ReMemBench and achieves notable improvements of 11%–15% on external RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks over models without short-term memory capabilities.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates PRISM's capacity to effectively handle memory-augmented visuomotor policies in diverse short-term memory scenarios while introducing ReMemBench to facilitate further research in scaling memory for long-horizon visuomotor tasks.

Abstract: Many robotic tasks require short-term memory, whether it's retrieving an object that's no longer visible or turning off an appliance after a set period. Yet, most visuomotor policies trained via imitation learning rely only on immediate sensory input without using past experiences to guide decisions. We present PRISM, a transformer-based architecture for visuomotor policies to effectively use short-term memory via two key components: (i) gated attention, which filters retrieved information to suppress irrelevant details, improving performance by reducing the spurious correlations between the history and current action prediction, (ii) a hierarchical architecture that first compresses local information into compact tokens and then integrates them to capture temporally extended dependencies, improving its compute and memory footprint. Together, these mechanisms enable us to scale short-term memory in visuomotor policies for up to two minutes. To systematically evaluate memory in visuomotor control, we introduce ReMemBench -- a benchmark of eight diverse household manipulation tasks spanning four categories of short-term memory -- designed to foster general memory mechanisms rather than siloed, task-specific solutions. PRISM consistently outperforms prior works, including recurrent architectures, transformers, and their variants -- achieving an absolute improvement of 5%--12% over the strongest baseline. On the RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks, it achieves absolute improvements of 11%--15% over its no-memory variant and fine-tuned Vision-Language-Action baselines such as GR00T-N1-3B and OpenVLA, despite not leveraging any large-scale pretraining. Together, PRISM and ReMemBench establish a foundation for developing and evaluating short-term memory-augmented visuomotor policies that scale to long-horizon tasks. Additional materials are available at https://shahrutav.github.io/short-term-memory

</details>


### [847] [ATHENA: Accelerated Multi-Task Heterogeneous Influence Functions for Robot Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16208)
*Tao Xu,Jiaxin Wang,Runhao Zhang,Jiayi Guan,Xianchao Zeng,Weixi Song,Xinyu Zhou,Zhetao Chen,Guang Chen,Yong-Lu Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ATHENA enhances robot imitation learning by optimizing data curation for large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, achieving significant speedup and improved outcomes using fewer data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling influence functions to billion-parameter VLA models for robot imitation learning faces computational and multitask issues that hinder effective data curation.

Method: ATHENA exploits Kronecker-structured gradients for cost reduction and employs a rank-r Random Truncated Approximation for efficient Hessian inversion, enabling faster and effective multitask influence computation.

Result: ATHENA achieves a 313.4x speedup in influence computation, outperforms full-data approaches with reduced demonstration data (50% in simulation, 66.7% in real robots), and balances multitask curation across 50 tasks.

Conclusion: ATHENA proves effective in multitask VLA fine-tuning for robot learning, minimizing data requirements while preserving or improving performance.

Abstract: In robot imitation learning, influence functions provide a principled approach to quantify each demonstration's effect on robot task outcomes, yet scaling them to billion-parameter Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is limited by computational and multitask bottlenecks. To this end, we propose ATHENA, an influence function framework tailored for multitask VLA data curation at a billion-parameter scale. Concretely, it leverages the Kronecker structure of linear-layer gradients to reduce projection cost, and approximates dense Hessian inversion with a rank-r Random Truncated Approximation, achieving about a 313.4x speedup in influence computation. Furthermore, ATHENA formulates global and local interactive influence to balance data curation across 50 jointly trained tasks. Extensive evaluations on RoboTwin 2.0 and real-robot deployment, covering 9.34 and 6.90 hours of demonstrations, respectively, show that ATHENA matches or exceeds full-data joint fine-tuning using only 50% of demonstrations in simulation and 66.7% of data across six real-robot tasks. Overall, ATHENA demonstrates its effectiveness for data curation in billion-parameter multitask VLA fine-tuning.

</details>


### [848] [PolyMerge: Compressing 3D Gaussian Splats with Polytope Coverings for Provably Safe Resource-Constrained Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16232)
*Jihoon Hong,Chih-Yuan Chiu,Sara Fridovich-Keil,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces PolyMerge, a method to transform memory-intensive 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) obstacle models into lightweight, union-comprised convex polytopes suitable for onboard path planning. It balances efficiency and collision safety and performs well in drone-based experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the memory- and compute-intensiveness of recent radiance field reconstruction methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), which inhibit their real-time applicability in onboard motion planning and obstacle avoidance.

Method: The paper describes PolyMerge, a framework that converts 3DGS scene representations into unions of convex polytopes, enabling tunable trade-offs between obstacle conservativeness and computational efficiency. These representations are integrated with control barrier functions (CBFs) for real-time trajectory planning.

Result: PolyMerge was showcased on a Crazyflie drone in simulations and hardware experiments, and it enabled the drone to compute and follow safe trajectories under strict computational constraints. It outperformed baseline methods in speed and maintained collision safety.

Conclusion: PolyMerge provides a lightweight, real-time capable alternative to existing 3DGS methods for safe navigation and motion planning in computation-constrained environments like drones, ensuring both safety and efficiency.

Abstract: Obstacle avoidance is essential for safe navigation and motion planning. Recent radiance field reconstruction methods enable object detection and modeling with high fidelity, but remain too memory- and compute-intensive for on-board perception-based path planning. To address these limitations, we propose PolyMerge to convert a large, photorealistic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model of a scene into a lightweight representation of convex polytopes whose union provably over-approximates all obstacles in the original 3DGS model. PolyMerge tunes the polytope count to trade off conservativeness and compute cost, and integrates with control barrier functions (CBFs) to plan collision-free paths. We showcase PolyMerge in simulation and hardware experiments on a Crazyflie drone, which uses PolyMerge to compute and follow safe trajectories in real time under severe onboard compute constraints, outperforming baselines in speed while guaranteeing safety. For our code and videos, visit https://athlon76.github.io/PolyMerge-website/.

</details>


### [849] [TopoRetarget: Interaction-Preserving Retargeting for Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16272)
*Jielin Wu,Shenzhe Yao,Guanqi He,Xiaohan Liu,Zhaoqing Zeng,Xiangrui Jiang,Han Yang,Wentao Zhang,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes TopoRetarget, a retargeting framework for adapting human hand-object demonstrations to dexterous robot hands while preserving interaction fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human hand-object demonstrations are dense motion references for training dexterous robot manipulation, but existing retargeting methods fail to adequately preserve the fine details of hand pose and hand-object interactions necessary for effective policy learning.

Method: The authors introduce TopoRetarget, which uses a sparse interaction graph with distance-weighted Laplacian deformation, directional consistency, kinematic constraints, and penetration handling for hand-object retargeting.

Result: TopoRetarget offers superior contact precision and alignment on the ContactPose dataset, improves training success in Pen-Spin tasks by 40.6 percentage points, and allows zero-shot transfer to Wuji Hand hardware for tasks like cube reorientation and pen spinning.

Conclusion: TopoRetarget effectively retains task-relevant interactions during retargeting, enhancing RL training outcomes and enabling successful deployment on robotic hands.

Abstract: Human hand-object demonstrations provide dense reference motions for training dexterous manipulation reinforcement learning (RL) policies through reference tracking. However, to use such demonstrations for RL policy learning, retargeting must preserve hand pose and task-relevant hand-object contact structure. Otherwise, contact and feasibility artifacts can degrade downstream RL policy performance. We introduce TopoRetarget, an interaction-preserving retargeting framework that uses a single set of parameters across diverse retargeting conditions while maintaining task-relevant hand-object interaction and adapting human demonstrations to dexterous robot hands. The method constructs a sparse interaction graph over hand and object keypoints and optimizes distance-weighted Laplacian deformation with directional consistency, kinematic constraints, and penetration handling. Evaluations show that the generated references improve both interaction fidelity and policy learning: TopoRetarget achieves the best contact precision and alignment over all baselines on the ContactPose Dataset, improves Pen-Spin training success by 40.6 percentage points over the existing baseline methods, and enables zero-shot transfer to Wuji Hand hardware on cube reorientation and pen spinning.

</details>


### [850] [Is Your Trajectory Displacement Safe in Long-tail?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16313)
*Qiao Sun,Weicheng Zheng,Yixin Huang,Hang Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces FluidTest, a novel method to evaluate autonomous driving planners by focusing on safety-relevant failures through human-aligned and explainable assessment methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation pipelines for autonomous driving struggle with long-tail, safety-critical scenarios and often lack human alignment, safety awareness, verifiability, and explainability, which limits their effectiveness in identifying planner deficiencies.

Method: The paper proposes FluidTest, which includes: (1) a structured WebUI protocol for pairwise human annotations; (2) a taxonomy of 32 semantic threats grounded in evidence-based decision graphs; and (3) a three-agent system for precision verification and auditability.

Result: Using the WOD-E2E dataset, FluidTest demonstrated consistent annotator agreement and identified additional safety-relevant failures in 65% of Poutine trajectories and 51% of RAP trajectories, revealing gaps in state-of-the-art planner evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: FluidTest enhances the evaluation of autonomous driving planners by detecting safety-relevant behaviors that conventional metrics like Rater Feedback Scores and Average Displacement Errors fail to capture, providing a more reliable and explainable approach to planner evaluation.

Abstract: Long-tail scenarios remain a major bottleneck for autonomous driving evaluation, even as datasets grow by orders of magnitude. Existing evaluation pipelines are rarely human-aligned, safety-aware, verifiable, and explainable at the same time: closed-loop metrics often saturate among strong planners, while unstructured human ratings can be noisy without a carefully designed protocol. We formulate planning evaluation as additional-threat detection: given a planner trajectory and an expert reference, does the planner's displacement introduce new unsafe driving behavior? We propose FluidTest, an evaluation pipeline with three components: a pairwise WebUI protocol for reliable human annotation; a taxonomy of 32 semantic threats with evidence-grounded decision graphs; and a three-agent verification system with reflection for precision and auditability. Experiments on the WOD-E2E dataset show that FluidTest produces consistent labels among trained annotators and identifies additional threats in 65% of Poutine trajectories and 51% of RAP trajectories. These results show that state-of-the-art planners can still exhibit substantial safety-relevant failures despite high Rater Feedback Scores (RFS) and low Average Displacement Error (ADE). Additional details, guidance, and code are available at https://fluidtest.web.app.

</details>


### [851] [ART-Glove: Articulated Tactile Glove for Contact-Grounded Dexterous Interaction Capture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16370)
*Changyi Lin,Ding Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces ART-Glove, a tactile glove designed to record dexterous hand movements and contact information for robot learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable capturing of human hand dexterity and contact-grounded data to advance dexterous robot learning.

Method: The glove combines 22 rigid jointed surfaces and piezoresistive tactile sensing for tracking motion and contact, recording synchronized data at 120 Hz.

Result: Evaluation confirms the glove maintains motion freedom while capturing both joint motion and tactile data effectively.

Conclusion: ART-Glove succeeds in preserving dexterity and capturing comprehensive information to aid robot learning.

Abstract: We present ART-Glove, an articulated tactile glove designed to capture contact-grounded dexterous demonstrations while preserving human dexterity. ART-Glove makes hand-side contact geometry explicit with 16 rigid functional surfaces covering the fingers, thumb, and palm. Twenty-two anatomically aligned joints connect these surfaces and allow them to follow human hand motion during dexterous manipulation. Encoder-based sensing tracks surface motion, while dense piezoresistive tactile sensing records contact over the same surfaces. The complete system captures synchronized 22-DoF joint measurements and 2048-taxel tactile measurements at 120 Hz. We evaluate ART-Glove across experiments on motion freedom, joint sensing, tactile sensing, and contact-rich interaction capture, demonstrating its ability to preserve human dexterity while recording contact-grounded information that can support downstream dexterous robot learning.

</details>


### [852] [SemGeoNav:A Safety-Guided Visual Navigation Approach with Semantic Reasoning and Geometric Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16400)
*Yu Liu,Zongyang Chen,Yan Guo,Chao Liu,Xianfei Pan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces SemGeoNav, combining end-to-end semantics with geometric planners for better visual navigation and obstacle avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end visual navigation models lack geometric constraints, leading to unreliable obstacle avoidance, while traditional geometric planners cannot handle high-dimensional visual targets effectively.

Method: SemGeoNav integrates end-to-end semantic reasoning with geometry-based local planning and includes a trajectory smoothing mechanism for robust navigation.

Result: Experiments on a Unitree Go2 robot show SemGeoNav outperforms methods like ViNT and NoMaD in higher success rates and shorter navigation times.

Conclusion: SemGeoNav achieves robust image-based navigation, improving both semantic reasoning and obstacle avoidance over existing methods.

Abstract: Learning-based visual navigation has enhanced semantic goal-reaching capabilities. However, due to their black-box nature, purely end-to-end models often lack explicit geometric constraints, leading to unpredictable and unreliable obstacle avoidance in open environments. Conversely, traditional geometric planners ensure safety but struggle with high-dimensional visual targets. To address these limitations, we propose SemGeoNav, a novel hierarchical visual navigation framework.It tightly integrates the high-level semantic reasoning of end-to-end models with the reliable local planning ability of geometry-based methods, achieving robust image-based navigation while significantly improving obstacle avoidance. Furthermore, we introduce a temporal trajectory smoothing mechanism to ensure continuous and stable robot motion. We evaluated SemGeoNav on a Unitree Go2 quadruped robot in real-world environments. The results demonstrate that SemGeoNav outperforms existing representative methods, including ViNT and NoMaD, achieving higher success rates and shorter navigation times.

</details>


### [853] [An Augmented Reality Brain-Robot Interface for Generalist Robot Arm Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16413)
*Shangkai Zhang,Rousslan Fernand Julien Dossa,Luca Nunziante,Marina Di Vincenzo,Kai Arulkumaran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces an augmented reality (AR) and brain-computer interface (BCI) system for general robotic arm manipulation using gaze and motor imagery, demonstrating feasibility in everyday tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the task-specific limitations of current AR-BCI systems and enable generalist, real-world robot manipulation for assistive purposes.

Method: The system combines gaze-based object selection and motor imagery for action control, using eye-tracking and context-aware visual overlays. It was tested with 18 participants performing multi-step daily activities in a shared autonomy framework.

Result: Participants successfully executed sequential tasks with high engagement, and the system achieved a usability score above 70 ("Good" rating) in its feasibility study.

Conclusion: The proposed AR BRI shows potential for complex robotic assistance, with promising usability and effectiveness, motivating further trials with the target population.

Abstract: The integration of augmented reality (AR) and EEG-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) offers a promising path for enabling intuitive control of robots for assistive purposes. However, existing AR brain-robot interface (BRI) systems are often constrained to task-specific structures, limiting their utility in real-world environments. We present an AR BRI designed for generalist robot arm manipulation that combines gaze-based object selection with motor imagery action control. Our system uses eye-tracking for intuitive object targeting and context-aware visual overlays ("Place" and "Use") to guide the user through tasks within a shared autonomy framework. We evaluated the interface through a feasibility study with 18 healthy participants performing three multi-step activities of daily living: drinking, using a drawer, and operating an oven. Our results demonstrate that this interaction paradigm enables effective sequential task execution and high user engagement, achieving a "Good" usability rating (SUS > 70). These findings support the feasibility of the proposed interaction paradigm for complex BCI-driven robotic assistance, and motivate future evaluation with the intended target population. Project website: https://ar-bri-manip.github.io/.

</details>


### [854] [V2P-Manip: Learning Dexterous Manipulation from Monocular Human Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16436)
*Kaihan Chen,Yanming Shao,Haifeng Ji,Xiaokang Yang,Yao Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces V2P-Manip, a framework for learning robotic dexterous manipulation from human demonstration videos, achieving high accuracy and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the high cost of teleoperation data by extracting physically plausible and visually accurate manipulation trajectories from monocular videos.

Method: The proposed method, V2P-Manip, integrates 3D asset acquisition, trajectory estimation, and policy learning, with a two-stage refinement to ensure physical and spatial consistency.

Result: V2P-Manip outperforms prior approaches in pose accuracy and adaptability on TACO and OakInk benchmarks, achieving over 75% task success in synthetic manipulation environments.

Conclusion: The framework provides an efficient approach to learning dexterous manipulation, capable of generalizing across varied tasks and hand embodiments.

Abstract: Achieving autonomous robotic dexterous manipulation requires precise, human-like action sequences at scale. As a scalable supplement to costly teleoperation data, extracting trajectories with both visual fidelity and physical plausibility from monocular videos represents a promising frontier in embodied AI. To this end, we introduce V2P-Manip, an efficient framework designed to learn dexterous manipulation policies directly from human demonstration videos. We establish an efficient, integrated pipeline encompassing 3D asset acquisition, trajectory estimation, and dexterous policy learning. To bridge the gap between visual perception and physical constraints, we introduce a two-stage refinement process to enforce spatial alignment and physical consistency. Evaluations on the TACO and OakInk benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms previous methods in pose accuracy, adaptability to unstructured environments, and training efficiency. Ultimately, experimental results confirm an average success rate of over 75% across multiple synthetic manipulation tasks and validate the adaptability of the extracted manipulation priors across diverse dexterous hand embodiments.

</details>


### [855] [Training and Evaluating Diffusion Policies with Long Context Lengths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16447)
*Abhinav Agarwal,Adam Wei,Taylan Kargin,Michael Zeng,Cole Becker,Arif Kerem Dayi,Pablo Parrilo,Asuman Ozdaglar,Russ Tedrake*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper explores the impact of context length in imitation learning for robotic tasks, proposing a new training method for policy improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of imitation learning policies that depend only on a short history of observations, thus failing in tasks requiring memory or getting stuck in repetitive errors.

Method: Benchmarking policies under different context lengths with a robust conditioning approach (UNet+Cross-Attention), and introducing a new algorithm to jointly train policies across multiple context lengths.

Result: Scaling context length is more robust than assumed, and the proposed techniques enhance performance, achieving high success across tasks with lower sample complexity.

Conclusion: Careful scaling of context length and new training methods substantially improve imitation learning policies, particularly for memory-intensive robotic tasks.

Abstract: Imitation learning has enabled highly-dexterous robotic manipulation from RGB observations. Policies trained with these methods, however, typically condition robot actions on only a short history of observations. These policies cannot solve tasks that require memory and can get stuck repeatedly executing the same failing motions. In this work, we first benchmark policy performance as context length is incrementally increased from short to long, across a spectrum of tasks with varying local stability and memory requirements, and in multiple data regimes. To our knowledge, this is the first study to investigate context length in imitation learning at this level of detail. Our results challenge prior claims: naively scaling context length is not as brittle as advertised in literature. With an appropriate conditioning method and denoising backbone (UNet+Cross-Attention), single-task policies achieve high success rates on many tasks in the usual data regime even with naive scaling. Next, we propose a training algorithm to jointly train policies at multiple context lengths, further reducing the sample complexity of long-context learning. Finally, we apply our findings to re-evaluate some previously proposed solutions to long-context imitation learning.

</details>


### [856] [RHO: Your Coding Agent is Secretly a Roboticist](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16458)
*Karim Elmaaroufi,Justin Svegliato,Sarunas Kalade,Graham Schelle,Sanjit A. Seshia,Matei Zaharia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces Robotics Harness Optimization (RHO), a novel paradigm to enable efficient real-time robotics task-solving with large language models by pre-training multi-file neurosymbolic policy repositories, achieving superior performance on various benchmarks compared to existing multi-turn approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from addressing the limitations of current CaP systems, which rely on multi-turn code generation loops that are impractical for real-time robot control, by exploring efficient ways to pre-compute interpretable policies.

Method: The paper proposes RHO, which trains LLM-based agents to optimize multi-file policy repositories with reflective feedback from environment execution rewards, enabling single-turn execution and eliminating corrective code edits post-deployment.

Result: RHO achieves substantial performance improvements—45.0% in LIBERO-PRO and a state-of-the-art 70.0% success in Robosuite—outperforming prior multi-turn systems. When integrated with real-time LLM control loops, it enhances results while reducing computational overhead.

Conclusion: RHO demonstrates that pre-computation of multi-file policy repositories leads to efficient and interpretable robotics task-solving, outperforming traditional multi-turn systems in both success rates and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Code-as-Policies (CaP) has shown that large language models (LLMs) can write code to solve robotics tasks by composing perception, planning, and control primitives. Recent CaP systems, however, rely on multi-turn code-generation loops at test time, which is often infeasible for real-time robot control. We introduce Robotics Harness Optimization (RHO), a novel paradigm in which tool-enabled coding agents, at training time, propose and search for interpretable, neurosymbolic multi-file policy repositories (Repositories-as-Policies) that compose these primitives rather than a single prompt, function, or file. RHO searches with reflective feedback from environment reward and execution rather than teleoperation demonstrations. It generalizes to perturbed pick-and-place settings like LIBERO-PRO, where OpenVLA scores 0.0% and $π_{0.5}$ averages 12.83%. Using the same low-level primitives, RHO reaches a 45.0% success rate, 2.5x higher than the strongest multi-turn agentic system, and 3.5x higher than $π_{0.5}$. On Robosuite, RHO sets a new state-of-the-art of 70.0%, exceeding the prior multi-turn record of 68.29% using single-turn execution with no corrective LLM code edits at deployment. When an LLM is used in the control loop, as on RAI's O3DE benchmark, RHO optimizes the deployed agent's multi-file harness of prompts, tools, and control code, improving held-out success from 23.5% to 44.3% with 20% less wall-clock time and 27% fewer tool calls.

</details>


### [857] [HOLO-MPPI: Multi-Scenario Motion Planning via Hierarchical Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16480)
*Youngjae Min,Jovin D'sa,Faizan M. Tariq,David Isele,Navid Azizan,Sangjae Bae*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HOLO-MPPI is a novel motion planning framework that combines learning-based high-level planning with real-time low-level control to improve performance in diverse scenarios, particularly for tasks like autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion planning methods struggle to handle multi-scenario environments due to challenges such as distribution shifts, reward misspecification, and manual design limitations.

Method: The framework integrates offline learning of a high-level policy for scenario-robust plans and a learned world model with online use of MPPI for fine-tuned local control using the high-level policy as a data-driven prior.

Result: HOLO-MPPI demonstrates better performance than MPPI and end-to-end RL across various driving scenarios, while ensuring real-time control capabilities.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively bridges high-level learning and low-level control, providing scalable and robust motion planning for real-world applications like autonomous driving.

Abstract: Robots deployed in the real world must plan motions across diverse scenarios without per-scenario retuning. End-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) can generalize across scenarios but often becomes brittle under distribution shift, reward misspecification, and stochastic interactions. Model predictive path integral (MPPI) control enables strong real-time refinement without gradients, but its performance depends on a well-shaped sampling prior, while manually designing the priors does not scale to multi-scenario deployment. We present HOLO-MPPI (High-level Offline, Low-level Online MPPI), a multi-scenario motion planning framework that combines high-level policy learning with low-level stochastic optimal control. Offline, we learn a high-level policy that proposes scenario-robust plans in an abstract action space, with a learned world model for online rollout. Online, the policy serves as a data-driven prior generator that parameterizes MPPI's sampling distribution conditioned on the current observation and goal. MPPI then optimizes low-level control sequences around this prior in real time to adapt to local disturbances. We instantiate HOLO-MPPI in autonomous driving by designing an effective high-level action space and tailored model architectures. Our evaluation across diverse driving scenarios shows that HOLO-MPPI improves upon MPPI and end-to-end RL baselines while maintaining real-time control.

</details>


### [858] [Robots that Collaborate: Sequential Asymmetric Imitation for Learning Coupled Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16490)
*Yincong Chen,Ranpeng Qiu,Zihao Li,Yanan Zhou,Guoqiang Ren,Weiming Zhi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Sequential Asymmetric Imitation (SAI), a method to train collaborative multi-robot teams for physical interaction in manipulation tasks without explicit communication or synchronized demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in coordinated robot collaboration, such as failures due to mistimed interactions or poor partner adaptations, especially when robots handle shared objects.

Method: The SAI method involves a staged curriculum: training one robot with human demonstrations, training its partner robot with the deployed first robot, and refining the first robot with targeted interventions in failure scenarios.

Result: SAI improves task success, synchronization, and cooperative yielding in real-world dual-robot manipulation tasks compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The findings show that well-structured imitation curriculum can enable effective learning for physically coupled robot collaboration without relying on explicit coordination or dual-operator demonstrations.

Abstract: Collaborative mobile manipulation requires robots to coordinate with a partially observed partner while physically interacting through shared objects. This is difficult because failures often arise not from poor local skills, but from mistimed waiting, yielding, pulling, releasing, or repositioning. We study this problem with two bimanual mobile manipulators coupled through rigid and deformable objects. We propose Sequential Asymmetric Imitation (SAI), a single-teleoperator curriculum for learning coupled multi-robot behaviors without synchronized dual-operator demonstrations or explicit inter-robot communication. SAI trains Robot A from unilateral demonstrations with a compliant human partner, trains Robot B against the deployed Robot A policy, and then refines Robot A using sparse interventions near coordination failures. This staged process exposes the policies to increasingly realistic partner behaviors, including delay, phase mismatch,insufficient yielding, and interaction conflict. Across real-world dual-robot manipulation tasks, SAI improves task success, phase synchronization, and partner-contingent yielding over independent imitation and curriculum-ablation baselines. These results suggest that physically coupled collaboration can be learned through the structure of the imitation curriculum, rather than through synchronized multi-operator demonstrations or explicit coordination mechanisms.Project page:http://cyc0429.github.io/sai-project-page/

</details>


### [859] [HATS: A Human-Agent Teleoperation System for Multi-Arm Data Collection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16491)
*Zesen Lin,Jian-Jian Jiang,Haoming Cen,Xiao-Ming Wu,Dandan Zhang,Wei-Shi Zheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces HATS, a novel system for multi-robot teleoperation that allows a single human operator to effectively control multiple robotic arms with MLLM-based assistance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-arm robotic manipulation for tasks requiring high coordination is challenging due to the difficulties of either single-operator cognitive load or multi-operator collaboration costs.

Method: HATS involves decoupled control for a human operator to handle two primary arms and a training-free agent for two assistive arms, with voice-command functionality for error correction and collision prevention.

Result: HATS achieves data collection success rates comparable to expert dual-human teams and generates effective datasets for training downstream policies.

Conclusion: The proposed single-operator, human-agent collaboration system effectively addresses the challenges of multi-arm manipulation, demonstrating efficiency and reliability in both data collection and task performance.

Abstract: Many real-world manipulation scenarios, such as handling complex collaborative tasks and dealing with large workspaces, require coordination of more than two robotic arms. Consequently, an effective multi-arm teleoperation system is required to collect demonstrations for training coordinated multi-arm manipulation policies. However, existing teleoperation frameworks mainly focus on single-operator or multi-operator setups, facing a practical trade-off between the cognitive load placed on a single operator and the coordination cost incurred by multiple operators. To address this problem, we introduce HATS, a human-agent teleoperation system that enables a single human operator, assisted by an MLLM-based agent, to collect data for multi-arm manipulation tasks. Our system decouples the control space: two primary arms are directly teleoperated by the human, while two assistive arms are controlled by a training-free agent that handles sub-tasks. In addition, the human operator can use voice commands to prevent collisions and correct assistive arm behaviors during execution. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that HATS achieves data collection efficiency and success rates comparable to expert dual-human teams. Moreover, downstream policy evaluations demonstrate the efficacy and quality of the data collected through HATS.

</details>


### [860] [APEX: Adaptive Policy Execution for Precise Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16504)
*Mengfei Zhao,Chenxi Jiang,Tuo An,Jindou Jia,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces APEX, a framework to address the execution gap caused by the disconnect between high-level policies and low-level controllers in imitation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the systematic deviation of realized actions from high-level policy commands in scenarios where modifying pretrained policies or controllers is infeasible.

Method: APEX serves as a plug-and-play module between a policy and a controller, dynamically adapting test-time references using low-level state feedback and guaranteeing convergence.

Result: APEX achieves a 41.2% reduction in tracking errors and enhances manipulation success by 4.8-25.8 percentage points across several policy classes.

Conclusion: APEX effectively bridges the execution gap in imitation learning setups without intrusive changes to policies or controllers, yielding significant performance improvements.

Abstract: Modern imitation learning methods, including visuomotor and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies, typically output high-level action references that are executed by low-level controllers. However, the absence of higher-order reference signals, together with the policy's lack of awareness of the underlying low-level control dynamics during training, inevitably induces an execution gap. As a result, realized actions deviate systematically from policy-commanded ones, with a critical impact on precision-sensitive manipulation. Prior work either modifies the policy architecture or the low-level controller, both requiring intrusive changes to the pretrained policy or packaged controller. This raises a natural question: when the policy and controller are both treated as inaccessible black boxes, can we bridge the execution gap? We propose Adaptive Policy Execution (APEX), a plug-and-play framework inserted between the policy and the controller that reconstructs a dynamically feasible reference from policy outputs and adapts at test-time according to low-level state feedback, with a provable convergence guarantee. Extensive empirical studies show that APEX reduces controller-induced tracking error by 41.2% on demonstration replay and improves manipulation success by 4.8--25.8 percentage points across four visuomotor and VLA policy classes.

</details>


### [861] [Agile Fall Recovery for Quadrotors with Bidirectional Thrust via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16513)
*Anke Zhao,Yuhang Zhong,Kenghou Hoi,Junyu Mou,Junjie Wang,Lijie Wang,Jialiang Hou,Fei Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents an RL-based framework for quadrotors to autonomously recover from falls to stable hover using minimal onboard sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of quadrotor fall recovery in real-world environments with limited sensing, constrained space, and unknown disturbances.

Method: Develop a recurrent policy trained through an asymmetric actor-critic architecture and integrate an INDI controller for policy output tracking, along with high-fidelity simulations.

Result: The method achieves zero-shot transfer to real-world experiments, robust fall recovery under variable conditions, and validates the significance of design choices through simulation studies.

Conclusion: Agile quadrotor fall recovery can be achieved without explicit state estimation, relying on limited and unreliable onboard sensing.

Abstract: Autonomous fall recovery is a critical capability for quadrotors operating in real-world environments, where collisions or failures may leave the vehicle resting on the ground in an arbitrary attitude. This problem is challenging because recovery must be achieved under limited onboard sensing, in constrained free space, with ground contact, and in the presence of unknown disturbances. In this letter, we present an RL-based framework for autonomous fall recovery of a quadrotor from arbitrary ground attitudes to stable hover using only lightweight onboard sensors. To address severe partial observability and intermittent sensor invalidity, we train a recurrent policy within an asymmetric actor--critic architecture, leveraging an Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller to track the policy output. Combined with high-fidelity simulations of motor response and optical flow, the overall training framework significantly reduces the sim-to-real gap. Simulation ablation studies validate the importance of the main design choices, while real-world experiments demonstrate zero-shot transfer and robust recovery under different initial attitudes, wind disturbances, and additional payloads. These results demonstrate that agile quadrotor fall recovery can be achieved without explicit state estimation using only limited and unreliable onboard sensing.

</details>


### [862] [ADAPT: Analytical Disturbance-Aware Policy Training for Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16542)
*Bofan Lyu,Jindou Jia,Kuangji Zuo,Yanshuo Lu,Shijia Han,Gen Li,Boyu Ma,Jingliang Li,Geng Li,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents ADAPT, a framework for humanoids to handle disturbance forces using an analytical whole-body observer, enhancing robustness and locomotion in human-centered environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of humanoids handling unexpected disturbances in force-interactive tasks while improving accuracy, task transferability, and robustness to out-of-distribution scenarios.

Method: The authors propose ADAPT, which employs an analytical whole-body disturbance observer to estimate external forces/torques online from accessible robot dynamics, integrating these estimations directly into the humanoid's control policy.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that ADAPT improves disturbance prediction accuracy, robustness under various perturbations, and velocity tracking for humanoids compared to baselines.

Conclusion: ADAPT equips humanoid robots with a physics-based means to handle disturbances, promotes lighter locomotion, and enhances robustness in diverse scenarios without relying on task-specific or motion history learning-based methods.

Abstract: Humanoids deployed in human-centered environments must handle force-interactive tasks, where external contacts introduce unexpected disturbances that disrupt locomotion accuracy and stability. Existing learning-based approaches rely on broad domain randomization, task-specific force objectives, or learning-based force estimators from motion history, each of which compromises accuracy, task transferability, or out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness. We present Analytical Disturbance-Aware Policy Training (ADAPT), a framework that equips humanoid policies with a physically grounded disturbance observer. The core of ADAPT is an analytical whole-body disturbance observer that estimates residual force/torque online with the accessible robot dynamics, without requiring force/torque sensors. Fed directly into the policy, the estimated disturbances give the humanoid an explicit, physics-derived sense of external force/torque that can generalize across diverse unseen scenes. Experiments on a Unitree G1 humanoid show that ADAPT achieves accurate disturbance prediction and stronger robustness than a proprioception-only baseline under torso perturbations, standing pushes, and asymmetric hand payloads, with improved velocity tracking even on OOD disturbances. Moreover, ADAPT enables penalizing inferred disturbances at lower-body joints to encourage lighter locomotion.

</details>


### [863] [Elastic ODYN: Differentiable Optimization for Infeasible Control and Learning in Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16564)
*Aristotelis Papatheodorou,Jose Rojas,Ioannis Havoutis,Carlos Mastalli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Elastic ODYN proposes a new QP solver to address infeasibility challenges in robotics and optimization, outperforming existing methods in robustness and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the issue of QPs becoming infeasible due to conflicting objectives, modeling errors, and contact degeneracy, which current solvers do not handle effectively.
