In this work, we introduce methods for comparing agents procedurally in different contexts, where the model, tasks, and approaches vary. We compare ten agents and find that they are identifiable by their behavioral habits, which we define as fingerprints: a probe over these procedural signatures attributes an unseen trajectory to the correct agent at 85.7% accuracy, controlling for leakage across tasks. We develop procedural representations for agent problem-solving procedures with an emergent vocabulary induction technique that is meant to be maximally compressive to avoid surface-level variation while being expressive enough to unveil the quirks of the models' patterns. We apply our framework to the software engineering evaluation dataset SWE-Bench to study the structural distinctness of agent trajectories and find that behavior is most similar between models from similar release periods and those that are distilled from one another (e.g., a distilled student model and its teacher have a Jensen-Shannon divergence of 0.25, about half the distance between other model pairs). As more models saturate evaluations, we believe that it will be important to probe model behavior along more holistic dimensions than success rates alone. We introduce ProcGrep, a library for auditing and evaluating agents for how they approach tasks at a procedural level given their traces in a top-down fashion. We believe this work has a range of applications to help developers work with and program coding agents, such as task-aware model routing, agent monitoring, and finer-grained cost analysis.

</details>


### [922] [Selection Without Signal, Recovery Through Expression: A Measurement Study of Post-Hoc Falsification Operators for Frozen Small Code Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16999)
*Mehmet Iscan*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper evaluates semantic post-hoc operators (selection, verification, repair, etc.) designed to improve the accuracy of small code models. However, it finds that these operators don't outperform Best-of-N (BoN) sampling, for systematic and mechanistic reasons such as task coverage limitations and error distinguishability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the plausible-but-wrong outputs of small frozen code models by applying post-hoc operators, without the need for retraining, to improve their practical use in privacy-constrained environments.

Method: The authors evaluate 26 semantic post-hoc operators using deterministic execution oracles and matched-compute protocols, testing against benchmarks like HumanEval+ and MBPP+ to analyze their effectiveness compared to Best-of-N sampling.

Result: The paper finds that most semantic post-hoc operators fail to improve held-out accuracy over BoN due to systematic issues like coverage gaps, inability to distinguish errors, and low consensus on correct alternatives. However, two solutions—expression-layer recovery (M1) and adaptive consensus early-stop (ACE)—show potential benefits.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that fixes to the model harness and enhanced coverage measurement are essential, and that semantic post-hoc reasoning alone is insufficient for improving the performance of small frozen code models.

Abstract: Frozen small code models (<=1.5B parameters, run locally without fine-tuning) suit offline and privacy-constrained use, but often emit plausible-but-wrong programs. A natural remedy is a post-hoc operator that selects, verifies, repairs, or re-processes the model's samples without retraining; in principled form it is Popperian: attack each candidate with a severe test, keep what survives. We measure whether such operators help. Under one deterministic execution oracle and a leakage-free, matched-compute protocol, 26 semantic post-hoc operators (selection, verification, repair, elimination, portfolios, sound vetoes, generation conditioning) are evaluated against Best-of-N (BoN); on the cells and benchmarks tested, none improves held-out accuracy over BoN. The negative is mechanistic: a coverage wall (systematic hard-task failures deeper sampling does not rescue), a capability scissors (a competent generator leaves almost no discriminable error among visible-test passers), and a near-empty consensus trap (the visible-pass-but-hidden-wrong majority a leakage-free selector needs rarely co-occurs with a correct alternative). A distribution-free do-no-harm bound cannot certify a harm rate <=alpha at zero observed harm unless n>=45. Two operators help on a different axis, outside the semantic output space. An expression-layer recovery (M1), the only accuracy gain here, recovers correct programs the standard extractor discards (robust extraction and public-test signature alignment); it does no harm (b10=0), is leakage-free, and lifts DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B by +12 tasks on HumanEval+ (p=2.4e-4). An adaptive consensus early-stop (ACE) is a calibrated compute-saving control (~19% saving, zero harm). M1 and the selection negative replicate on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ across three model cells. The lesson: fix the harness and measure coverage before blaming semantic post-hoc reasoning.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [923] [Do Large Language Models Have Emotions?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14742)
*Amit Goldenberg,James J. Gross*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the claim that the LLM Claude has 'functional emotions,' arguing that while Claude exhibits partial characteristics of emotions, it lacks key dynamic features observed in biological emotional systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to assess whether language models like Claude exhibit emotions and clarify how their functionalities compare to the emotional processes in biological systems.

Method: The evaluation compares the identified emotional representations in Claude Sonnet 4.5 against core functions of emotions in humans: situation interpretation and system-wide processing reorganization.

Result: Findings indicate that while Claude partially supports context-sensitive interpretation of emotions, its consistent emotional signatures conflict with neuroscience's observation of variable human emotion signatures. It also lacks the dynamic reorganization seen in biological emotions.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that current LLMs do not truly have emotions as defined in biological terms, but suggests criteria for what would be required for LLMs to exhibit such emotions.

Abstract: Do LLMs have emotions? A recent paper from Anthropic reports finding internal representations of emotion concepts in Claude Sonnet 4.5, concluding that the LLM has 'functional emotions.' We evaluate this claim against what is known about how emotions actually function in biological systems. We argue that emotions serve two core functions: the context-sensitive interpretation of situations, and the reorganization of processing across multiple systems in response to those interpretations. The Anthropic findings offer partial support for the first function, though the consistent, discrete emotional representations identified in Claude sit uneasily with affective neuroscience findings that human emotion is characterized by variable rather than uniform neural signatures. On the second function, the evidence is mixed: Claude's representations modulate output without producing the dynamic reorganization of attention, decision speed, and motivational state that defines emotion in biological systems. We close by proposing what it would take for an LLM to have emotions.

</details>


### [924] [OpTI-Mouse: Optimization for Targeted Temporal Interference Stimulation in the Mouse Brain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15192)
*Jingsheng Tang,Zhengkang Zhou,Yingyue Xin,Zihan Ning,Pengfei Wei,Mo Wang,Quanying Liu*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper develops a computational tool combining mouse head modeling and optimization algorithms to improve Temporal Interference (TI) stimulation strategies targeting deep brain regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the limited availability of precise optimization tools for deep brain targeting using TI stimulation in mouse models.

Method: A computational optimization tool was created to model mouse head structures and apply optimization algorithms for predefined target areas. The performance was validated on simulated and phantom models.

Result: The optimized strategy achieved a 7-fold increase in intensity for the CA3-CA1 region and better spatial confinement for the Dentate Gyrus compared to baseline methods, as well as validation using a standardized phantom model.

Conclusion: The framework provides a robust and precise method to enhance the effectiveness and reproducibility of TI stimulation in preclinical studies.

Abstract: Temporal Interference (TI) stimulation enables deep brain targeting, yet precise optimization tools for mouse models remain limited. We developed a computational optimization tool integrating mouse head modeling with the optimization algorithm to optimize stimulation strategies for predefined target regions. By balancing target intensity and spatial focality, the optimized strategy significantly outperformed empirical baselines. For the CA3-CA1 target, it achieved a 7-fold intensity increase (10.29 vs. 2.89 V/m) under iso-focality conditions. Conversely, for the Dentate Gyrus, it improved spatial confinement ($r_{0.5}$ reduced from 3.99 to 3.54 mm) while maintaining comparable intensity. Cross-model validation on a standardized Sim4Life phantom further confirmed the framework's robustness. This approach offers a powerful tool for enhancing the precision and reproducibility of preclinical TI stimulation studies.

</details>


### [925] [Intrinsic Computational Functionalism and Simulated Consciousness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15348)
*Ryota Kanai,Shuqin Ma*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper addresses objections to simulated consciousness via a framework called Intrinsic Causal-Computational Realization (ICCR), emphasizing internal mechanisms and causal organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To counter objections to artificial consciousness by focusing on the computational and causal structures a system inherently realizes.

Method: Develops a mechanism-enriched canonical model to define Intrinsic Causal-Computational Realization (ICCR), ensuring physical and intrinsic causal-structural consistency.

Result: Proposes that systems meeting the ICCR criteria realize the same consciousness-relevant properties, regardless of being biological, artificial, or simulated.

Conclusion: Rejecting simulated consciousness requires identifying a causal-computational structure missing in these systems.

Abstract: A common objection to artificial or simulated consciousness is that a simulated brain is no more conscious than simulated water is wet. We address this from the perspective of Intrinsic Computational Functionalism (ICF): if consciousness is computationally constituted, it depends not on externally imposed descriptions but on the computational structures a system physically realizes in virtue of its own causal-dynamical organization. In previous work we developed Canonical Functionalism as a mathematically precise special case of this anti-interpretivist program, identifying functional states by their complete future input-output roles under a fixed interface. Here we argue that this input-output construction, though important, is incomplete: as a behavioral boundary case of ICF, it makes lookup tables and unfolded systems that preserve the same boundary behavior canonically equivalent. A consciousness-relevant canonical representation must instead include internal mechanisms, interventions, and joint readouts belonging to the relevant intrinsic organization. We therefore define a mechanism-enriched canonical structure and use it to formulate Intrinsic Causal-Computational Realization (ICCR), a realization relation preserving physical implementation, intrinsic state individuation, transition structure, intervention profiles, and the relevant agent-body-world boundary. The central result is conditional: if conscious properties are invariants of intrinsic causal-computational organization, then any system satisfying ICCR realizes the same consciousness-relevant properties, whether biological, artificial, or simulated. We discuss objections including biological naturalism and integrated information theory. We conclude that to deny consciousness to a simulation, one must identify a consciousness-relevant intrinsic causal-computational structure that the simulation fails to realize.

</details>


### [926] [Task-guided cross-subject latent alignment: a multi-encoder-decoder VAE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15989)
*Angeliki Papathanasiou,Jascha Achterberg,Thomas E. Nichols,Rui Ponte Costa*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study presents MED-VAE, a model enabling neural alignment across subjects without shared stimuli, achieving better semantic organization and cross-subject predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional alignment methods requiring shared stimuli, which restrict applications to broader and more naturalistic paradigms.

Method: A Multi-Encoder-Decoder Variational Autoencoder (MED-VAE) is proposed, utilizing a pretrained artificial neural network scaffold to align neural activity across subjects in a shared latent space.

Result: MED-VAE outperforms existing cross-subject alignment methods in semantic organization, robustness to held-out stimuli, and cross-subject image decoding.

Conclusion: MED-VAE successfully provides a framework for identifying generalizable common subspaces, enabling improved cross-subject predictions and downstream applications.

Abstract: Aligning neural activity across subjects offers the promise of discovering shared computational principles and generalizable decoders. However, traditional alignment methods require shared stimuli across subjects, a constraint that limits applicability to naturalistic paradigms with limited or non-overlapping data. We introduce a Multi-Encoder-Decoder Variational Autoencoder (MED-VAE) that achieves cross-subject alignment without shared stimuli by anchoring representations to a common scaffold provided by a pretrained ANN. Using the Natural Scenes Dataset, we show that MED-VAE creates common latent spaces with superior semantic organisation, achieving higher cross-subject alignment than common methods while maintaining robust generalisation to held-out stimuli where traditional methods degrade. Reconstructing from these common spaces back to each subject's original neural space, MED-VAE preserves equal stimulus-driven signal in its cross-subject latent space. Finally, we show that this superior alignment directly enables cross-subject neural prediction, as demonstrated via cross-subject image decoding. In summary, we introduce a framework to identify generalisable common subspaces for cross-subject predictions and downstream tasks, demonstrated here for visual cortex responses to static images.

</details>


### [927] [EEGDash: An open-source platform for machine learning on public neurophysiological data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16041)
*Bruno Aristimunha,Aviv Dotan,Pierre Guetschel,Aman Jaiswal,Gal Ashkenazi,Dung Truong,Kuntal Kokate,Amitrava Majumdar,Oren Shriki,Arnaud Delorme*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: EEG-Dash standardizes access to 791 neurophysiological datasets for easy machine-learning integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simplify the process of utilizing public neurophysiological datasets for machine-learning tasks.

Method: EEG-Dash organizes datasets into importable, compliant, and queryable classes, integrating tools like MNE-Python, Braindecode, and BIDS validator.

Result: The software catalogues 791 datasets and provides features like semantic search, metadata tagging, and format repair.

Conclusion: EEG-Dash enables easier reuse of neurophysiological datasets, fostering benchmarking, model development, and cross-dataset analyses.

Abstract: Public neurophysiological datasets are increasingly accessible but remain hard to reuse: turning one into a trained model still takes thousands of lines of code for download, loading, format repair, windowing, and evaluation, and a dataset that meets metadata standards can still fail to load. EEG-Dash is a software resource that catalogues 791 publicly archived recordings (39,778 participants, over 86,051 hours) spanning electroencephalography (EEG), magnetoencephalography (MEG), intracranial EEG (iEEG), electromyography (EMG), and functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) from the OpenNeuro and NEMAR archives. It exposes each dataset as an importable, queryable class that preserves signal attributes and loads into machine-learning workflows without custom code, delegating signal handling to MNE-Python, windowing to Braindecode, and format compliance to the official Brain Imaging Data Structure (BIDS) validator. A metadata-first registry adds semantic search, a format-repair layer, automatic dataset-level tags drawn from each source publication, and a feature-extraction framework. The catalogue, with per-record loadability and compliance metadata, supports benchmarking, model development, and cross-dataset analysis.

</details>


### [928] [Learning Hybrid Biophysical Neuron Models with Neural ODEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16693)
*Jonas Beck,Michael Deistler,Dóra Viktória Molnár,Jakob H. Macke,Philipp Berens*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hybrid framework that combines neural ODEs with biophysical neuron models to flexibly and interpretable capture unknown neural dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the challenge of bridging gaps between computational neuron models and biological reality due to poorly characterized ion channel kinetics and practical simplifications in modeling.

Method: A hybrid approach is used, embedding neural ordinary differential equations into conductance-based models to parameterize and recover unknown gating dynamics directly from voltage recordings.

Result: The approach successfully fits various ion channel models, recovers gating dynamics, generalizes across stimuli and parameter variations, and reduces computational cost in simplified neuron models.

Conclusion: This framework enables selective replacement of unknown components in conductance-based models with learned neural ODEs, maintaining biological interpretability while improving flexibility and adaptability.

Abstract: Biophysical neuron models link measurements of neural activity to underlying cellular mechanisms. Yet, a central challenge is that the kinetics of many ion channels are poorly characterized, and practical simplifications -- omitting channels or reducing morphological detail -- introduce systematic gaps between model and biology. Bridging these gaps requires approaches that can flexibly discover unmodeled dynamics while preserving mechanistic interpretability. Here, we introduce a hybrid modeling framework that embeds neural ordinary differential equations into conductance-based biophysical models to capture unknown currents or mis-specified channel kinetics. By parameterizing the neural ODE in terms of voltage-dependent steady-state and time-constant functions, we recover interpretable gating dynamics directly from voltage recordings without assuming a functional form. We show that the hybrid model fits the gating kinetics of 2400 ion channel models and recovers unknown gating dynamics from single current-clamp recordings, generalizing to out-of-distribution stimulus regimes under realistic inputs and parameter misspecification. We also use our method to reduce a multicompartment model of a cortical neuron into a single-compartment hybrid model with a learned axial current, yielding up to an order of magnitude lower computational cost. Together, our results establish a plug-and-play framework for selectively replacing unknown components of conductance-based models with neural ODEs while preserving their mechanistic structure.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [929] [Audited Conformal Prediction for Classification under Unknown Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14909)
*Yanfei Zhou,Rizal Fathony,Nam H. Nguyen,Matteo Sesia*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: A method called Audited Conformal Prediction (ACP) is developed to quantify uncertainty in predictions from pretrained classification models under distribution shifts, striking a balance between prediction set size, conditional, and marginal coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of ensuring reliable predictions from pretrained classification models when deployed in new environments where the input distribution differs from the training distribution.

Method: The authors introduce ACP, which uses a small labeled dataset from the target distribution to train an audit model. The audit model predicts where the pretrained legacy model might fail, and its outputs are integrated into a conformal prediction framework using either of two strategies to ensure higher conditional coverage or explicit group-conditional coverage.

Result: Theoretical guarantees are established for the method, and experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate its effectiveness and the trade-offs between prediction set size and enhanced conditional coverage.

Conclusion: ACP offers a robust solution for uncertainty quantification under distribution shifts by combining audit models and conformal prediction, resulting in better conditional performance than existing techniques.

Abstract: We consider the problem of uncertainty quantification for a pretrained classification model deployed under unknown distribution shift. We propose Audited Conformal Prediction (ACP), a method that leverages a small labeled dataset from the target population to train an auxiliary audit model identifying inputs where the legacy model is likely to fail. By integrating the audit model's outputs into the conformal prediction framework, ACP produces prediction sets that guarantee marginal coverage while achieving substantially higher conditional coverage in practice than existing approaches. We develop and analyze two complementary integration strategies -- one targeting marginal coverage with improved conditional performance, the other providing explicit group-conditional coverage guarantees -- and establish theoretical guarantees for both. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate the method and illustrate trade-offs between prediction set size and conditional coverage.

</details>


### [930] [Conformal Candidate Certification for Offline Model-Based Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15217)
*Seungjin Choi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Conformal Candidate Certification (CCC) post-hoc wrapper providing calibrated statistical bounds for candidate solutions in offline model-based optimization (MBO).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for MBO lack statistical guarantees that proposed candidates meet a desired target threshold, especially for out-of-distribution candidates where surrogate rankings are unreliable.

Method: The authors propose CCC, a method leveraging entropy-regularized surrogate maximization and weighted conformal prediction, using the same surrogate to supply importance weights without the need for density-ratio estimation.

Result: In synthetic studies, CCC demonstrated superior certification performance, certifying 16.7% of candidates with strong empirical coverage of 0.990 versus 0.416 for standard conformal prediction under covariate shift.

Conclusion: CCC is an effective post-hoc solution for improving statistical reliability in MBO, addressing reliability gaps in existing methods and enabling better certified candidate selection.

Abstract: Offline model-based optimization (MBO) proposes candidates by optimizing a surrogate trained on a fixed historical dataset. Because candidates are deliberately out-of-distribution, surrogate rankings are least reliable exactly where the optimizer is most aggressive, yet existing methods provide no per-candidate statistical certificate that a design meets a target threshold. We propose \emph{Conformal Candidate Certification} (CCC), a post-hoc wrapper that attaches a calibrated one-sided lower bound to each candidate and advances only those whose bound exceeds the target. We show that entropy-regularized surrogate maximization induces a Gibbs-tilted proposal, so the same surrogate supplies importance weights for weighted conformal prediction without a separate density-ratio estimation step. In a controlled synthetic study, CCC certifies $16.7\%$ of an aggressive proposal pool with empirical coverage 0.990 at nominal 0.90, while standard conformal prediction ignoring the covariate shift collapses to 0.416 coverage.

</details>


### [931] [Finite Resources False Discovery Rate Control in Structured Hypothesis Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15393)
*Binyamin Perets,Shie Mannor*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework to control false discovery rates (FDR) under conditions of finite-data uncertainty and structured hypothesis spaces, introducing two robust decision rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in controlling false discovery rates due to limited null distribution data and structured hypothesis spaces in large-scale hypothesis testing.

Method: Utilizes a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) framework and introduces two decision rules for FDR control: one guaranteeing exact control, and another prioritizing higher statistical power using adapted mirror-statistics.

Result: Achieves robust FDR control that adapts to mis-specified structures and investigates efficient use of null distribution samples under finite-data constraints.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively balances FDR control and statistical power in structured and uncertain hypothesis testing scenarios, with practical strategies for sample allocation.

Abstract: Scientific discovery relies on large-scale hypothesis testing. However, the capacity to identify true discoveries while controlling false discovery faces major challenges: obtaining relevant reference data (the null distribution) is resource-intensive, leaving finite-data uncertainty, and the procedure should account for the inherent structure in the hypothesis space, when such structure exists. Here, we present a framework for controlling the false discovery rate both when each hypothesis is evidenced only by a finite count of null draws, leaving its p-value uncertain, and when the hypothesis space carries arbitrary structure, requiring only that the structure be represented through a suitable reproducing kernel. We present two decision rules that are both robust to structural mis-specification, yet offer a distinct trade-off between exact FDR control and statistical power. The first rule guarantees exact FDR control; the second maximizes power by adapting mirror-statistic control into count space, utilizing an analytical framework to assess FDR control when exact mirror symmetry is relaxed. Furthermore, the tractability gained by the RKHS framework allows us to directly investigate finite-data uncertainties, which we leverage to suggest a policy for the efficient allocation of null distribution samples.

</details>


### [932] [The Reverse Telescoping Coordinate System for Positive Definite Matrices: Geometry, Computation, and Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15442)
*Anindya Bhadra*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel coordinate system to represent symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices using a reverse telescoping map, enabling efficient computations and advanced generative modeling leveraging the matrix structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simplify the representation and manipulation of SPD matrices, which have constraints and structural properties that complicate their handling, especially in generative modeling and other computational tasks.

Method: Introduces a reverse telescoping map, $Θ(x)=\rm{RT}(x)$, where an SPD matrix is parametrized by $x=(v,d,r)$. This representation allows for fast $O(p^2)$ computations in the transformed domain, decouples volume and shape transformations, and offers symbolic representations for both the matrix and its inverse.

Result: Demonstrates the method's advantages in computational efficiency, generative modeling up to $p=200$, and applications like synthetic bimodal targets, brain connectivity networks (fMRI data), and intrinsic diffusion on the SPD manifold.

Conclusion: The proposed coordinate system provides a more efficient and intuitive framework for SPD matrices, revealing the unexpected ease of designing flows for volume-normalized shapes, and showing utility across various data-driven applications.

Abstract: We design a new unconstrained coordinate system where a $p\times p$ symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix $Θ$ is represented by a reverse telescoping map $Θ(x)=\rm{RT}(x)$, with $x=(v,d,r)\in\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}^{(p-1)}\times\mathbb{R}^{p(p-1)/2}$, representing respectively the log volume or log determinant; and the shape, as encoded by log relative diagonal scales and partial covariances among the nodes. This construction results in important properties not available in other charts, e.g., matrix logarithm, such as Jacobian depending on only the log-determinant. A useful feature of our construction is $x$ contains a lossless symbolic representation of both the matrix and its inverse. Many important computations involving a matrix and its inverse can be performed in $O(p^2)$ in the transformed domain, while it is the rendering of results in matrix forms (on demand) that must incur an $O(p^3)$ cost. Moreover, two unit-determinant matrices in the transformed domain can be joined by a straight line with pathwise unit determinant. For generative modeling, this allows designing a split volume-shape flow model trained by conditional flow matching for transporting the shape over the unit-determinant path, with a separate one-dimensional flow for transporting the volume or the determinant. The forbidding SPD constraint, tamed thus into a powerful guiding force, leads to the surprising insight that it is in some sense easier to design a volume-normalized shape flow for SPD compared to the unconstrained $\mathbb{R}^{p\times p}$, with no intrinsic notion of volume to aid normalization, unlike the determinant of SPD matrices. We apply our construction for up to $p=200$ in generative modeling of SPD matrices on a difficult synthetic bimodal target, and in generating brain connectivity networks by models trained on fMRI data; as well as in intrinsic diffusion on the SPD manifold.

</details>


### [933] [Structured Nonparametric Variational Inference for Dependent Latent Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15458)
*Yuda Shao,Zhiling Gu,Shan Yu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Structured Nonparametric Variational Inference (SN-VI), a method for modeling latent variable dependencies using multivariate splines, enhancing posterior approximation beyond mean-field approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional variational inference methods often use the mean-field assumption, which simplifies computations but overlooks intricate dependencies between latent variables, limiting the quality of posterior approximations.

Method: The authors propose SN-VI, leveraging multivariate spline techniques to capture complex dependencies in posterior approximations. It includes an algorithm to automatically identify dependence structures among latent variables without manual input.

Result: Simulation studies confirm that SN-VI effectively approximates posterior distributions with bounded support and complex dependencies. It also improves generative model performance and uncovers coupled signals in structured data applications like computer vision and spatial transcriptomics.

Conclusion: SN-VI advances variational inference by preserving latent variable dependencies, offering a more flexible and accurate framework for posterior approximation in high-dimensional datasets.

Abstract: Variational inference (VI) is a core engine of modern AI, enabling scalable approximate Bayesian learning and uncertainty-aware training of large probabilistic and generative models. In this paper, we propose Structured Nonparametric Variational Inference (SN-VI), a novel framework for modeling complex dependencies among latent variables in posterior approximation, leveraging multivariate spline techniques. Unlike traditional methods that rely on the mean-field assumption, SN-VI preserves intricate latent variable dependencies, providing a flexible and accurate approximation of posteriors with arbitrary shapes. We establish rigorous theoretical guarantees, including the derivation of the lower bound for the variational objective and proof of asymptotic consistency in posterior estimation. To facilitate practical implementation, we develop an algorithm that automatically identifies dependent latent variables and their underlying dependence structure, without requiring manual specification. Simulation studies validate the effectiveness of SN-VI in approximating posterior distributions with bounded support and complex dependencies. The proposed method has been successfully applied to high-dimensional structured data, including computer vision datasets and spatial transcriptomics. In these applications, SN-VI demonstrates improved generative model performance and effectively uncovers coupled biological signals through the learned dependency structure.

</details>


### [934] [Ricci-Filtration: Boosting Retrieval-Augmented Generation Reranker to Query-Answer Tasks by Discrete Ricci Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15482)
*Tian Qin,Wei-Min Huang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Ricci-Filtration, a novel geometry-based reranker for improving query-document retrieval by leveraging discrete Ricci flow and curvature concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the accuracy and robustness of query-document reranking by using geometric insights into network structures, specifically discrete Ricci curvature.

Method: The method involves using discrete Ricci flow to model query-chunk relations as a network, filter chunks based on curvature, and rerank them to enhance retrieval. Theoretical results on curvature's impact support these steps.

Result: Ricci-Filtration demonstrated superior performance over baseline methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores across multiple experiments. It also exhibited robustness under various configurations in ablation studies.

Conclusion: The study concludes that Ricci-Filtration effectively uses geometric features like Ricci curvature to filter and rerank document chunks, showing strong performance and adaptability.

Abstract: Ricci flow is a curvature-guided diffusion process that deforms space by shrinking regions of high positive curvature and expanding those with negative curvature. Similarly, discrete Ricci flow on weighted graphs modifies edge weights by shrinking edges with positive Ricci curvature and stretching those with negative Ricci curvature, effectively increasing the separation between clusters. Inspired by these two cornerstone works, we propose a geometry-based RAG reranker enhancement procedure called Ricci-Filtration. By modeling the input query and initial retrieved chunks as a network, where the input query and chunks serve as nodes and embedding-based pairwise relations define an initial graph, Ricci-Filtration leverages discrete curvature and Ricci flow to evaluate the structural importance of each chunk with respect to the user query. The system first filters the initial chunks based on their geometric curvature relative to the query; then, a reranker processes the remaining chunks to enhance generative performance. We theoretically prove that normalized discrete Ricci flow can detect community structures by identifying distinct asymptotic behaviors in edge weights. This supports the removal of ``noisy'' document chunks characterized by large weights and negative Ricci curvature relative to the query node. Extensive experiments confirm that Ricci-Filtration outperforms several baseline reranking methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores. Furthermore, ablation studies demonstrate that the Ricci-Filtration generally outperforms the baseline under various settings, highlighting the framework's robustness across different architectures.

</details>


### [935] [Phase Transition in Convex Relaxations for Graph Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15581)
*Laurent Massoulié,Sushil Mahavir Varma,Louis Vassaux,Irène Waldspurger*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper analyzes solutions for the graph alignment problem for correlated GOE matrices, focusing on computational relaxations for efficient hidden vertex permutation recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks efficient solutions to the intractable graph alignment problem involving correlated symmetric Gaussian matrices.

Method: The study explores convex relaxations involving minimization over doubly stochastic matrices and the unit hypercube, characterizing the accuracy of alignments relative to correlation levels.

Result: Results demonstrate recovery accuracy of the relaxed solutions depends critically on the correlation parameter, tightening prior bounds.

Conclusion: Convex relaxations provide near-optimal recovery results for small correlation parameters, effectively advancing relaxed solution analysis for graph alignment.

Abstract: We study the graph alignment problem for correlated Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE) matrices, where the goal is to recover a hidden vertex permutation given two correlated symmetric Gaussian matrices $(A, B)$ with correlation $1/\sqrt{1+σ^2}$. While the maximum likelihood estimator is information-theoretically optimal, its computation, which reduces to a quadratic assignment problem, is intractable. Motivated by this, we analyze convex relaxations based on minimizing $\|AX - XB\|_F$ over the set of doubly stochastic matrices and the unit hypercube. We show that when the correlation parameter satisfies $σ= o(n^{-1/2}/\log^4 n)$, the solution of either relaxation $(X^\star)$ concentrates around the ground-truth permutation matrix $(Π^\star)$, i.e., $\|X^\star-Π^\star\|_F^2 = o(n)$, implying recovery of all but a vanishing fraction of vertices after simple post-processing. Combined with existing lower bounds, our results precisely characterize that $\|X^\star-Π^\star\|_F^2$ transitions from $o(n)$ for $σ= \tilde{o}(n^{-1/2})$ to $Ω(n)$ for $σ= \tildeΩ(n^{-1/2})$. In doing so, our analysis significantly tightens prior results and extends them beyond doubly stochastic relaxations.

</details>


### [936] [Information Gap and Feasibility-Aware Inference in Binomial Logistic Mixtures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15665)
*Yuta Hayashida,Shonosuke Sugasawa*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper explores the gap between mixture detection and label recovery in binomial logistic mixtures, proposes new methods to bridge this gap, and validates the findings with numerical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to investigate why standard likelihood-based criteria, like BIC, detect mixture components but fail to ensure recoverable labels in binomial logistic mixtures, addressing an intrinsic information gap.

Method: Proposed methods include a recoverability-aware Bayesian Information Criterion with a posterior-entropy penalty and an entropy-regularized estimator to improve component selection and label probability calibration.

Result: The paper confirms the theoretical gap between mixture detection and label recovery, and demonstrates through numerical experiments that the proposed methods mitigate misleading component selection and enhance label probability calibration.

Conclusion: There exists an intrinsic detectable-but-unrecoverable regime in binomial logistic mixtures. The proposed methods successfully address this issue by improving label recoverability without misleading detections.

Abstract: This paper studies the information gap between mixture detection and label recovery in binomial logistic mixtures. Standard likelihood-based criteria such as the Bayesian information criterion (BIC) can detect the presence of two components, but this does not guarantee that the corresponding labels are recoverable. We show that this gap is intrinsic to binomial logistic mixtures with a fixed number of trials: observed-data evidence for mixture structure and per-observation information for label recovery have different local orders in the component separation, and only the former accumulates with the sample size. As a result, there exists a detectable-but-unrecoverable regime in which BIC selects two components while the posterior labels remain essentially uninformative. To address this issue, we propose two feasibility-aware inference procedures: a recoverability-aware BIC with a posterior-entropy penalty and an entropy-regularized estimator that mitigates the tendency of the maximum likelihood estimator to produce overly separated components and overly concentrated posterior responsibilities. Numerical experiments confirm the predicted gap and demonstrate that the proposed methods avoid misleading component selections and improve the calibration of posterior label probabilities.

</details>


### [937] [Stochastic trace estimation with tensor train random vectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15679)
*Zvonimir Bujanović,Daniel Kressner,Hrvoje Olić*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores Gaussian random tensor train (TT) vectors as a structured alternative for stochastic trace estimation, achieving similar guarantees as methods using unstructured Gaussian vectors while avoiding storage and computational challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the challenges in using unstructured Gaussian or Rademacher test vectors for stochastic trace estimation in tensor-structured settings, due to storage and computational costs.

Method: The authors analyze Gaussian random TT vectors and their ability to recover dimension-independent guarantees for the Girard-Hutchinson estimator, using a median-of-means variant with appropriate TT ranks. They also study these vectors for subspace injection in sketches and their integration within the Nyström++ framework.

Result: They demonstrate that with a tensor train rank ≥ d−1, Gaussian random TT vectors achieve accuracy and failure probability guarantees comparable to unstructured Gaussian methods. They also establish sufficient conditions for achieving desired sample complexities in specific applications.

Conclusion: Gaussian random TT vectors represent a practical alternative in high-dimensional tensor settings, offering a balance between computational feasibility and effective trace estimation. However, their effectiveness depends on proper rank selection and additional conditions in certain applications.

Abstract: Stochastic trace estimation is a standard tool for approximating the trace of a large-scale matrix available only through matrix-vector products. However, in tensor-structured settings, unstructured Gaussian or Rademacher test vectors may be prohibitively expensive to store and compute with, while cheaper rank-one tensor-product vectors can require sample complexities that grow exponentially with the tensor order. This work studies Gaussian random tensor train vectors as a structured alternative for stochastic trace estimation. We show that, with a suitable choice of the tensor train rank, random tensor train vectors recover dimension-independent guarantees for the Girard--Hutchinson estimator. In particular, a median-of-means variant with tensor train rank $r \geq d-1$ achieves the same dependence on the accuracy $\varepsilon$ and failure probability $δ$ as the classical estimator based on unstructured Gaussian vectors. We further prove an oblivious subspace injection result for sketches formed from independent Gaussian random tensor train vectors: tensor train rank $r\geq d-1$ and $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-2}(k+\log(1/δ)))$ samples suffice for a $k$-dimensional target subspace. Finally, we investigate the use of such sketches within the Nyström++ framework. We show that the resulting estimator can achieve the desired $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-1})$ sample complexity under an additional spectral-tail condition. These results provide clarififcation on both the potential and the limitations of random tensor train vectors in stochastic trace estimation.

</details>


### [938] [Spectral Adaptive Conformal Prediction for Structured Non-Exchangeable Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15950)
*Jeffery Opoku,David Banahene*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method called spectral adaptive conformal prediction, which enhances conformal prediction in time-indexed datasets with structured dependence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of traditional conformal prediction when applied to non-exchangeable, time-indexed datasets with structures like seasons, recurring regimes, and varying uncertainty over time.

Method: The proposed method uses spectral weights to identify locally relevant data points for conformal quantiles and dynamically adjusts the miscoverage level to maintain calibration over time. Both fixed and adaptive approaches are studied and validated.

Result: The method demonstrates improved performance in simulations with recurring and changing patterns and provided insights on real U.S. datasets. However, it emphasizes the need for monitoring via effective sample size diagnostics.

Conclusion: Spectral adaptive conformal prediction effectively combines spectral weighting and adaptive updates to enhance coverage in non-exchangeable datasets, demonstrating both theoretical and empirical advantages over traditional methods.

Abstract: Conformal prediction gives prediction intervals with finite-sample coverage when the data are exchangeable. Many time-indexed datasets are not exchangeable. They have seasons, recurring regimes, changing frequencies, or other forms of structured dependence. This paper studies a simple way to use that structure. We propose spectral adaptive conformal prediction, a method that forms weighted conformal quantiles using local spectral similarity and then updates the target miscoverage level online. The spectral weights choose calibration residuals that look relevant to the current test point. The adaptive update corrects the long-run miss rate when uncertainty changes over time. We give an approximate coverage result for the fixed spectral weighted quantile and a deterministic long-run calibration result for the adaptive update. Simulations with recurring regimes and slowly changing frequencies, together with three U.S. real-data examples, show that the hybrid method can improve on fixed spectral weighting, while also showing that spectral weighting must be monitored through effective sample size diagnostics.

</details>


### [939] [PromptShift-CRC: Drift-Aware Conformal Risk Control for Foundation Models Under Prompt and Domain Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15964)
*Jeffery Opoku,David Banahene*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces PromptShift CRC, a drift-aware method to ensure consistent output accuracy for foundation models under changing prompts and domains. It adapts and updates calibration dynamically and performs better than static methods in handling prompt-domain shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models often face rapidly changing prompts and domains, making static calibration unreliable. The authors aim to develop an adaptable framework to handle error control under such shifts.

Method: They propose PromptShift CRC, which dynamically adapts weights for calibration examples, measures prompt drift, and adjusts risk levels online. The method also includes diagnostics for monitoring drift and calibration effectiveness.

Result: In synthetic and benchmark tests, PromptShift CRC outperformed static conformal risk control and achieved better error coverage in tasks like question answering, toxicity analysis, summarization, and hallucination detection.

Conclusion: PromptShift CRC improves robustness in foundation models under prompt-domain shifts by adaptively managing calibration and error control, offering a more reliable approach than static calibration. It extends the applicability of conformal methods to dynamic settings.

Abstract: Foundation models are now used in settings where the prompts they receive can change quickly. Users change, topics change, policies change, and the model may suddenly face a kind of request that was rare in the calibration data. This makes fixed calibration risky. Conformal prediction and conformal risk control give model-agnostic ways to control error, but they work best when the calibration data still look like the future data. This paper develops PromptShift CRC, a drift-aware conformal risk control method for foundation-model outputs under prompt and domain shift. The method embeds prompts and responses, measures how far the current prompt stream has moved from the calibration pool, gives more weight to relevant or recent calibration examples, and updates the risk level online after observed violations. It reports three practical diagnostics: realized risk error, prompt drift, and effective calibration size. We give conditions under which the method controls risk up to terms for distribution mismatch and weighted quantile uncertainty. In a synthetic prompt-shift benchmark, static conformal risk control fails sharply after drift, while PromptShift-CRC gives the best coverage among the adaptive baselines considered. We then evaluate the same calibration layer on public benchmark derived streams for question answering, toxicity, summarization factuality, and long-context hallucination risk

</details>


### [940] [Closing the Approximation Gap in Simulation-free Latent SDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16138)
*Henry D. Smith,Brian L. Trippe,Scott W. Linderman*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Helmholtz-SDE, a new algorithm that improves simulation-free variational inference for latent SDEs with speed and fidelity advantages over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent stochastic differential equations (SDEs) are essential in modeling unobserved systems from noisy data in fields like neuroscience and physics. Existing simulation-free VI methods for inferring latent SDEs face limitations in posterior flexibility and accuracy.

Method: The authors propose a novel VI algorithm, Helmholtz-SDE, that unlike previous simulation-free methods, optimizes over path laws compatible with prescribed marginals, enhancing flexibility and inference quality.

Result: Helmholtz-SDE improves posterior inference and parameter learning, especially under high posterior uncertainty, and matches the results of traditional simulation-based methods at a reduced computational cost.

Conclusion: Helmholtz-SDE delivers better accuracy and efficiency in recovering latent dynamics, bridging the gap between simulation-based and simulation-free methods for latent SDE modeling.

Abstract: Recovering dynamical systems from noisy observations is a recurring challenge across scientific domains, including neuroscience and physics. Latent stochastic differential equations (SDEs) address this by modeling the system as an unobserved state that evolves according to a learnable SDE and generates the observations. Variational inference (VI) provides a tractable objective for fitting latent SDEs. Traditional VI algorithms evaluate this objective by numerical simulation over a time discretization, trading fidelity for computational cost. A recent class of algorithms, simulation-free VI, sidesteps this tradeoff by parameterizing the posterior through its instantaneous marginals rather than its drift. In this work, we show that the efficiency of existing simulation-free VI algorithms comes at a price: their parameterizations restrict the approximate posterior to a subset of the SDEs available to simulation-based methods, degrading posterior inference and parameter learning. We propose Helmholtz-SDE, a simulation-free VI algorithm that closes this gap by optimizing over path laws compatible with a prescribed collection of marginals. Helmholtz-SDE recovers dynamics more faithfully than prior simulation-free methods, with the largest gains under high posterior uncertainty. It further matches the performance of simulation-based VI at a fraction of the runtime.

</details>


### [941] [Generative Modeling on Metric Graphs via Neural Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16273)
*Alessandro Micheli,Yueqi Cao,Anthea Monod,Samir Bhatt*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a deep generative model for probability distributions on compact metric graphs, embedding the graph, solving a transport problem, and projecting samples back onto the graph, demonstrated on various datasets including urban mobility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to create a robust framework for deep generative modeling on compact metric graphs, addressing challenges in representing and transporting probability measures in these complex geometries.

Method: The approach involves embedding the graph into a smooth space, solving an entropic Kantorovich problem with neural network parameterization, and projecting samples back to the graph using two types of embedded geometries.

Result: The proposed method empirically performs better than baseline heuristic graph optimal transport methods on diverse graph structures and scales efficiently to handle large datasets like urban mobility data.

Conclusion: This methodology establishes a scalable, accurate, and elegant solution to generative modeling on metric graphs, with applications demonstrated in both synthetic and real-world data scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce, to our knowledge, the first deep generative modeling framework for probability distributions continuously supported on compact metric graphs. Given source and target measures on a metric graph, our method embeds the graph into a smooth ambient space, solves an entropic Kantorovich problem via a neural semidual parameterization, and projects generated samples back onto the original graph. We study two embedded geometries: an extrinsic Euclidean realization and the intrinsic tropical Abel--Jacobi embedding into the Jacobian torus. In both cases, the resulting generator is graph-supported by construction. We prove that, in the joint limit of increasing neural expressivity, the learned generator converges weakly to a valid transport coupling between the original graph measures. Empirically, across a range of geometrically distinct graphs, our method matches or improves upon heuristic transport baselines based on discrete graph OT, while scaling more favorably. Finally, we demonstrate scalability on real-world urban mobility data by training our model on one million Uber pickup locations in Manhattan, New York City.

</details>


### [942] [Diffusion Flow Matching: Dimension-Improved KL Bounds and Wasserstein Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16610)
*Marta Gentiloni Silveri,Giovanni Conforti,Alain Durmus*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper refines and extends theoretical convergence guarantees for Diffusion Flow Matching (DFM), specifically for the discretization error. It derives improved KL divergence and 2-Wasserstein distance bounds under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the partially understood theoretical convergence properties of DFMs, focusing on providing stronger guarantees and dimensional scalability for Brownian motion-based DFM models.

Method: The analysis uses KL divergence and 2-Wasserstein distance metrics to study convergence under finite-moment conditions, mild score integrability, and log-concavity assumptions. It develops novel dimensional dependency bounds for convergence.

Result: The study achieves state-of-the-art dimensional scaling for KL convergence bounds under minimal conditions, extending a similar consistency to 2-Wasserstein distances with additional assumptions.

Conclusion: This work establishes enhanced theoretical guarantees for DFMs, addressing both KL divergence and 2-Wasserstein distances, making significant advancements in understanding and scaling the convergence of Brownian motion-based generative models.

Abstract: Diffusion Flow Matching (DFM) has recently emerged as a versatile framework for generative modeling, yet its theoretical convergence properties remain only partially understood. In this work, we provide refined and novel convergence guarantees for Brownian motion based DFMs, focusing on the discretization error. Our analysis is conducted under the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence and the 2-Wasserstein distance. Under finite-moment conditions and a mild score integrability assumption, we derive KL convergence bounds with improved dimensional dependence compared to prior work, achieving, up to our knowledge, state-of-the-art scaling under minimal conditions. We further extend the analysis to the 2-Wasserstein distance: under an additional first-order score integrability assumption and a weak log-concavity condition, we obtain convergence guarantees with dimensional dependence consistent with the KL case.

</details>


### [943] [Attention is Just Another Name for Coupling?: A Fast-Slow ODE Perspective on Hierarchical Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16730)
*Zhengyuan Gao*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a neural network architecture inspired by fast-slow dynamics in singularly perturbed ODEs, employing a slow subsystem coupled with fast self-attention, using a zero-initialized gate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to investigate whether a second-time scale coupling, operating slower than typical causal self-attention, can complement the token-level fast path dynamics in neural networks.

Method: The method involves framing fast-slow dynamics using singularly perturbed ODEs and instantiating it as a neural network with fast causal self-attention and a separate slow subsystem using downsampled tokens and a zero-initialized gate.

Result: At 500k tokens, the slow coupling mechanism showed neutral contribution, with performance comparable to a dense attention baseline, indicating the primary value lies in the proposed mapping framework itself.

Conclusion: The primary contribution of the paper is introducing a structured coupling framework inspired by dynamical systems, rather than aiming for immediate performance benefits.

Abstract: Causal self-attention is a coupling mechanism: each token's hidden state is updated by a learned mixture of preceding tokens at the same timescale. This paper asks whether a second, temporally slower coupling-a slow sub-system operating on a temporally-downsampled view of the sequence and fed back into the fast path through a zero-initialised gate-complements it. The question is framed in the language of singularly perturbed ordinary differential equations (ODEs), where the fast variable $x$ evolves at the token rate, the slow variable $y$ evolves at one update per $P$ tokens, and the timescale ratio $\varepsilon = 1/P$ is enforced structurally by causal block-mean pooling.
  The paper instantiates the fast-slow ODE formalism as a concrete neural network: a fast path of standard causal attention over $T$ tokens, a slow path of full attention over $T/P$ pooled tokens ($P^2 \times$ cheaper per layer), and a zero-initialised additive gate. In addition, under a linear-generator assumption on the fast dynamics, we prove that the equilibrium manifold $x = φ(y)$ is exactly the master-equation (ME) stationary distribution $p_{\mathrm{st}}(y)$; in that regime a learned MLP $φ_θ(y)$ is a variational approximation of it (the trained block is not a generator, so this identity is the structured limit, not a claim about the network as trained). Empirically, at $500$k tokens the coupling is neutral -- the gate stays closed and the coupled and frozen ablations are within run-to-run noise -- at a wall-clock cost comparable to a dense baseline. The contribution is the precise, gap-marked mapping itself, not a performance gain.

</details>


### [944] [A nonparametric two-sample test using a parametric integral probability metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16941)
*Yuha Park,Yongdai Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new nonparametric two-sample testing procedure, PReLU-TST, based on a novel metric called PReLU-IPM, showing theoretical guarantees and superior performance in various scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the fundamental problem of determining whether two independent samples are drawn from the same distribution, without relying on parametric assumptions.

Method: The authors propose a two-sample test statistic (PReLU-IPM) using a specially designed parametric discriminator class with a single neural network node, and establish its theoretical properties under regularity conditions.

Result: Empirical experiments on simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed PReLU-TST test statistic outperforms or performs comparably to existing methods in terms of power for finite sample sizes.

Conclusion: PReLU-TST is a robust and effective two-sample testing method with solid theoretical guarantees and practical advantages in various scenarios.

Abstract: Detecting distributional differences between two independent samples is a fundamental problem in statistics and machine learning. Nonparametric two-sample testing provides a principled framework for determining whether two samples are drawn from the same underlying distribution, without assuming any specific parametric form for the distribution. In this study, we propose a new two-sample test statistic based on a newly introduced integral probability metric (IPM), using a specially designed parametric discriminator class with a single node of a neural network. We show that the resulting test statistic, called PReLU-IPM, is nonparametric and establish theoretical guarantees for the associated two-sample testing procedure, PReLU-TST, including its consistency and asymptotical equivalence to nonparametric IPM-based tests under regularity conditions. By analyzing multiple simulated and real benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that PReLU-TST achieves higher power across a range of alternatives or performs comparably to its competitors, for finite samples.

</details>


### [945] [Sobolev Approximation by Fixed-Size Neural Networks with Arbitrary Accuracy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16975)
*Baicheng Li,Haizhao Yang,Shijun Zhang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper develops new activation functions to enable fixed-size neural networks to achieve arbitrary-accuracy Sobolev norm approximations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches often require large, complex neural networks to achieve high accuracy in approximating functions in Sobolev spaces, creating a need for compact and efficient solutions.

Method: The authors introduce and analyze new activation functions (EUAF, DUAF, and sigmoidal DUAF variants), with explicit width and depth bounds, to achieve arbitrary-accuracy approximations in specified Sobolev norms.

Result: They prove that fixed-size networks using these activations can approximate functions in $W^{s, \infty}$ spaces to arbitrary accuracy in $W^{s-1,\infty}$ norms.

Conclusion: The proposed activation functions enable efficient and elementary fixed-size neural network architectures to approximate Sobolev space functions with high precision, expanding the theoretical capabilities of compact networks.

Abstract: In this work, we investigate new activation functions for achieving arbitrary-accuracy Sobolev approximation by fixed-size neural networks. We first show that any function in $W^{2,\infty}((a,b)^d)$ can be approximated with arbitrary accuracy, measured in the $W^{1,\infty}$-norm, by a fixed-size neural network using the Elementary Universal Activation Function ($\mathrm{EUAF}$). To extend this result to $W^{s,\infty}((a,b)^d)$ for $s\in\mathbb{N}$, we introduce a smooth activation $\mathrm{DUAF}_{\infty}$ from the family of Differentiable Universal Activation Functions ($\mathrm{DUAF}_n$). We prove that any function in $W^{s,\infty}((a,b)^d)$ can be approximated with arbitrary accuracy in the $W^{s-1,\infty}$-norm by a fixed-size $\mathrm{DUAF}_{\infty}$-activated network. We further construct sigmoidal variants $\widetilde{\mathrm{DUAF}}_n$ and show that, for every $1\leq s\leq n$, fixed-size $\widetilde{\mathrm{DUAF}}_n$-activated networks still approximate any $f\in W^{s,\infty}((a,b)^d)$ with arbitrary accuracy in the $W^{s-1,\infty}$-norm. In all these results, the width and depth bounds are computed explicitly, and the proposed activations are elementary.

</details>


### [946] [Dynestyx: A Probabilistic Programming Library for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16985)
*Daniel Waxman,Dmitry Batenkov,John Feser,Andy Zane,Eli Bingham,Youssef Marzouk,Matthew E. Levine*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents dynestyx, a probabilistic programming library designed to effectively handle state-space models (SSMs) with state-of-the-art methods for both state and parameter estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in incorporating dynamical systems into probabilistic programming languages (PPLs), facilitating accessibility to advanced Bayesian treatment methods for practitioners.

Method: The authors developed dynestyx, a library offering a unified interface that supports arbitrary priors, mixed-effect data inference, and principled uncertainty quantification for discrete-time or continuous-time SSMs.

Result: dynestyx simplifies Bayesian inference for dynamical systems and incorporates state-of-the-art methods for estimating states and parameters with uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: dynestyx bridges the gap in existing probabilistic programming languages by providing easy and robust tools for handling complex state-space models.

Abstract: State-space models (SSMs) are the standard formalism for Bayesian treatment of dynamical systems, with natural applications in statistics, signal processing, and machine learning. Despite their importance in both theory and application, dynamical systems have proven difficult to incorporate in modern probabilistic programming languages (PPLs), making state-of-the-art methods less accessible to practitioners and introducing friction in following the "Bayesian workflow." We introduce dynestyx, a probabilistic programming library with first-class support for SSMs, including state-of-the-art methods in the estimation of both states and parameters. Through a single, unified interface, users may specify arbitrary priors for discrete-time or continuous-time dynamical systems, perform inference over mixed-effect data, and make state and parameter estimates with principled uncertainty quantification.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [947] [Provenance-Enhanced Statements in Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15246)
*Fabio Vitali,Valentina Pasqual*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: This paper introduces the DEC framework to integrate provenance, epistemic stance, and reasoning into RDF datasets while preserving consistency across disagreements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current provenance models, which fail to specify how provenance relates to reasoning, factual commitment, and inconsistencies in knowledge graphs.

Method: The authors developed the DEC framework, integrating cognitive modal logics (e.g., doxastic, epistemic), grouping provenance-homogeneous statements into cognitive worlds, and formalizing this for RDF datasets.

Result: DEC enables principled reasoning without collapsing disagreements into inconsistencies, supporting controlled factualization and detecting disagreements via an implemented prototype on a Fuseki dataset module.

Conclusion: The DEC framework enriches provenance handling and reasoning in RDF datasets by introducing cognitive worlds and maintaining consistency, thus enhancing the Semantic Web's treatment of attributed claims and disagreements.

Abstract: Provenance-enhanced statements of the form "according to $X$, $\varphi$" are pervasive in contemporary knowledge graphs, especially in domains where graph content primarily represents claims, interpretations, and hypotheses (\emph{capta}) rather than observer-independent facts (\emph{data}). Current provenance models can record who asserted what, but they typically treat provenance as semantically neutral, leaving underspecified how attributed claims relate to factual commitment, to one another, and to reasoning.
  In this paper we introduce DEC, a framework that interprets provenance predicates as indicators of epistemic stance and groups provenance-homogeneous sets of statements into \emph{cognitive worlds}. Drawing on cognitive modal logics (doxastic, epistemic, and conjectural), DEC characterizes locality, rationality, and controlled permeation between cognitive worlds and a distinguished factual core ("reality"), thereby enabling principled reasoning over attributed content without collapsing disagreements into inconsistencies.
  We formalize a DEC interpretation for RDF datasets that is conservative over RDF~1.2 semantics, clarify the role of intensionality and identity (including the Superman paradox), and illustrate the approach on common Semantic Web representations (named graphs, quoted triples/RDF-star, and reification). Finally, we describe our prototype DEC reasoner implemented as a Fuseki dataset module, supporting controlled factualisation and explicit detection of disagreements and delusions.

</details>


### [948] [The algebra of Krom logic programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15719)
*Christian Antić*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper explores the algebraic structure of Krom logic programs by establishing their monoid structure and extending to advanced algebraic constructs, connecting them with automata and graph theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance the understanding of Krom logic programs through algebraic theory, providing connections with automata theory and graph theory.

Method: The paper develops a monoid structure through sequential composition of Krom programs and extends the framework to algebraic constructs like seminearrings and quemirings. It also employs graph-theoretic and automata-theoretic characterizations.

Result: The authors identified generating sets, canonical decompositions, and the Kleene star in graph-theoretic terms. They also established relationships between finite Krom monoids, transformation monoids, and finite-state automata.

Conclusion: The work bridges the domains of logic programming, algebraic automata theory, and algebraic graph theory, offering a richer understanding of Krom logic programs and their applications.

Abstract: This paper investigates the algebraic structure of Krom logic programs, consisting only of facts and rules with at most one body atom. We show that sequential composition endows the class of Krom programs with a natural monoid structure and that this structure admits rich algebraic extensions to Krom seminearrings, Krom quemirings, Krom-Conway seminearrings, and Krom-Conway omegaseminearrings. Furthermore, we establish explicit generating sets and canonical decompositions, study the associated ${}^ω$-operator, characterize the Kleene star in graph-theoretic terms, and relate finite Krom monoids to transformation monoids and finite-state automata. These results provide new connections between logic programming, algebraic automata theory, and algebraic graph theory.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [949] [Graphical conditional generative modeling for digital twin modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16219)
*Zongren Zou,Théo Bourdais,Ricardo Baptista,Houman Owhadi*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper presents a data-driven framework for building simple yet effective stochastic surrogate models for complex systems. This is achieved by identifying significant input variables that influence the full conditional distribution of target quantities, not just the conditional mean.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges with the increasing complexity in digital twin models caused by countless variables, data streams, and time scales. It aims to discover parsimonious surrogate models that remain interpretable while retaining performance in predicting relevant quantities of interest.

Method: The approach combines conditional generative modeling with Gaussian-process-based analysis of variance. It iteratively prunes non-influential variables and identifies the most significant ones by focusing on the full conditional distribution's variability and structure.

Result: The method successfully discovers essential input variables while maintaining interpretability and Markovian dynamics in control settings. The performance of resulting surrogate models is comparable to those trained on full variable sets across diverse applications.

Conclusion: Building parsimonious surrogate models using this framework addresses the fidelity and complexity trade-offs in complex stochastic systems. It improves interpretability and reduces computational burden without sacrificing model performance.

Abstract: Digital twin modeling, including control and data assimilation under model uncertainty, often faces an open-ended fidelity problem: adding variables, data streams, and time scales can indefinitely increase model complexity, ultimately producing systems that are difficult to maintain, validate, interpret, and use for stress or safety testing. As an alternative, one can seek parsimonious stochastic surrogate models built only on the variables needed to describe the relevant quantities of interest. We introduce a framework for discovering such variables from observational data by identifying which candidate inputs influence the full conditional law of a target quantity, rather than only its conditional mean. This distinction is essential in stochastic, coarse-grained, or partially observed systems, where dependencies may appear through changes in variability, tail behavior, multimodality, or uncertainty rather than through deterministic functional relationships. The framework couples conditional generative modeling, which learns the conditional distribution of the target given candidate inputs, with Gaussian-process-based analysis of variance (through kernel mode decomposition), which enables iterative pruning of non-influential inputs and interpretable structure discovery. In control settings, the resulting surrogate can be interpreted as a learned Markov decision process: the method identifies not only a transition model, but also the state, action, and memory variables needed to make the learned dynamics effectively Markovian. Across examples involving stochastic dynamical systems, missing variables, PDE control, reinforcement learning, and economic data, the discovered structures yield interpretable stochastic surrogates whose downstream performance is comparable to models trained on the full variable set.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [950] [Integrating Multi-Label Classification and Generative AI for Scalable Analysis of User Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.23018)
*Sandra Loop,Erik Bertram,Sebastian Juhl,Martin Schrepp*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper discusses methods for efficiently analyzing large-scale user feedback in UX evaluation, using supervised machine learning for topic labeling and generative AI for summarization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of processing and interpreting extensive user feedback in UX evaluations for ensuring software quality and product success.

Method: The authors employed supervised machine learning to assign topic labels to user comments and utilized generative AI to create summaries, while also assessing sentiment analysis as an indicator of product satisfaction.

Result: Sentiment analysis alone was found insufficient to measure user satisfaction; explicit surveys are necessary for gauging product perception.

Conclusion: Machine learning and AI can streamline the analysis of large user feedback datasets, but additional direct measures like surveys are essential for comprehensively understanding product satisfaction.

Abstract: In highly competitive software markets, user experience (UX) evaluation is crucial for ensuring software quality and fostering long-term product success. Such UX evaluations typically combine quantitative metrics from standardized questionnaires with qualitative feedback collected through open-ended questions. While open-ended feedback offers valuable insights for improvement and helps explain quantitative results, analyzing large volumes of user comments is challenging and time-consuming. In this paper, we present techniques developed during a long-term UX measurement project at a major software company to efficiently process and interpret extensive volumes of user comments. To provide a high-level overview of the collected comments, we employ a supervised machine learning approach that assigns meaningful, pre-defined topic labels to each comment. Additionally, we demonstrate how generative AI (GenAI) can be leveraged to create concise and informative summaries of user feedback, facilitating effective communication of findings to the organization and especially upper management. Finally, we investigate whether the sentiment expressed in user comments can serve as an indicator for overall product satisfaction. Our results show that sentiment analysis alone does not reliably reflect user satisfaction. Instead, product satisfaction needs to be assessed explicitly in surveys to measure the user's perception of the product.

</details>


### [951] [Cloze: An Open Research Platform for Studying Human-AI Conversations in Mental Health Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15033)
*Matthew Flathers,Francesco Cipriani,John Torous*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Cloze is an open-source platform enabling controlled studies of human-AI interaction in mental health research, addressing drawbacks of consumer LLM products.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
