### [1101] [The Algebra of Units: From Buckingham's Pi-grec Theorem to Latent-Variable Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16737)
*Mauro Valorani*

Main category: math-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a data-driven approach to discover dimensionless groups in engineering systems using no prior physics knowledge, based on geometrical insights and computational methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate the reliance on expert knowledge and physical insight traditionally needed to identify dimensionless groups, enabling automation via data-driven techniques.

Method: The approach uses logarithmic transformation to map measurements onto a low-dimensional manifold, applies singular value decomposition (SVD) for manifold identification, and performs a search over integer exponents to recover dimensionless quantities, filtering candidates based on characteristic scales.

Result: On a synthetic compressor dataset with 16,000 measurements, the method recovered correct dimensionless groups with numerical precision and reconstructed performance maps with errors below 0.01%.

Conclusion: The study establishes a bridge between classical dimensional analysis and modern machine learning, enabling interpretable, scalable, and data-efficient physical modeling through a fully automated process.

Abstract: Engineers often measure many quantities-speed, pressure, temperature, length-expressed in different physical units. The Buckingham Pi-grec theorem states that these variables can always be combined into a smaller set of dimensionless numbers whose values fully determine the system's behaviour.
  Identifying the appropriate dimensionless groups has traditionally required expert knowledge and physical insight. This paper shows that they can instead be discovered automatically from data, without prior knowledge of the governing physics.
  The key observation is that, after logarithmic transformation, measurements collected under different scalings of the same system lie on a low-dimensional manifold whose geometry is determined by the underlying dimensionless groups. Singular value decomposition (SVD) identifies this manifold directly from data. A subsequent search over integer-exponent combinations recovers candidate dimensionless quantities, while a repeating-variable filter retains only those constructed from the machine's characteristic scales. This procedure recovers familiar engineering groups, including the flow coefficient, head coefficient, and Mach number, while excluding equivalent but less interpretable alternatives.
  The method is demonstrated on a synthetic compressor dataset containing 16,000 measurements. Starting from raw dimensional variables and no physics input, it recovers the correct dimensionless groups to numerical precision and reproduces the compressor performance map with an error below 0.01%.
  More broadly, the work reveals a close connection between classical dimensional analysis and modern data-driven learning. Both rely on the same underlying algebraic structure, suggesting new approaches for building physical models that are simultaneously interpretable, scalable, and data-efficient.

</details>


<div id='hep-lat'></div>

# hep-lat [[Back]](#toc)

### [1102] [Learning the generating functional for variance reduction in lattice QCD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15986)
*Ryan Abbott,Yang Fu,Daniel C. Hackett,Gurtej Kanwar,Fernando Romero-López,Phiala E. Shanahan*

Main category: hep-lat

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method using machine-learned normalizing flows to drastically reduce the variance in calculating $N$-point correlation functions in lattice gauge theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Variance reduction in the calculation of $N$-point correlation functions is crucial for achieving precise results in lattice gauge theory, including Quantum Chromodynamics and Yang-Mills theory.

Method: The authors employ machine-learned normalizing flows to model the generating functional of quantum field theory, enabling efficient and low-variance estimations of bosonic operator correlation functions.

Result: The method achieves up to three orders of magnitude variance reduction in correlation function calculations, demonstrated in glueball correlators and Wilson loops.

Conclusion: Machine-learning-based normalizing flows provide a systematic approach to noiseless correlation function estimation, enhancing computational efficiency and accuracy in lattice gauge theory.

Abstract: The generating functional in quantum field theory provides the natural framework for constructing correlation functions as derivatives with respect to source operators. We present a methodology that leverages machine-learned normalizing flows to reduce the variance of arbitrary $N$-point correlation functions of bosonic operators in lattice gauge field theory calculations by encoding a representation of the generating functional. We show that it is possible to systematically approach noiseless estimators of correlation functions in this framework. We demonstrate this methodology with applications to calculations of glueball correlation functions and Wilson loops in Quantum Chromodynamics and Yang-Mills theory. The results show up to three orders of magnitude variance reduction.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.dis-nn'></div>

# cond-mat.dis-nn [[Back]](#toc)

### [1103] [The limits of interpretability in multiple linear regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16013)
*Anand Sharma,Chen Liu,Daniele Coslovich,Misaki Ozawa*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: The paper discusses challenges in the interpretability of machine-learning models, particularly linear regression, under multicollinearity and proposes insights through theoretical and numerical analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address issues in interpreting machine-learning models in the physical sciences, focusing on linear regression models under multicollinearity, which complicates understanding of the underlying mechanisms.

Method: The investigation involves theoretical analysis of eigenmodes in feature correlation matrices, numerical testing on physics datasets, and general validation on diverse datasets.

Result: It shows that small eigenvalue modes amplify weight fluctuations and oscillations. Although Ridge regularization mitigates some instability, interpretation issues persist. This is verified across multiple datasets.

Conclusion: The study highlights the difficulties of interpreting linear regression models under multicollinearity, providing a theoretical basis and demonstrating the limitations of current approaches like Ridge regularization.

Abstract: Interpreting machine-learning models has attracted increasing attention, particularly in the physical sciences, where one often seeks to understand the underlying mechanisms rather than merely make predictions. Multiple linear regression is often regarded as an interpretable alternative to more complex models, such as deep neural networks, because its predictions are expressed as explicit weighted sums of input features. However, when input features are strongly correlated, namely in the presence of multicollinearity, the learned weights can exhibit large dataset-to-dataset fluctuations and oscillatory behavior across physically similar features, making their interpretation difficult or even impossible. Although the instability of the weights under multicollinearity is well known in statistics, its consequences for physical interpretation, in particular its connection to oscillatory weights across physically similar features, have not been systematically clarified. Here, we theoretically discuss the mechanism behind this loss of interpretability by analyzing the eigenmodes of the feature correlation matrix. We show that small-eigenvalue modes associated with multicollinearity amplify fluctuations in the weights and generate oscillatory patterns that do not necessarily reflect meaningful contributions. We test this theoretical picture numerically on physics datasets and show that Ridge regularization suppresses these unstable modes, although the resulting weights must still be interpreted with caution. We further confirm the generality of our findings beyond physics by analyzing a diverse collection of publicly available datasets. Our results clarify why, in the presence of multicollinearity, physical interpretation can remain difficult even for linear regression models.

</details>


### [1104] [Generative modelling powered by room-temperature polariton condensates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15344)
*Yuan Wang,Marcin Muszynski,Avinash Dash,Rishabh Kaurav,Vinod M. Menon,Oleksandr Kyriienko*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates leveraging exciton-polariton condensates for generative modelling, outperforming traditional systems in a Polariton GAN workflow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To use physical platforms, specifically nonlinear optical systems, for efficient stochastic transformations in conditional generative modelling, overcoming limitations of digitally injected perturbations.

Method: The study employs room-temperature exciton-polariton condensates in organic dye microcavities as physical layers within a generative adversarial network to perform digit-to-image translation.

Result: The Polariton GAN outperformed digital and laser-based systems in inception score, digit preservation accuracy, and structural similarity metrics, while also enhancing adversarial training with natural regularisation and diverse outputs.

Conclusion: Polariton condensation offers a novel computational resource for generative modelling and provides a pathway to integrate physical processes into machine learning systems.

Abstract: Generative modelling requires efficient stochastic nonlinear transformations and physical platforms that can naturally realise them. We experimentally demonstrate that nonlinear optical systems operating in the strong light-matter coupling regime can serve as physical transformation layers for conditional generative modelling. Specifically, we develop a workflow in which room-temperature exciton-polariton condensates formed in organic dye microcavities act as a physical stochastic transform within a generative adversarial network and enable conditional digit-to-image translation. By using the nonlinear many-body dynamics and intrinsic stochasticity of polariton condensates, the workflow outperforms baseline approaches based on digitally injected perturbations. We find that polariton-enabled sampling via generative adversarial network (Polariton GAN) yields improved inception score, digit preservation accuracy and structural similarity compared with both digital sampling and laser-based systems. We further show that spatially correlated output variations can naturally regularise adversarial training and enhance output diversity. Our results establish polariton condensation as a new computational resource for generative modelling, opening a pathway towards physics-enhanced machine learning systems.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [1105] [Impedance MPC with Patient-Torque Estimation for Knee Rehabilitation Exoskeletons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13485)
*Yongyan Cao,Jinshan Tang*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper proposes an Impedance Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for knee rehabilitation exoskeletons, offering superior compliance and precision compared to classical impedance controllers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of ensuring safe compliance with involuntary spasms and voluntary patient effort while enforcing prescribed joint trajectories in knee rehabilitation exoskeletons.

Method: Developed an MPC framework using a series-elastic-actuator (SEA) platform, including feedforward dynamics reduction, a receding-horizon quadratic program (QP), and SEA-based torque sensing for disturbance estimation.

Result: The MPC approach achieved superior performance with 0.1 mrad RMS accuracy even under 15 Nm spasm, outperforming classical impedance controllers with significant offsets. All MPC variants met clinical criteria while classical controllers did not.

Conclusion: The proposed MPC framework demonstrates significant advantages in precision, compliance, and clinical effectiveness for knee rehabilitation over traditional control methods.

Abstract: Knee rehabilitation exoskeletons must enforce a prescribed joint trajectory while remaining safely compliant with involuntary spasm and voluntary patient effort-objectives in tension for any fixed-gain impedance controller. We present an Impedance Model Predictive Control framework for knee rehabilitation exoskeletons, demonstrated on a series-elastic-actuator (SEA) platform: an algebraic feedforward reduces the knee dynamics to a constant-coefficient scalar double integrator, and a receding-horizon quadratic program (QP) computes corrective torques while enforcing hard range-of-motion, torque, and velocity limits (ISO 13482). A Kalman disturbance state driven by direct SEA-based torque sensing (the series-elastic spring deflection measured through the elastic element - an intrinsic, EMG-free patient-torque estimate, not a separate load cell) gives a nominal offset-free guarantee and, via its sign and the desired-motion direction, sensorless Assist-as-Needed. The constant state matrix permits offline precomputation of the QP cost inverse, enabling 500 Hz operation with a multi-step horizon. Across seven-controller benchmarks (sinusoidal tracking, isometric hold), the 500 Hz Kalman MPC is offset free 0.1 mrad RMS, 0.1 mrad steady-state, 0.2 mrad peak under 15 Nm spasm, versus a 515 mrad steady-state offset for classical impedance at the same stiffness - the direct-measurement channel converging the estimate near-immediately (within a few sampling periods). Without the estimator it realizes a classical impedance (4.8 mrad RMS, 8.3 mrad steady-state). All MPC variants meet the 87 mrad clinical criterion; no classical controller does. The architecture is formulated for the 20 DOF MyoSuite myoLeg via coupling-aware per-joint QPs.

</details>


### [1106] [Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning for Emergency Collision Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15311)
*Yuhong Jiang,Shiyue Zhao,Junzhi Zhang,Junfeng Zhang,Xinhan Li,Shijie Zhao,Chengkun He*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a safety-critical control framework for collision avoidance during extreme driving scenarios by integrating reinforcement learning with a Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collision avoidance methods often rely on reactive safety evaluations, lacking foresight in accounting for both obstacle avoidance and vehicle stability, especially under extreme driving conditions.

Method: The proposed framework formulates a unified signed safety function combining geometric collision margins and stability limits. This function is extended into a finite-horizon motion safety set via reachability analysis, which is approximated using offline driving data to reduce computational load. The safety set is integrated into a constrained Markov decision process, and a PID-Lagrangian optimization scheme regulates safety constraint enforcement.

Result: Simulations and real-vehicle experiments in low-adhesion obstacle-avoidance scenarios showed higher goal-reaching rates, smoother maneuvers, and improved safety margins compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines forward-looking motion safety analysis with reinforcement learning, resulting in superior performance regarding collision avoidance and vehicle stability in extreme conditions.

Abstract: Emergency collision avoidance under extreme driving conditions demands safety-critical control that accounts for both obstacle proximity and vehicle dynamic stability over a future time horizon, yet existing methods often rely on instantaneous or local safety evaluations. This paper proposes a safe reinforcement learning framework guided by a Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability based motion safety set that provides forward-looking safety supervision for constrained policy optimization. Specifically, a unified signed safety function is formulated by combining geometric collision margins and chassis stability limits, and is then extended through reachability analysis into a finite-horizon motion safety set that characterizes whether safety can be maintained under future vehicle state evolution. To enable practical computation, the motion safety set is approximated from offline extreme driving data, mitigating the computational burden of grid-based HJ solvers. The learned motion safety set is then embedded as a continuous safety cost into a constrained Markov decision process, and a PID-Lagrangian policy optimization scheme is employed to adaptively regulate the Lagrange multiplier for safety constraint enforcement. Simulation and real-vehicle experiments on low-adhesion obstacle-avoidance scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher goal-reaching rates, produces smoother avoidance maneuvers, and maintains larger unified safety margins than baseline methods.

</details>


### [1107] [Robust Conformal CBF and CLF Controllers via Iterative Policy Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15366)
*Omid Mirzaeedodangeh,Eliot Shekhtman,Nikolai Matni,Lars Lindemann*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method to address distribution shifts in conformal prediction-based robust control frameworks, ensuring stability and safety guarantees while iteratively improving policy robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distribution shifts in closed-loop control systems undermine safety and stability guarantees derived from conformal predictions.

Method: The authors propose an episodic framework that refines conformal CLF/CBF policies through adversarially robust conformal prediction and a distribution shift budget determined via trajectory sensitivity analysis.

Result: The paper establishes theoretical guarantees for stability and safety in the proposed framework, supported by convergence analysis and validation on three case studies.

Conclusion: This is the first approach providing stability and safety guarantees for robust conformal CLF/CBF policies, effectively mitigating the challenges posed by distribution shifts.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) has been used to obtain probabilistic bounds on the error between a learned dynamics model and the true but unknown system. Such CP bounds can then be embedded into robust control Lyapunov function (CLF) and control barrier function (CBF) frameworks. However, such an approach does not retain stability/safety guarantees because of the distribution shift between the closed-loop trajectory distribution under the deployed CLF/CBF policy and the trajectory distribution from which the CP bound and its guarantees were derived. To address this issue, we propose an episodic framework that iteratively updates the robust conformal CLF/CBF policy while maintaining stability/safety guarantees across episodes. We achieve this by (1) using adversarially robust conformal prediction, and (2) quantifying a distribution shift budget that allows us to control how much the model error can increase across policy updates. This distribution shift budget is derived via a closed-loop trajectory sensitivity analysis, yielding an implicit and an explicit update rule for the CP bound. We analyze convergence of our algorithm, which we demonstrate on three case studies. To the best of our knowledge, these are the first results that provide stability/safety guarantees for robust conformal CBF/CLF policies.

</details>


### [1108] [Anisotropic Template Ansätze for Robust Positive Invariance under State-Dependent Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16068)
*Abdelrahman Ramadan,Melissa Greeff,Sidney Givigi*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper develops robust invariance conditions for systems with state- and input-dependent disturbances using anisotropic covariance structures, demonstrating significant performance improvements in simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust invariant sets are critical in control and safety verification, but current methods often lack adaptability to anisotropic uncertainties or entail high computational overhead. This work aims to improve adaptability and computational efficiency.

Method: The approach leverages a Gaussian process (GP)-based positive-definite matrix field to represent disturbances. This is mapped through a fixed ellipsoidal template and linked to Schur-stable dynamics via linear matrix inequality (LMI) conditions. An isotropic fallback ensures robustness, and the method eliminates the need for online set iterations or LMI solves.

Result: Simulations of a quadrotor demonstrate a 195x reduction in 3D velocity-tube volume and a 2.1x10⁵ reduction in a 7D velocity-control subspace compared to a non-adaptive baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed method achieves robust invariance and adaptivity to anisotropic disturbances with high computational efficiency, marking a substantial improvement over existing techniques.

Abstract: We establish sufficient conditions for robust positive invariance under state- and input-dependent disturbances with anisotropic covariance structure. The proposed ansatz maps a fixed ellipsoidal template through a GP-derived positive-definite matrix field, subsuming scalar homothetic scaling while retaining finite graph-based verification. The resulting LMI conditions couple the learned field to Schur-stable dynamics; an isotropic fallback with inflation factor $r=1/(1-γ_{\mathrm{cl}})$ proves admissibility. During each learning epoch the field is frozen, so online tube evaluation is one GP covariance query and a small matrix square root, with no online set iteration or LMI solve. Quadrotor simulations show a $195\times$ reduction in 3D velocity-tube volume and a $2.1{\times}10^5$ reduction in the joint 7D velocity-control subspace relative to a non-adaptive homothetic baseline. This extended version adds full proofs, a separated offline/online complexity analysis, and controller-sweep, contraction, and projection-area studies.

</details>


### [1109] [Distributed Safe Consensus Under Asymmetric Input and Time-Varying Output Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16116)
*Abhinav Sinha,Shashi Ranjan Kumar*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper proposes a distributed control strategy for coordinating multi-agent systems under asymmetric actuation bounds and safety constraints, ensuring safety and synchronization using a barrier-coordinate transformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of achieving consensus and synchronization in multi-agent systems while ensuring the agents operate within asymmetric actuation bounds and predefined safety constraints.

Method: The authors introduce a barrier-coordinate transformation for handling safety constraints and develop a distributed synchronization law. The proposed controller integrates graph-based coordination with actuator tracking to manage input admissibility, output safety, and synchronization.

Result: The framework ensures all signals remain bounded, input and output constraints are respected, and synchronization is achieved under compact initial conditions. Additionally, transformed synchronization errors converge exponentially, and original outputs asymptotically synchronize.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that safe and synchronized multi-agent consensus can be achieved even under asymmetric actuation and time-varying safety constraints, validated through numerical simulations.

Abstract: This paper studies safe distributed consensus for single-integrator multi-agent systems over connected undirected graphs under simultaneous asymmetric actuator constraints and output safety constraints. Each agent is equipped with a continuously differentiable asymmetric actuator dynamics that maps a commanded control signal to the realized plant input while keeping the latter strictly inside a prescribed admissible interval. To address output safety, a barrier-coordinate transformation is introduced over a common time-varying safe interval, and a distributed synchronization law is designed in the transformed coordinates. The resulting controller integrates a graph-based coordination layer with an actuator-side tracking layer, thereby enabling simultaneous enforcement of input admissibility, forward invariance of the safe output set, and asymptotic synchronization. For compact admissible sets of initial conditions, it is shown that the closed-loop solution is complete, all signals remain bounded, the actuator inputs remain strictly within their asymmetric bounds, and the agent outputs remain inside the prescribed safe interval for all time. Moreover, the transformed synchronization errors converge exponentially to zero, and the original agent outputs asymptotically synchronize to a designer-selected admissible trajectory embedded in the common safe interval. Numerical simulations validate the proposed framework and demonstrate safe consensus under both asymmetric actuation bounds and time-varying output constraints.

</details>


### [1110] [CREST: Deployment-Realistic Hardware-in-the-Loop NAS for Embedded Sensing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15004)
*Joseph Q. Zales,Pragya Sharma,Mani Srivastava*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces CREST (Cross-platform Runtime Evaluation and Search Tool), a deployment-specific neural architecture search framework for microcontrollers (MCUs) that emphasizes hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing for more accurate evaluation of memory, energy, and latency constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selecting neural network models for low-power MCUs has been traditionally based on proxies like FLOPs or parameters, often ignoring deployment-relevant metrics such as real-world hardware-specific constraints and runtime schedules, leading to suboptimal or infeasible deployments.

Method: CREST combines hardware-in-the-loop measurements and an optimized search workflow with configurable axes such as workload, model family, backend, schedule, and scoring policy. This allows for a comprehensive evaluation that mirrors real-world deployment conditions.

Result: CREST demonstrates a 41.7% reduction in energy usage compared to FLOPs-based selection in inertial odometry tasks, prevents choices of infeasible deployments on constrained platforms, and highlights differences in Pareto frontiers for continuous vs. duty-cycled inference on MCUs.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that accurate MCU neural architecture selection must move beyond traditional static proxies by incorporating deployment-relevant variables, showcasing CREST’s ability to refine architecture choices based on realistic use cases, platforms, and schedules.

Abstract: Deploying neural networks on low-power microcontrollers (MCUs) requires selecting model architectures under tight memory, latency, and energy constraints. Existing workflows often simplify this process along one or more axes: static proxy costs such as FLOPs or parameters, treating one MCU as representative, and continuous-inference tests instead of deployed sensing schedules. These assumptions can mis-rank Pareto-front candidates, miss infeasible deployments, and obscure schedule-dependent energy.
  We present CREST (Cross-platform Runtime Evaluation and Search Tool), a deployment-realistic hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) neural architecture search (NAS) framework for MCU sensing systems. CREST keeps the optimizer, HIL measurement boundary, logging, and replay workflow fixed while exposing workload, model family, target backend, schedule, quantization, and scoring policy as configurable axes. This makes deployment effects experimentally separable within one reusable workflow.
  We evaluate CREST on inertial odometry and audio classification across three Arm Cortex-M targets. For inertial odometry, measured-energy HIL search reduces median per-inference energy by 41.7% versus FLOPs-based selection and 40.8% versus memory-traffic-based selection at similar error. FLOPs-based selection also chooses infeasible deployments on memory-constrained targets. On the STM32 N657 target, continuous-inference and duty-cycled searches produce different Pareto frontiers. For audio classification, the same application-level policy selects different DS-CNN architectures on different boards, and cross-board replay changes deployment cost substantially.
  Overall, CREST shows that deployment-realistic MCU NAS must jointly optimize model architecture, target platform, runtime schedule, and deployment policy rather than relying only on static proxy costs or continuous-inference measurements.

</details>


### [1111] [Data-driven Control with Real-time Uncertainty Compensation for Multi-Fuel Engines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16171)
*Rajasree Sarkar,Arunava Banerjee,Sathya Aswath Govind Raju,Ishan Berk Altiner,Zongxuan Sun,Kenneth Kim,Chol-Bum Mike Keown*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a data-driven framework using Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) for real-time uncertainty compensation in the combustion control of multi-fuel CI engines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Consistent and optimal combustion phasing in multi-fuel CI engines is challenging due to modeling uncertainties and varied operating conditions.

Method: The paper employs Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) to model the combustion process and a pseudo-engine speed approach to adaptively adjust control inputs in real-time, addressing uncertainties.

Result: Through simulations, the method successfully aligns combustion phasing to the desired values in real-time, offering adaptive and scalable control.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles uncertainties and ensures optimal combustion phasing, making it suitable for multi-fuel CI engine control.

Abstract: Multi-fuel compression ignition (CI) engines offer superior power density and fuel flexibility. However, achieving consistent and optimal combustion phasing across a wide range of operating conditions remains a major challenge, particularly in the presence of modeling uncertainties. This paper presents a novel, data-driven real-time uncertainty compensation framework for combustion control in multi-fuel CI engines. The proposed approach introduces a pseudo-engine speed that enables dynamic adaptation of control inputs in response to uncertainty affecting the engine. To model the underlying combustion process, a Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) model is first trained on available input-output data, capturing the nonlinear and fuel-dependent behavior across varying operating conditions. Control inputs are then synthesized through model inversion of the learned GPR surrogate and augmented with an uncertainty compensator designed to mitigate deviations caused by dynamic variations in operating conditions and model inaccuracies. This integrated control strategy allows for real-time input corrections within a finite number of combustion cycles. Theoretical analysis establishes finite-time convergence guarantees for the proposed controller. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method steers the combustion phasing to the desired value in real-time, providing a scalable and adaptive control solution for multi-fuel CI engine operation.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [1112] [Learning Topological Representations for Molecular Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14737)
*Dominik Geng,Florian Graf,Martin Uray,Roland Kwitt*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: This paper proposes persistent homology (PH)-based descriptors for molecular dynamics simulations, evaluates their performance on diverse tasks, and demonstrates improvements in generative modeling of protein conformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Analyzing high-dimensional trajectories from molecular dynamics simulations requires designing effective molecular descriptors. Traditional handcrafted or learned descriptors may lack general applicability or expressiveness.

Method: The authors propose using persistent homology (PH) as a general-purpose representation for protein simulations and introduce the masked Flood complex to emphasize inter-residue structure efficiently. Tasks such as protein class prediction, observable regression, and Markov state model (MSM) estimation are analyzed using vectorized persistence diagrams.

Result: PH-based descriptors showed competitive and consistent performance across various benchmarks. Masked Flood PH outperformed others in overall performance, and its integration into the MarS-FM framework resulted in better ensemble statistics for generative modeling.

Conclusion: Persistent homology, especially with the masked Flood complex, is shown to be a valuable tool for molecular dynamics data, offering robust and transferrable representations for diverse computational tasks.

Abstract: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations generate trajectories in a high-dimensional configuration space whose analysis critically depends on molecular descriptors, typically handcrafted observables or learned kinetic embeddings. Designing descriptors that are both expressive and broadly applicable, however, remains challenging. We study persistent homology (PH) as a general-purpose representation for MD and introduce the masked Flood complex, a protein-tailored modification of a recently introduced simplicial complex construction that emphasizes inter-residue structure at low computational cost. Vectorized persistence diagrams then provide information-rich, geometry-aware summaries of protein conformations, which we evaluate on protein class prediction, frame-level observable regression, and Markov state model (MSM) estimation from learned low-dimensional coordinates in a single shared representation space. Results on the mdCATH dataset show that PH-based descriptors are competitive across tasks, with masked Flood PH yielding the most consistent overall performance. Further, when using topologically-informed MSMs as a drop-in replacement within the recent MarS-FM framework for generative modeling of protein conformations, we obtain consistently better ensemble statistics than MSMs based on physical observables. Finally, we explore the transferability of the generative model to qualitatively different, fast folding, proteins.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [1113] [Pixel-TTS: Image based Text Rendering for Robust Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14750)
*Adarsh Arigala,Arjun Gangwar,S Umesh,Yova Kementchedjhieva*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: Pixel-TTS is a visually grounded speech synthesis framework converting text into images for embeddings, enhancing zero-shot and cross-lingual performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in text-based approaches that treat characters independently, hindering generalization and requiring embedding matrix expansion when adapting to unseen characters or cross-lingual scenarios.

Method: Pixel-TTS renders text as images and uses a 2D convolutional layer to create embeddings, removing the need for embedding matrix expansion.

Result: Pixel-TTS demonstrates competitive performance, faster convergence, and robust zero-shot generalization compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: Visually grounding text enables Pixel-TTS to handle unseen characters and variations more effectively, offering a promising alternative to traditional speech synthesis techniques.

Abstract: Recent advances in pixel-based text modeling show that representing text as images enables models to exploit visual cues for language understanding. Grounding text in its visual form allows structurally similar characters with different Unicode encodings to produce similar embeddings, benefiting cross-lingual and zero-shot scenarios. Conventional text-based approaches treat each character independently, limiting generalization to unseen characters and requiring embedding expansion during cross-lingual adaptation. We propose Pixel-TTS, the first framework for visually grounded speech synthesis. It renders text as images and projects them through a 2D convolutional layer to generate embeddings. This design eliminates embedding matrix expansion during fine-tuning while improving robustness to unseen characters and orthographic variations. Extensive experiments show Pixel-TTS achieves competitive performance with strong baselines, faster convergence and robust zero-shot generalization.

</details>


### [1114] [EChO-Agent: Evidence Chain Orchestration Agent for Audio Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15141)
*Siyuan Zhang,Jian Zong,Junyu Wang,Peiyuan Jiang,Jiahao Yan,Jingyu Zhang,Tianrui Wang,Xiaobao Wang,Longbiao Wang,Jianwu Dang*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: EChO-Agent is proposed to improve question-relevant audio reasoning by introducing a modular framework that integrates planning, evidence integration, and verification processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language-audio models (LALMs) struggle with focusing on question-relevant segments of audio and providing clear reasoning for complex questions.

Method: The authors propose a modular agent framework, EChO-Agent, which includes planning, tool execution, evidence integration, and answer verification for audio QA.

Result: EChO-Agent outperforms baseline models in terms of both accuracy and rubric scores, with evidence integration shown to be a key factor.

Conclusion: EChO-Agent effectively addresses the challenges in complex audio question answering by introducing a structured and verifiable approach, showcasing improved results over existing methods.

Abstract: While LALMs show promise on audio question answering, they fail to focus on question-relevant segments of audio and provide a clear, checkable reasoning process when dealing with complex audio reasoning. Reinforcement learning and tool-augmented prompting can help models better relate questions to audio but lack a reliable way to understand, integrate, and self-verify audio segments. To address this gap, we present EChO-Agent, a modular agent framework that reformulates complex audio QA as a planning, tool execution, evidence integration, and answer verification workflow. Experiments on MMAR benchmark show EChO-Agent improves both accuracy and rubric scores over baseline and ablation studies show evidence integration is the key factor.

</details>


### [1115] [From Physics to Representation: Audio Learning with Synthetic Pre-training via Procedural Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14791)
*Fengrui Liu,Ruiyang Huang,Qijian Zheng,Yuanfang Wang,Feng Liu*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: AudioPG utilizes procedural synthesis to pre-train audio models without real-world corpora, achieving competitive benchmarks quickly on a single GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce reliance on massive datasets, privacy concerns, and high training costs in audio analysis by exploring alternative pre-training methods.

Method: AudioPG employs a Transformer-based masked autoencoder trained on synthetically generated waveforms, allowing pre-training without real audio data.

Result: The framework achieves high accuracy and performance on real audio benchmarks like ESC-50, FSD50K, UrbanSound8K, and Speech Commands V2, while completing pre-training in less than 20 minutes on one GPU.

Conclusion: Procedural synthesis provides an efficient and interpretable alternative for audio pre-training, enabling competitive transfer performance without large datasets.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning advances audio representation for multimedia analysis. However, prevailing data-centric approaches rely on massive real-world corpora, increasing training costs, curation burdens, and privacy barriers. To address this, we present AudioPG, a procedural synthesis framework eliminating real audio recordings during pre-training. AudioPG trains a Transformer-based masked autoencoder on waveforms generated on-the-fly from basic acoustic primitives and composition rules. The encoder transfers effectively to real audio benchmarks, achieving 90.60% accuracy on ESC-50, 0.546 mAP on FSD50K, 88.17% on UrbanSound8K, and 97.03% on Speech Commands V2. Notably, pre-training completes in under 20 minutes on a single GPU. Latent space analysis reveals physical factors, including fundamental frequency and relative intensity, emerge in orthogonal subspaces, making representations linearly decodable. These results establish procedural synthesis as an efficient, interpretable pre-training signal when large-scale corpora are unavailable. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Freyliu0516/audioPG.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [1116] [MatchLM2Lite: A Scalable MLLM-to-Lite Framework for Reproduced Content Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14786)
*Xiaotian Fan,Hiok Hian Ong,David Yuchen Wang,Zirui Zhu,Kanchan Sarkar,Kun Xu*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The paper introduces MatchLM2Lite, a real-time system for identifying reproduced content on video platforms, focusing on multimodal inputs (video, audio, text) and achieving high efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure content diversity, authenticity, and safety on online video platforms by effectively identifying low-value reproduced content at scale.

Method: The system includes a high-capacity teacher model (MatchLM) and its streamlined student model (MatchLite). MatchLite is trained using knowledge distillation from MatchLM and optimizes video pair comparisons using multimodal data inputs.

Result: MatchLM shows an improvement of +8.57 F1-score over the previous model, while MatchLite retains a +6.55 F1-score gain, reducing computational cost by 35x. The system decreases reproduced video views by 2.5% without negative effects on user engagement.

Conclusion: MatchLM2Lite is an effective, scalable solution for identifying and reducing reproduced content volume on video platforms without compromising user satisfaction.

Abstract: Content moderation is critical for online video platforms to ensure content safety, protect creators, and sustain positive user experiences. Beyond filtering harmful content, platforms must guarantee content authenticity at scale so that users are exposed to diverse, original videos rather than low-value reproductions. We present MatchLM2Lite, a real-time, production-grade reproduced content identification (RCI) system that leverages the powerful understanding of a multimodal large language model (MLLM) distilled into a small and fast-inference model. Our system jointly models video, audio, and text signals, operating on pairs of videos to produce fine-grained reproduction scores. The system comprises two modules, MatchLM and MatchLite, and a two-stage training recipe. First, our high-capacity MLLM, MatchLM, serves as a teacher model to define the upper bound of RCI performance. Its capabilities are then distilled into a compact student model, MatchLite. This design allows MatchLite to deliver low-latency, high-throughput inference on video pairs while preserving much of MatchLM's accuracy, making it suitable for integration into real-time recommendation systems. MatchLM achieves an F1-score improvement of +8.57 compared to our previous production model. After knowledge distillation, MatchLite retains a +6.55 gain in F1-score while reducing computational cost by 35x. Deployed at scale, MatchLM2Lite enables efficient, pairwise multimodal RCI, stably serving online traffic at high queries per second (QPS) with an end-to-end latency below 30 seconds. This system has reduced the reproduced video view rate on our platform by 2.5% without degrading user engagement, demonstrating its effectiveness in a large-scale production environment.

</details>


### [1117] [Teacher-Student Structure for Domain Adaptation in Ensemble Audio-Visual Video Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15117)
*Elham Abolhasani,Maryam Ramezani,Hamid R. Rabiee*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The paper addresses improved generalization in deepfake detection by introducing the EAV-DFD model, which uses an ensemble audio-visual approach and domain adaptation techniques. Testing on multiple datasets shows significant improvements in unseen domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for the study stems from the increasing realism of deepfake media and the need for generalized detection methods that work across diverse domains to address privacy and societal challenges.

Method: The authors developed the EAV-DFD, a deep ensemble audio-visual model, enhanced by a teacher-student framework for domain adaptation. The approach incorporates multiple modalities—audio, video, interactions—while training the student model with limited data from unseen domains.

Result: The EAV-DFD framework achieved notable domain adaptation performance, with AUC improvements of 4.09%, 17.94%, and 0.5% across the DFDC, Deepfake_TIMIT, and PolyGlotFake datasets, respectively, using only a small subset of unseen domain data.

Conclusion: The proposed EAV-DFD model demonstrates a robust capability to adapt to diverse domains and interpret manipulated modalities, showcasing its potential for practical deepfake detection applications in real-world contexts.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of generative AI models is leading to more realistic deepfake media, encompassing the manipulation of audio, video, or both. This raises severe privacy and societal concerns. Numerous studies in this area have yielded promising intra-domain results; however, these models frequently exhibit decreased efficacy when faced with data from dissimilar domains. Consequently, recent deepfake detection approaches focus on enhancing the generalization ability through multiple techniques that incorporate all input modalities, including audio, images, and their interactions. In this regard, we propose the EAV-DFD method, a generalized deep ensemble audio-visual model (EAV-DFD) combined with a domain adaptation mechanism utilizing a teacher-student framework to enhance the model's ability to perform and generalize effectively across unseen domains. To evaluate the model's performance, we used the FakeAVCeleb dataset as the primary domain and the DFDC, Deepfake_TIMIT, and PolyGlotFake datasets as an unseen domain. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework is efficient in domain adaptation, improving AUC performance of the model by 4.09%, 17.94%, and 0.5% on three unseen datasets, using only a small portion of them to train the student model. This leads to a novel deepfake detection model capable of adapting to new domains and interpreting which modality has been manipulated, highlighting the potential of our approach for real-world applications.

</details>


### [1118] [MAF: Multimodal Adaptive Few-shot Prompting for Sentiment Analysis with MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15694)
*Hangling Xie*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework, MAF, to improve sentiment analysis in multimodal large language models by using adaptive, context-sensitive prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static, uniform prompts in multimodal large language models fail to capture nuanced differences across multimodal inputs, resulting in suboptimal sentiment analysis.

Method: The proposed MAF framework dynamically retrieves context-relevant demonstrations by encoding facial expressions, scene context, and textual semantics. It uses a coefficient generation network for real-time weighted fusion and retrieves top-K demonstrations weighted by multimodal similarity scores. Prediction stability is enhanced with majority voting.

Result: Experiments show that the MAF framework improves performance over existing models and remains competitive with leading sentiment-analysis baselines.

Conclusion: MAF effectively addresses prompt design sensitivity issues in MLLMs, resulting in more nuanced and accurate sentiment analysis across multimodal inputs.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in understanding complex multimodal content. However, their performance in sentiment analysis exhibits acute sensitivity to prompt design, rendering static, uniformly applied prompts inherently suboptimal for capturing the nuanced multimodal cues that vary across inputs. To address this limitation, we propose a Multimodal Adaptive Few-Shot Prompting (MAF) framework, which dynamically retrieves and integrates query-relevant demonstrations to elicit the sentiment reasoning capabilities of MLLMs in a context-sensitive manner. MAF constructs a demonstration retrieval module that holistically encodes facial expressions, scene context, and textual semantics, with a lip movement amplitude detection mechanism introduced for accurate speaker identification in multi-person scenarios. Departing from conventional fixed-weight fusion, a lightweight coefficient generation network is trained to output query-conditioned fusion weights in real time, enabling weighted aggregation of multimodal similarity scores to retrieve the top-K most informative demonstrations. Prediction stability is further enhanced through majority voting over multiple candidate outputs generated by the MLLM. Extensive experiments on public benchmark datasets demonstrate that MAF achieves substantial and consistent performance improvements over the corresponding backbone variants and remains competitive with strong multimodal sentiment-analysis baselines.

</details>


### [1119] [Effective and Low-cost Lane-based Map Localization for Vehicle-Centric Route Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16101)
*Hong-Shiang Lin,Jung-Hsin Chen,Yu-Luen Tzeng,Wei-Hao Chen,Yi-Chen Lee,Li-Jhe Chen,Peng-Yuan Chen*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper introduces OLRA, a low-cost framework that improves driver-view-aligned route representation using map localization and lane marking detection, surpassing OpenPilot in complex road scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of creating more accurate and visually consistent route representations for intuitive driving guidance, especially in complex road conditions, using low-cost methods.

Method: OLRA combines map-based navigation routes with camera-detected lane markings to enhance vehicle localization accuracy and visual route alignment. It also proposes new evaluation metrics and benchmarks its performance against OpenPilot using the nuScenes dataset.

Result: Experimental results show that OLRA outperforms OpenPilot in complex road segments and in route estimation over distances exceeding 20 meters, with lower overall Euclidean error.

Conclusion: OLRA demonstrates improved route representation and localization accuracy, encouraging further developments in low-cost, map-localization-based driving assistance systems.

Abstract: Driver-centric route representation plays a vital role in intuitive driving guidance systems. This paper presents OLRA, a low-cost, map-localization-based framework that derives driver-view-aligned routes by matching map-based navigation routes with camera-detected lane markings. This alignment process mutually enhances vehicle localization accuracy and visual route consistency. To bridge the evaluation gap across different paradigms, we introduce practical route evaluation metrics and benchmark OLRA against OpenPilot, a representative direct-generation approach. Experimental results on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate that OLRA outperforms OpenPilot in complex road segments and in route estimation at distance beyond 20 meters, achieving lower overall Euclidean error. This study is expected to promote future research in low-cost, maplocalization-based route generation methods.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.GN'></div>

# q-bio.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [1120] [Human genetic evidence is associated with drug approval across therapeutic areas: an observational analysis of 26,278 target-disease pairs with temporal validation and feature ablation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14823)
*Victoria Paterson*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: The study finds genetic evidence significantly increases the likelihood of drug target approval, with limitations in predictive modeling. Findings are cataloged for further research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to evaluate whether genetic evidence is associated with higher drug target approval rates and to assess its utility in predictive modeling.

Method: The researchers analyzed 26,278 target-disease pairs using Open Targets and ChEMBL databases, performed statistical tests (e.g., odds ratios, sensitivity analyses), and explored performance of predictive models with and without literature evidence.

Result: Targets with genetic evidence had a 3.25-fold higher approval rate; literature mining dominated predictive performance, while genetic evidence added minimal gain. 1,433 Phase 1/2 targets were catalogued.

Conclusion: Genetic evidence is strongly associated with drug approval but offers limited predictive utility in isolation. Observational findings warrant further exploration.

Abstract: Genetic evidence is enriched among approved drug targets: in an observational analysis of 26,278 target-disease pairs from Open Targets and ChEMBL, targets with any genetic association had a 3.25-fold higher approval rate than those without (OR = 3.25, 95% CI 2.79-3.79, p = 1.91e-42). A target-level analysis accounting for non-independence of pairs sharing the same gene gave OR = 2.79 (bootstrap 95% CI 2.22-3.53); the oncology pair-level OR of 6.72 attenuates to 2.71 at the target level, illustrating how non-independence inflates area-specific estimates. The enrichment replicated in post-2015 approvals (OR = 3.51, p = 1.72e-8). Feature ablation across six evidence types revealed that literature mining alone accounts for most classifier performance (AUPRC = 0.099 versus 0.109 for all features), consistent with temporal leakage from post-approval publications. Excluding literature, remaining evidence types retain above-baseline signal (AUPRC = 0.084, 1.63x baseline). Sensitivity analyses bracket the pair-level OR between 3.25 and 4.93. Genetic evidence alone yields only a 1.0-percentage-point absolute AUPRC gain and the best model has poor calibration; the classifier has limited practical predictive value. We catalogue 1,433 genetically supported Phase 1/2 pairs as a hypothesis-generating resource. All findings are observational.

</details>


<div id='math.FA'></div>

# math.FA [[Back]](#toc)

### [1121] [Representation Costs in Data Science: Foundations and the Quasi-Banach Spaces of Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14954)
*Greg Ongie,Rahul Parhi*

Main category: math.FA

TL;DR: This paper establishes a general framework for analyzing representation costs in parametric data-fitting methods, unifying various approaches across different models and introducing new insights for deep neural network spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified, abstract perspective on representation costs across diverse parametric models, connecting classical and modern methods under a shared framework.

Method: The authors develop an abstract framework that defines representation costs and their induced function spaces for parametric models, proving general results like representer theorems and connecting parametric and nonparametric descriptions under overparameterization.

Result: Classical methods like reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces and modern models like deep neural networks are shown to fit within this framework. For deep ReLU networks, the authors identify their native spaces as $p$-normable quasi-Banach spaces, with significant implications for describing their inductive biases.

Conclusion: The framework not only unifies existing understanding of representation costs but also extends it, offering new theoretical insights, particularly for deep neural networks.

Abstract: We develop a general framework for analyzing representation costs of parametric data-fitting methods through their parameter-space regularizers. From this abstract perspective, we define representation costs for arbitrary parametric models and reveal their induced (native) function spaces. This unifies recent function-space views of data-fitting methods. We also prove that many natural results hold in this abstract setting, including representer theorems for parametric methods on their native spaces. The framework also rigorously connects parametric methods with their equivalent nonparametric descriptions under sufficient overparameterization. Classical methods and their native spaces, such as kernel methods / reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces, wavelets / Besov spaces, and shallow neural networks / variation spaces emerge as special cases of our abstract framework. A byproduct of "axiomatizing" the study of representation costs is that we also immediately obtain new results for deep neural networks: For depth-$L$ feedforward ReLU networks, their induced native spaces are $p$-normable quasi-Banach spaces with $p = 2/L$. This reveals that the inductive bias of deep neural networks (as given by the representation cost) cannot be captured by norms for depths $L > 2$.

</details>


<div id='stat.AP'></div>

# stat.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [1122] [A Koopman-PINN Framework for Epidemic Models: Parameter Inference and Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15201)
*Achraf Zinihi,Matthias Ehrhardt,Moulay Rchid Sidi Ammi*

Main category: stat.AP

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Koopman-enhanced physics-informed neural network (K--PINN) for better parameter inference and forecasting in epidemic models, demonstrating improved performance over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance interpretability, parameter identifiability, and long-term predictive stability in modeling nonlinear epidemic systems by integrating Koopman operator theory with physics-informed learning.

Method: The proposed K--PINN maps epidemic states to a latent space using Koopman theory, where dynamics evolve linearly, ensuring compliance with governing epidemic equations via automatic differentiation.

Result: K--PINN, tested on synthetic and real-world datasets, outperformed classical PINNs and Koopman-EDMD in parameter estimation, trajectory reconstruction, and long-term prediction.

Conclusion: K--PINN is a promising machine learning framework for accurate epidemic modeling and can potentially be applied to complex systems beyond the studied cases.

Abstract: We propose a Koopman-enhanced physics-informed neural network (K--PINN) framework for parameter inference and forecasting in nonlinear epidemic models. This method combines Koopman operator theory and physics-informed learning. It maps epidemic states into a latent observable space where the dynamics evolve approximately linearly while satisfying the governing epidemic equations through automatic differentiation. This integration improves interpretability, parameter identifiability, and long-term predictive stability.
  We apply the proposed framework to a normalized SEIRSD epidemic model and evaluate it using synthetic monkeypox (Mpox) data and real-world datasets from Germany, Morocco, and Sweden for the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Synthetic trajectories are generated using a structure-preserving, nonstandard finite difference scheme to ensure reliable training data. Numerical results demonstrate that K--PINN achieves more accurate parameter estimation, trajectory reconstruction, and long-term forecasting than classical PINNs and Koopman-EDMD approaches.
  These results suggest that K--PINN is an effective machine learning framework for epidemic modeling that can be extended to more complex systems.

</details>


<div id='hep-ph'></div>

# hep-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [1123] [Event Generation with Parallel Langevin Sampling and Learned Stein Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14854)
*Rob Verheyen*

Main category: hep-ph

TL;DR: This paper explores a Langevin-based approach to efficiently generate events for collider phenomenology, particularly for high-multiplicity final states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the computational inefficiency in event generation for precision collider studies, especially when dealing with high-multiplicity final states with expensive matrix-element evaluations.

Method: The method uses parallel underdamped Langevin chains, alongside a learned Stein discrepancy as a convergence diagnostic, and incorporates neural-network surrogate initialization to optimize computations.

Result: The approach requires only a modest number of Langevin steps to reach relaxation, showing scalability over different multiplicities, and surrogate initialization significantly reduces matrix-element evaluations.

Conclusion: This method offers a computationally efficient and scalable alternative for event generation in collider phenomenology, improving performance for complex final states.

Abstract: Efficient event generation is a major computational challenge for precision collider phenomenology, especially for high-multiplicity final states where matrix-element evaluations are expensive and rejection-sampling efficiencies are low. We study an alternative approach based on many parallel underdamped Langevin chains, retaining one terminal state from each chain to obtain unweighted events while avoiding within-chain autocorrelation. A learned Stein discrepancy is used as a convergence diagnostic, providing a data-driven estimate of the relaxation time. We apply the method to tree-level $u\bar u\to Z+n g$ event generation and find that relaxation requires only a modest number of exact-target Langevin steps, with mild growth over the multiplicities studied. Finally, we show that simple neural-network surrogate initialization can substantially reduce the required number of exact matrix-element and gradient evaluations.

</details>


### [1124] [JetParticle-JEPA: An Efficient Self-Supervised Representation Learning method for Jet Tagging in High-Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14813)
*Guillaume Letellier,Antonin Vacheret,Frédéric Jurie*

Main category: hep-ph

TL;DR: JP-JEPA, a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture, is introduced for jet tagging at the LHC. It uses self-supervised learning on particle clouds and achieves performance rivaling supervised methods while being more robust and data-efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jet tagging methods at the LHC rely heavily on deep learning trained on large simulated datasets, but face challenges with computational costs and robustness to detector mismodeling.

Method: The method, JP-JEPA, leverages a Particle Transformer backbone and self-supervised learning to predict latent representations of jets from continuous particle clouds, bypassing tokenization and raw data reconstruction.

Result: JP-JEPA performs comparably to supervised state-of-the-art models on full datasets, surpasses them in low-label settings, and outperforms prior self-supervised approaches. It also exhibits robustness to missing data and better uncertainty management.

Conclusion: JP-JEPA presents a novel, efficient, and robust framework for jet physics, making it a strong candidate as a foundational model for applications at the LHC.

Abstract: Jet tagging at the Large Hadron Collider increasingly relies on deep learning models trained on massive simulated datasets, leading to high computational costs and limited robustness to detector mismodeling. We introduce JetParticle-JEPA (JP-JEPA), a self-supervised Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture that learns physically meaningful jet representations directly from continuous particle clouds without tokenization or reconstruction of raw inputs. Built on a Particle Transformer backbone, JP-JEPA predicts latent representations of masked particles while preserving fine-grained kinematic correlations. On the JetClass benchmark, JP-JEPA achieves performance comparable to fully supervised state-of-the-art methods on the full dataset, surpasses supervised baselines in low-label regimes, and significantly outperforms existing SSL approaches. On Top Quark and Quark-Gluon Tagging benchmarks, it remains on par with supervised methods. The learned representations also exhibit strong robustness to missing detector information and improved uncertainty behavior, highlighting JP-JEPA as a promising foundation-model framework for robust and data-efficient jet physics at the LHC.

</details>


### [1125] [Pre-Training for Simulation-Based Science: A Study on Jet Foundation Model Training Objectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14870)
*Ibrahim Elsharkawy,Joschka Birk,Vinicius Mikuni,Wahid Bhimji,Gregor Kasieczka,Benjamin Nachman*

Main category: hep-ph

TL;DR: The study compares pre-training methods using the OmniLearned High Energy Physics FM framework, assessing classification, generation, and self-supervised modeling. Each pre-training approach performs differently based on the downstream tasks and label availability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and optimize pre-training strategies for foundation models in scientific contexts, leveraging abundant labeled simulation data for enhanced downstream performance.

Method: Systematic evaluation of pre-training methods (supervised classification, flow-matching generative, and self-supervised masked particle modeling) on scientific data and fine-tuning these models on classification and generation downstream tasks.

Result: Classifier pre-training is optimal for classification with abundant labels. Combined classifier and masked particle modeling excels in low-label regimes. Flow matching generative pre-training benefits generative downstream tasks but not classification.

Conclusion: Pre-training objectives should reflect downstream requirements. Efficient transferability to hybrid tasks (classification and generation) necessitates incorporating diverse pre-training objectives, providing a scalable framework for simulation-based sciences.

Abstract: Foundation models (FMs) trained on large datasets and fine-tuned on downstream tasks have emerged as a powerful paradigm in AI for science. Industrial FMs are typically trained using self-supervision with masking due to the lack of labels. In many scientific domains, accurate simulations are plentiful and facilitate large, labeled datasets. This opens up new possibilities for pre-training. We present a systematic comparison of pre-training methods using the OmniLearned High Energy Physics FM framework. We test supervised classification, flow-matching generation, and self-supervised masked particle modeling. All models are pre-trained on the JetClass dataset and fine-tuned on two representative downstream tasks, top jet classification and JetNet conditional generation. Among other observations, for classification tasks, we find that pure classifier pre-training is optimal when downstream labels and model capacity are plentiful, but combining it with self-supervised masked particle modeling (MPM) is uniquely powerful in the low-finetuning label regime. Flow matching-based generative pre-training seems to provide little benefit for downstream classification, and interestingly, for downstream generation, we find that flow matching must be in the pre-training objective to see a significant finetuning advantage, hinting at the orthogonality of classification and generation tasks. That is, for a model to transfer to both generative and classification downstream tasks, it must be pre-trained on both. This study provides a template for controlled scaling analysis of pre-training objectives for foundation models in simulation-based sciences.

</details>
